# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Define a sensor. List any four types of position sensors used in mechatronic systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A sensor is a device that detects a physical quantity (such as displacement, force, temperature, or flow) and converts it into an electrical signal whose magnitude is proportional to the stimulus. In mechatronics, sensors form the input stage of the measurement and control chain.

Four position sensors are:
1. **Potentiometer** — resistive wiper-based, contact type.
2. **LVDT** — transformer-based, contact type, high accuracy.
3. **Optical Encoder** — non-contact, digital pulse output.
4. **IR Sensor** — non-contact, reflective or through-beam.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 0.5 each for the four sensors.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Differentiate between acoustic emission sensor and ultrasonic sensor in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Acoustic Emission Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|---|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Passive** — listens to waves emitted by the material itself. | **Active** — injects a wave and receives its echo. |
| 2 | Frequency range typically **20 kHz – 1 MHz**, transient bursts. | Frequency range typically **20 kHz – 10 MHz**, continuous pulses. |
| 3 | Used for **NDT, crack monitoring, leak detection**. | Used for **distance measurement, level sensing, flaw detection**. |

*(3 marks — one mark per valid differentiating point.)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified from Past Year Patterns]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **optical encoder**. Distinguish between incremental and absolute encoders. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** An optical encoder consists of:
1. **Light source** — typically an infrared LED.
2. **Code disk** — a glass or metal disk mounted on the rotating shaft, marked with equally spaced radial slots (or coded patterns for absolute type).
3. **Photodetectors** — photodiodes/phototransistors that receive light through the slots.
4. **Signal conditioner** — comparators and Schmitt triggers that shape the sinusoidal detector output into clean square-wave pulses.
5. **Counter / Decoder** — counts pulses (incremental) or decodes the bit pattern (absolute).

**Working:** As the shaft rotates, slots alternately pass and block the light beam. Each slot-edge transition produces one pulse. The pulse count is directly proportional to angular displacement; pulse rate is proportional to angular velocity. Quadrature outputs (A and B, 90° out of phase) allow detection of rotation direction.

**Incremental vs Absolute:**

| Feature | Incremental | Absolute |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| Tracks | One (or two for quadrature) | $n$ tracks, binary / Gray coded |
| Output | Pulses (counted from reference) | Unique $n$-bit word for every angle |
| Power loss memory | **Lost** — needs homing | **Retained** — always knows position |
| Resolution | $360^\circ / N$ | $360^\circ / 2^n$ |

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for working, 2 for comparison.)*

---

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage in terms of displacement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A cylindrical hollow former carries three coils:
- **Primary winding $P$** in the center, energized by an AC carrier (1–10 kHz, typically 5 V RMS).
- **Two identical secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$** wound on either side of $P$, connected in **series opposition** (opposite polarity).
- A **soft-iron core** slides inside the bore, attached via a rod to the measured object.

**Working:**
- **Null position:** Core is centered → equal mutual coupling with $S_1$ and $S_2$ → EMFs are equal and opposite → $V_{out} = 0$.
- **Core moves upward:** Coupling with $S_1$ increases → $V_{S_1} > V_{S_2}$ → $V_{out}$ is positive.
- **Core moves downward:** Coupling with $S_2$ increases → $V_{S_2} > V_{S_1}$ → $V_{out}$ is negative (phase reversal).
- The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ is proportional to displacement; the **phase** indicates direction.

**Derivation:** (write the four key steps as in SECTION 3.1)

1. $V_{S_1} = K(1 + \kappa x)$, $V_{S_2} = K(1 - \kappa x)$
2. $V_{out} = V_{S_1} - V_{S_2}$
3. $V_{out} = 2K\kappa x$
4. Defining sensitivity $K_{LVDT} = 2K\kappa$:
   $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for working, 3 for derivation including units and explanation.)*

---

### 📌 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified from Past Year Patterns]`**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **piezoelectric accelerometer** as a vibration sensor. List four advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a **piezoelectric crystal** (typically PZT or quartz) sandwiched between a **base** (fixed to the vibrating body) and an **inertial seismic mass** $m$.

When the base accelerates with acceleration $a$, the inertial mass exerts a force on the crystal:

$$
F = m \cdot a
$$

By the **direct piezoelectric effect**, the crystal generates a charge:

$$
Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a
$$

The corresponding voltage (after charge amplifier):

$$
V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p}
$$

The sensitivity is:

$$
S = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_p} \quad (\text{V/g or pC/g})
$$

The output voltage is directly proportional to acceleration, and hence to vibration amplitude (after integration in the signal conditioner).

**Advantages:**
1. Wide frequency range (0.1 Hz – 50 kHz).
2. High sensitivity and resolution.
3. Rugged, compact, no moving parts (apart from the small internal mass).
4. No external power required (passive sensor).

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot measure **static (DC) acceleration** — the piezo effect requires *changing* force.
2. Sensitive to **temperature variations**, which can cause drift.

*(7 marks — 3 for working, 2 for derivation, 2 for advantages/limitations.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **turbine flow meter** with a neat diagram. State its advantages over an orifice plate meter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A turbine flow meter consists of:
- A **multi-bladed rotor (turbine)** mounted coaxially inside the flow pipe on a shaft supported by bearings.
- A **pickup coil** (magnetic or inductive) mounted on the outside of the pipe.
- A **permanent magnet** associated with the pickup.
- A **pulse counter / frequency-to-flow converter**.

**Working:** When fluid flows through the pipe, it impinges on the angled rotor blades, causing the rotor to spin. The angular velocity of the rotor is proportional to the volumetric flow rate. As each blade tip passes the magnetic pickup, it generates a **voltage pulse** (variable reluctance / induction principle). The frequency $f$ of these pulses is:

$$
f = K \cdot Q
$$

where $K$ is the **meter constant** (pulses per unit volume). Therefore:

$$
Q = \frac{f}{K}
$$

**Advantages over orifice plate:**
1. **Higher accuracy** (±0.5% vs. ±2%–3% for orifice).
2. **Better rangeability** (10:1 vs. 4:1).
3. **Lower permanent pressure loss** (no sharp edge obstruction).
4. **Direct digital output** (pulse train) — no $\Delta P$ sensor needed.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for advantages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "sensor" with "transducer"** — Marks lost for not writing the broader/narrower relationship.
> 2. **LVDT answers missing the AC excitation requirement** — Examiners expect explicit mention of **carrier frequency (1–10 kHz)**.
> 3. **Optical encoder answers forgetting direction detection** — Always mention **quadrature channels A and B**.
> 4. **Piezoelectric accelerometer answers stating "measures vibration directly"** — The sensor measures **acceleration**, not vibration. Vibration is derived by integration.
> 5. **Venturi formula missing the $(1 - \beta^4)$ term** — Writing only $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ will lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly deducts for missing SI units.
> 7. **Mixing up AE and ultrasonic** — AE is passive; ultrasonic is active. Saying "AE uses a transmitter" is a 2-mark loss.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

- **Sensor vs Transducer vs Actuator** — definitions must be precise.
- **Potentiometer** → voltage divider, contact, wiper wear is the main drawback.
- **LVDT** → AC excitation, null position, differential output, **no friction**, infinite resolution.
- **Optical Encoder** → incremental vs absolute, resolution $= 360/N$ or $360/2^n$, quadrature for direction.
- **IR Sensor** → reflective (triangulation) vs through-beam; non-contact proximity.
- **AE Sensor** → passive, piezoelectric, listens to 20 kHz–1 MHz waves from defects.
- **Vibration Sensor (Accelerometer)** → measures **acceleration**, not vibration; charge $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$.
- **Tactile Sensor** → piezoresistive/capacitive/piezo; used in robotic fingertips.
- **Turbine Flow Meter** → $Q = f/K$; high accuracy, digital output.
- **Venturi Flow Meter** → $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$; ideal for low-pressure-loss measurement.
- **Memorize**: Range, Resolution, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Bandwidth — write these in the *first paragraph* of any sensor answer.
- **Always mention** the output form (analog voltage / digital pulse / frequency / charge) — examiners reward completeness.
- **Never say** "LVDT measures displacement directly" without mentioning **AC excitation and phase-sensitive demodulation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuators and Control Systems - Types of Actuators (Basics Only) - Electromechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, and Piezoelectric; Overview of Relays and Solenoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Actuators and Control Systems - Types of Actuators (Basics Only) - Electromechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, and Piezoelectric; Overview of Relays and Solenoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuator? (The "Muscle" of a Mechatronic System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a **physical motion** (linear displacement, rotation, or force/torque). It is the **output element** of a mechatronic system that performs the actual work on the environment.

In a mechatronic chain — `Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Controller → Actuator → Process` — the actuator is the **final stage** that interacts with the physical world.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Think of a mechatronic system like a **human body**:
- **Brain** → Microcontroller/Processor
- **Nerves** → Wires/Buses
- **Senses** → Sensors
- **Muscles** → **Actuators** ✓

Just as muscles convert nerve signals (electrical) into physical movement, actuators convert controller signals into mechanical action (push, pull, rotate, lift).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Actuators (KTU Module 2 Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus restricts this module to **basic types only**. The four families are:

| Sl. No. | Type | Energy Domain Converted From | Typical Output |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Electromechanical** (DC Motor, Stepper, Servo) | Electrical → Mechanical (rotation) | Rotary motion / precise position |
| 2 | **Hydraulic** | Fluid Pressure → Mechanical | Large linear force, heavy lifting |
| 3 | **Pneumatic** | Compressed Air → Mechanical | Fast, clean linear motion |
| 4 | **Piezoelectric** | Electrical → Mechanical (micro-strain) | Sub-micron precision displacement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU asks only for *basics* — operating principle, one-line definition, typical application, advantage, and limitation. No deep mathematical modeling of each actuator type is required at this stage.

---

## 1.4 Electromechanical Actuators — Basics

**Definition:** A device that converts **electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy** using the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors (Lorentz force principle) or the attraction/repulsion of ferromagnetic parts.

**Common Forms:**
- **DC Motors** — Continuous rotation, speed controlled by armature voltage.
- **Stepper Motors** — Discrete angular steps (e.g., **1.8°** per step), open-loop positioning.
- **Servo Motors** — Closed-loop, high precision rotary/linear positioning.
- **Solenoids** — Linear push/pull (covered separately under relays).

**Real-world analogy:** A ceiling fan's motor — flip a switch, the coil energizes, the rotor spins.

---

## 1.5 Hydraulic Actuators — Basics

**Definition:** A device that uses a **pressurized incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil)** flowing through a cylinder to generate linear or rotary motion.

**Key Components:**
- Reservoir (tank) → Pump → Control Valves → Cylinder/Motor → Return line

**Real-world analogy:** The hydraulic jack used to lift a car — small force on a small piston creates huge pressure, which lifts a heavy load on a larger piston.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constant:** Hydraulic systems operate at pressures of **70 – 700 bar** (≈ **7 – 70 MPa**). Standard working fluid is **ISO VG 32 / VG 46 hydraulic oil**.

---

## 1.6 Pneumatic Actuators — Basics

**Definition:** A device that converts the energy of **compressed air (compressible gas)** into linear or rotary mechanical motion.

**Key Components:**
- Air Compressor → Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL) → Directional Control Valve (DCV) → Cylinder/Air Motor → Exhaust

**Real-world analogy:** A dentist's air-driven drill, or the brakes on a heavy truck — air does the pushing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pressure:** Industrial pneumatics operate at **6 – 8 bar (≈ 600 – 800 kPa)** gauge pressure.

---

## 1.7 Piezoelectric Actuators — Basics

**Definition:** A solid-state device that produces a **small mechanical strain (typically 0.1% – 0.2%)** when subjected to an electric field — this is the **converse piezoelectric effect**, discovered in 1880 by Jacques and Pierre Curie.

**Working Principle:** Certain crystals (Quartz, Lead Zirconate Titanate — **PZT**, Barium Titanate) deform by a tiny but extremely precise amount when a voltage is applied across them.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a speaker in reverse — instead of vibration producing sound, an electric signal produces a tiny, controlled vibration/displacement. Used in atomic force microscopes (AFM), inkjet printer heads, and precision lens positioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material of Choice:** **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** is the most widely used piezoelectric ceramic because of its high piezoelectric charge coefficient **d₃₃ ≈ 300 – 600 × 10⁻¹² m/V** (pC/N).

---

## 1.8 Relays — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **Relay** is an **electromagnetic switch** in which a small control current through a coil mechanically operates (opens/closes) one or more sets of contacts that switch a separate, often higher-power, circuit.

**Why relays are used:**
- **Isolation:** A 5 V microcontroller signal can switch a **230 V AC** appliance.
- **Amplification:** Small currents (≈ **10 – 100 mA** for coil) can switch large currents (up to **30 A** for contacts).
- **Logic Implementation:** Ladder logic in PLCs is built using relay symbols historically.

---

## 1.9 Solenoids — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **Solenoid** is a **cylindrical coil of wire** (often wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger/core) that produces a controlled **linear mechanical force** when energized. It is a *linear* electromechanical actuator.

**Key Fact:** The word "solenoid" comes from the Greek *solen* (channel/pipe) + *eidos* (form). A pure inductor is a "solenoid"; an actuator version includes a movable core.

**Typical Applications:** Car starter motor, door locks, fuel injectors, valve actuators, vending machines, dot-matrix printers.

---

## 1.10 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Electromechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Piezoelectric |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Energy Source | Electric current | Pressurized oil | Compressed air | Electric field |
| Output Type | Rotary (mostly) | Linear / Rotary | Linear / Rotary | Micro-displacement |
| Precision | Medium–High | Medium | Low–Medium | Ultra-High (nm) |
| Force/Stroke | Medium / Medium | **Very High** / Long | Low–Medium / Long | **Very Low** / Very Short |
| Speed | High | Medium | Very High | Very High (response) |
| Cleanliness | Clean | Leaky (oil) | **Very Clean** | **Ultra Clean** |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High | Low | Very High |
| Example Use | Robot arm joint | Excavator arm | Pick-and-place | AFM probe positioner |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Actuator Model

Every actuator, regardless of physical principle, can be mathematically represented as an **energy converter** with the following functional blocks:

$$
\text{Input Energy} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Converter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mechanical Output} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Load}
$$

The **output power** of any actuator is:

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v \quad \text{(linear)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad P_{out} = T \cdot \omega \quad \text{(rotary)}
$$

where $F$ = force (N), $v$ = linear velocity (m/s), $T$ = torque (N·m), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

The **efficiency** of an actuator is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out,\;mech}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 2.2 Electromechanical Actuators — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor (DC Motor Basis)

When a conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ (perpendicular), it experiences a force:

$$
F = B \cdot I \cdot L
$$

This is the **Lorentz Force** — the foundational equation of all DC motors and many electromechanical actuators.

- $F$ in **Newtons (N)**
- $B$ in **Tesla (T)**
- $I$ in **Amperes (A)**
- $L$ in **metres (m)**

### 2.2.2 Torque of a DC Motor

For a motor with $N$ conductors arranged on the armature, average flux density $B$, effective radius $r$, and current $I_a$:

$$
T = K_t \cdot I_a \quad \text{where} \quad K_t = \frac{B \cdot N \cdot L \cdot r}{2\pi}
$$

- $K_t$ = torque constant (N·m/A)
- Back-EMF equation: $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$, with $K_e = K_t$ in SI units.

### 2.2.3 Stepper Motor — Step Angle

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N \cdot m}
$$

where $N$ = number of teeth on rotor, $m$ = number of stator phases × number of pole pairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A common hybrid stepper has **50 teeth** and operates in **full-step (4-phase)** mode → $\theta_{step} = 360°/(50 \times 4) = 1.8°$ per step.

---

## 2.3 Hydraulic Actuators — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 Pascal's Law (Foundation of All Hydraulics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** Pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to every surface in contact with the fluid.

### 2.3.2 Force Generated by a Hydraulic Cylinder

For a double-acting cylinder with piston area $A_p$ supplied with pressure $P$:

$$
F_{out} = P \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_r
$$

For a single-rod cylinder (asymmetric areas) during **extension**:

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

During **retraction** (annulus area $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$, where $d$ is rod diameter):

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

### 2.3.3 Hydraulic Power Equation

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q
$$

- $P$ in **Pa**, $Q$ in **m³/s** → $P_{hyd}$ in **Watts (W)**
- **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa**

### 2.3.4 Mechanical Advantage of a Hydraulic Press

$$
\frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{A_{out}}{A_{in}}
$$

This is why a small input force (small piston) lifts a huge load (large piston).

---

## 2.4 Pneumatic Actuators — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.4.1 Cylinder Force (Pneumatic)

Same form as hydraulic, but air is compressible — so we use **effective working pressure**:

$$
F_{out} = \eta_m \cdot P_{gauge} \cdot A_p
$$

where $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**) accounting for friction and load-back pressure.

### 2.4.2 Air Consumption

$$
Q_{air} = \frac{V_{cyl}}{\eta_v} \cdot \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}}
$$

- $V_{cyl}$ = geometric cylinder volume
- $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency
- $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$

### 2.4.3 Ideal Gas Law (Basis of Pneumatic Calculations)

$$
P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T
$$

where $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — universal gas constant.

---

## 2.5 Piezoelectric Actuators — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.5.1 Converse Piezoelectric Effect (Core Equation)

The mechanical strain $S$ produced by an applied electric field $E$ is:

$$
S = d \cdot E
$$

where $d$ = **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (m/V or pC/N).

The resulting **displacement** of a piezo element of length $L$ is:

$$
\Delta L = d \cdot V \cdot \frac{L}{t} = d \cdot E \cdot L
$$

where $V$ = applied voltage, $t$ = element thickness, $E = V/t$ = electric field.

### 2.5.2 Generated Force (Blocking Force)

A piezoelectric stack actuator's **maximum blocking force** (force at zero displacement) is:

$$
F_{block} = \frac{A \cdot d \cdot V}{s^{E}_{33} \cdot L} = A \cdot E \cdot d \cdot \frac{1}{s^{E}_{33}}
$$

where $s^{E}_{33}$ = elastic compliance at constant E-field (≈ **15 × 10⁻¹² m²/N** for PZT).

### 2.5.3 Typical Strain of PZT

$$
S_{max} \approx 0.1\% \text{ to } 0.2\% \quad (\text{under } E \approx 2 \text{ kV/mm})
$$

---

## 2.6 Relays — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.6.1 Coil Resistance & Pickup Voltage

A relay coil is a wire-wound inductor. Its DC resistance:

$$
R_{coil} = \frac{\rho \cdot l_{wire}}{A_{wire}} \quad \text{with} \quad \rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}
$$

The **pickup voltage** $V_{pickup}$ is the minimum coil voltage that pulls the armature in. Typically:

$$
V_{pickup} \approx 0.7 \times V_{rated}
$$

The **dropout voltage** $V_{dropout}$ is when the armature releases:

$$
V_{dropout} \approx 0.3 \times V_{rated}
$$

### 2.6.2 Coil Time Constant (Relay Switching Speed)

$$
\tau = \frac{L_{coil}}{R_{coil}} \quad ; \quad t_{operate} \approx 3\tau \text{ to } 5\tau
$$

where $L_{coil}$ is the coil inductance in Henries.

---

## 2.7 Solenoids — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.7.1 Magnetic Field Inside a Long Solenoid

For an air-core solenoid of $N$ turns, length $l$, carrying current $I$:

$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{l}
$$

where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\;\text{H/m}$ — permeability of free space.

### 2.7.2 Inductance of a Solenoid

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}
$$

- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the coil
- $l$ = length of the coil
- Units: **Henry (H)**

### 2.7.3 Force on a Solenoid Plunger (Approximate)

The axial pulling force on a ferromagnetic plunger (magnetic circuit method):

$$
F \approx \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 l^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** Force on a solenoid is **proportional to I²**, i.e., it works on **AC as well as DC** (the direction of force does not change with current sign because I² is always positive). This is why AC solenoids are feasible.

### 2.7.4 Stored Magnetic Energy in a Solenoid

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2
$$

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations that appear in KTU Part-A and Part-B questions.**

| # | Topic | Formula | Variable Meaning | SI Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Lorentz Force (Motor) | $F = BIL$ | B = flux density, I = current, L = conductor length | N, T, A, m |
| 2 | Motor Torque | $T = K_t I_a$ | K_t = torque const, I_a = armature current | N·m, N·m/A, A |
| 3 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \omega$ | K_e = back-EMF const, ω = angular speed | V, rad/s |
| 4 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta = 360°/(N \cdot m)$ | N = teeth, m = phase factor | degrees |
| 5 | Hydraulic Force (Ext.) | $F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | P = pressure, D = bore dia | N, Pa, m |
| 6 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$ | P = press, Q = flow rate | W, Pa, m³/s |
| 7 | Mech. Advantage | $F_{out}/F_{in} = A_{out}/A_{in}$ | Pascal's law | dimensionless |
| 8 | Pneumatic Cyl. Force | $F = \eta_m \cdot P \cdot A$ | η_m = mech. efficiency | N, Pa |
| 9 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Pa, m³, mol, K |
| 10 | Piezo Strain | $S = d \cdot E$ | d = piezo coeff, E = electric field | m/V, V/m |
| 11 | Piezo Displacement | $\Delta L = d \cdot V \cdot L/t$ | V = voltage, t = thickness | m, V, m, m |
| 12 | Solenoid B-field | $B = \mu_0 N I / l$ | μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ H/m | T, H/m, A, m |
| 13 | Solenoid Inductance | $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / l$ | A = cross-section area | H, m², m |
| 14 | Solenoid Force | $F = B^2 A / (2\mu_0)$ | Pulling force on plunger | N, T, m², H/m |
| 15 | Magnetic Energy | $W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Stored energy in inductor | J, H, A |
| 16 | Relay Time Const. | $\tau = L/R$ | L = coil ind., R = coil res. | s, H, Ω |
| 17 | Coil Resistance | $R = \rho l / A$ | ρ = resistivity of Cu = 1.72×10⁻⁸ Ω·m | Ω |
| 18 | Actuator Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$ | Power ratio | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use SI units. State the units explicitly in every answer. Marks are deducted when units are omitted, especially in numerical problems.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Actuator Type | Use Case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Industrial Robotics | Electromechanical (Servo) | Joint articulation, pick-and-place |
| Earth-moving Equipment (JCB, Excavator) | Hydraulic | Boom, arm, bucket actuation |
| Food / Pharma Industry | Pneumatic | Clean, oil-free motion (valves, conveyors) |
| Semiconductor Lithography | Piezoelectric | Sub-nanometer wafer-stage positioning |
| Automotive Starter | Solenoid | Engages starter motor pinion to flywheel |
| PLC Ladder Logic | Relays | Switching motor starters, contactors |
| MEMS / Nanotechnology | Piezoelectric | AFM tip scanning, nanopositioning |
| Aircraft Flight Control | Hydraulic + Electromechanical (fly-by-wire) | Ailerons, rudders, flaps |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Multiplication in a Hydraulic Press (Pascal's Law)

### Statement
Using Pascal's Law, derive the mechanical advantage of a hydraulic lift system.

### Given
- Input piston area: $A_1$ (m²)
- Output piston area: $A_2$ (m²)
- Input force: $F_1$ (N)
- Output force: $F_2$ (N) — to be determined
- Pressure: $P$ (Pa) — same throughout (Pascal's Law)

### Step 1 — Pressure at the Input Piston
Pressure created by the small input force $F_1$ on area $A_1$:

$$
P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law
By Pascal's Law, the pressure is transmitted undiminished to the output piston:

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} = P
$$

### Step 3 — Output Force on the Larger Piston
The same pressure $P$ acts on the larger area $A_2$:

$$
F_2 = P \cdot A_2
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Expression for $P$

$$
F_2 = \left(\frac{F_1}{A_1}\right) \cdot A_2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}\;}
$$

This is the **mechanical advantage** of a hydraulic press.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** If $A_2 = 100 \cdot A_1$, then $F_2 = 100 \cdot F_1$. A 100 N push generates 10,000 N of lifting force. Energy is *conserved* (with losses), but force is *multiplied* — at the cost of stroke distance: $A_1 \cdot d_1 = A_2 \cdot d_2$ (incompressible fluid → volume conservation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solenoid Pulling Force from Magnetic Energy

### Step 1 — Inductance of a Solenoid of Length $l$

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}
$$

### Step 2 — Stored Magnetic Energy

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} \cdot I^2
$$

### Step 3 — Virtual Work Method
The mechanical work done as the plunger moves by $dx$ (force $F$ in the direction of motion) equals the change in stored energy at constant current (this is a force–current analogy for inductors):

$$
F \cdot dx = -dW
$$

Differentiating $W$ w.r.t. $l$ (since increasing $l$ decreases stored energy):

$$
F = -\frac{dW}{dl} = -\frac{d}{dl}\left(\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2l}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiation

$$
F = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{l^2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 l^2}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent B-field Form
Since $B = \mu_0 N I / l$:

$$
F = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}
$$

This is the **Maxwell Stress / Magnetic Pressure** form — force per unit area on the ferromagnetic plunger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Force is proportional to $I^2$ (squared). This explains why:
> 1. The force is **unidirectional** even with AC (since $I^2 > 0$ always).
> 2. Doubling current gives **4× the force** (not 2×).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoelectric Displacement of a Stack Actuator

### Setup
A **piezo stack** consists of $n$ thin PZT discs of thickness $t$ each, electrically in parallel, mechanically in series.

### Step 1 — Strain in a Single Layer
For applied electric field $E$ across one layer:

$$
S = d_{33} \cdot E
$$

where $d_{33}$ is the longitudinal piezoelectric coefficient.

### Step 2 — Electric Field per Layer
Each layer sees the full applied voltage $V$ across its own thickness $t$:

$$
E = \frac{V}{t}
$$

### Step 3 — Displacement of a Single Layer

$$
\Delta L_{single} = S \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot E \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V
$$

### Step 4 — Displacement of the Stack ($n$ Layers)

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta L_{stack} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** For higher displacement, increase the **number of layers** $n$ (e.g., $n = 100$ gives 100× the displacement of a single layer). For higher force, increase the **cross-sectional area** $A$. This is the designer's leverage in piezo stack design.

### Numerical Example
Given: $n = 100$, $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m/V}$, $V = 100\;\text{V}$.

$$
\Delta L = 100 \times 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 4 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} = 4\;\mu\text{m}
$$

A 4-micron displacement — typical for precision nanopositioning.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stepper Motor Step Angle

### Setup
A hybrid stepper has:
- Rotor teeth: $N_r = 50$
- Stator pole pairs: 4 (8 poles)

### Step 1 — Rotor Pitch
Angle between adjacent rotor teeth (mechanical):

$$
\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{50} = 7.2°
$$

### Step 2 — Stator Pole Pitch
Stator poles are spaced $360°/(2 \times \text{number of pole pairs})$ apart. For 4 pole pairs (8 poles):

$$
\theta_{stator} = \frac{360°}{8} = 45°
$$

### Step 3 — Step Angle (Geometric Offset Per Phase)

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{\theta_{tooth}}{\text{phases}} = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}
$$

With $m = 4$ (full-step, 2-phase ON), $N_r = 50$:

$$
\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8° \text{ per step}
$$

### Step 4 — Steps per Revolution

$$
\text{SPR} = \frac{360°}{\theta_{step}} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \;\text{steps/rev}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microstepping:** Modern drives can divide each full step into 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/64, 1/128, or 1/256 microsteps, achieving effective resolutions up to **51,200 steps/rev** from a 1.8° motor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Selection

| Selection Criterion | Electromechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Piezoelectric |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Force Range** | 1 N – 10 kN | **10 kN – 10 MN** | 10 N – 50 kN | 0.1 N – 10 kN |
| **Stroke Length** | Unlimited (rotary) | Up to 2 m | Up to 1 m | **< 1 mm** |
| **Position Resolution** | 0.01° (servo) | ~1 mm | ~0.5 mm | **0.1 nm** |
| **Response Time** | 1 – 100 ms | 50 – 500 ms | **< 10 ms** | **< 1 μs** |
| **Stiffness** | Medium | Medium | Low | **Very High** |
| **Operating Environment** | Clean, dry | Indoor (oil) | **Clean rooms OK** | **Vacuum OK** |
| **Power Density** | High | **Very High** | Medium | High (volumetric) |
| **Maintenance** | Brushes (DC) | Oil leak, filter | Filter, lubricant | **Almost none** |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | **Very High** | Low | **High** |
| **Operating Cost** | Low (electricity) | High (pumps, oil) | Medium (compressor) | Very Low |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Question:** A hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 80\;\text{mm}$ and a rod diameter $d = 35\;\text{mm}$. It is supplied with fluid at $P = 100\;\text{bar}$. Calculate: (a) the extension force, (b) the retraction force, (c) the hydraulic power if the extension velocity is $v = 0.1\;\text{m/s}$.

### Part (a) — Extension Force
Piston area (full):

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2
$$

Force:

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = (100 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} = 50{,}265\;\text{N} \approx 50.27\;\text{kN}}
$$

### Part (b) — Retraction Force
Annulus area:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left((0.080)^2 - (0.035)^2\right)
$$

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.00640 - 0.001225) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005175) = 4.064 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2
$$

Force:

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = (100 \times 10^5) \times (4.064 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ret} = 40{,}640\;\text{N} \approx 40.64\;\text{kN}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ by a factor of $\frac{D^2}{D^2-d^2} = \frac{50.27}{40.64} \approx 1.237$. This is why hydraulic cylinders are *asymmetric* — the rod side has less area, hence less force on retraction.

### Part (c) — Hydraulic Power
Flow rate $Q = A_p \cdot v = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$

Power:

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = (100 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = 5{,}026.5\;\text{W} \approx 5.03\;\text{kW}}
$$

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram 7 — Energy Conversion Chain in a Mechatronic System

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1[Electrical Input]:::e --> E2[Controller and Drive]
    E2 --> E3[Actuator Stage]
    E3 --> E4[Mechanical Motion]
    E4 --> E5[Process or Load]
    E5 --> E6[Feedback via Sensor]
    E6 -.->|Closed loop signal| E2

    A1[Solenoid: E to Linear]:::a -.-> E3
    A2[Relay: E to Switch]:::a -.-> E2
    A3[Hydraulic: Pressure to Force]:::a -.-> E3
    A4[Pneumatic: Air to Motion]:::a -.-> E3
    A5[Piezo: E to Micro-strain]:::a -.-> E3

    classDef e fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000,stroke:#2e75b6;
    classDef a fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000,stroke:#bf9000;
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part-A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Direct Recall)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any four types of actuators used in mechatronic systems. Give one application of each."**

**Model Answer (4×½ mark list + 4×½ mark app = 4 marks + adjustment for 3-mark format):**

The four major types of actuators used in mechatronic systems are:

| Sl. No. | Actuator Type | Energy Conversion | One Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Electromechanical** (DC/Stepper/Servo) | Electrical → Mechanical (rotary) | Robotic arm joint |
| 2 | **Hydraulic** | Pressure (oil) → Mechanical | Excavator boom |
| 3 | **Pneumatic** | Compressed air → Mechanical | Industrial pick-and-place cylinder |
| 4 | **Piezoelectric** | Electrical → Micro-strain | AFM nanopositioner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners give **½ mark for naming** + **½ mark for a valid application**. 4 × 1 = 4 → trimmed to **3 marks** by giving 3 types and 3 applications in the actual answer, or by partial credit.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"State Pascal's Law. How is it used in hydraulic actuators?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *"Pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, acting perpendicular to every surface in contact with it."*

**Application in Hydraulic Actuators (1 mark):**
Pascal's Law is the working principle of hydraulic cylinders and presses. A small input force $F_1$ applied on a small piston of area $A_1$ generates pressure $P = F_1/A_1$. This pressure is transmitted through the hydraulic oil to a larger piston of area $A_2$, producing a magnified output force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = (A_2/A_1) \cdot F_1$. This is the basis of all hydraulic lifts, jacks, and braking systems.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part-B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Omitting the principle of operation** = −2 marks immediately.
> - **Not drawing a labeled diagram** = −2 marks. Even a hand-drawn block diagram is acceptable.
> - **No comparison table at the end** of comparative questions = −1 mark.
> - **Wrong units or missing units** in numerical sub-questions = −1 to −2 marks.
> - **Confusing solenoid with relay** (or vice versa) = −1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **solenoid** with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the force exerted on the plunger.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **classification of actuators** used in mechatronic systems. Compare **hydraulic** and **pneumatic** actuators on at least six parameters.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **solenoid** is an electromechanical device consisting of a cylindrical coil of insulated wire wound on a former, with a movable **ferromagnetic plunger (core)** that translates linearly when the coil is energized.

**2. Construction (1 mark):**
- **Helical coil** of $N$ turns of insulated copper wire
- **Bobbin/former** for mechanical support
- **Ferromagnetic plunger** (soft iron) free to slide along the axis
- **Return spring** to restore position when de-energized
- **Frame/yoke** completing the magnetic circuit

**3. Working Principle (2 marks):**
When DC current $I$ flows through the coil, a magnetic field is set up along the axis of the coil (right-hand thumb rule). The magnetic flux lines prefer the path through the soft-iron plunger (low reluctance). The plunger experiences a strong axial pull into the coil region, generating a linear mechanical force. The spring is compressed. When the coil is de-energized, the spring pushes the plunger back to its rest position. This linear push-pull motion is used to operate valves, latches, locks, etc.

**4. Sketch (1 mark):**
*(Student should draw: coil + plunger + spring + magnetic flux lines + input terminals + mechanical output rod)*

**5. Derivation of Force (2 marks):**
Magnetic field inside a long solenoid of length $l$:

$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}
$$

Inductance:

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}
$$

Stored magnetic energy:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2l}
$$

Using virtual work principle, force on plunger:

$$
F = -\frac{dW}{dl} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 l^2} = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**6. Key Observation (½ mark):** Force $\propto I^2$ — direction is independent of current sign → works on AC and DC.

**7. Application (½ mark):** Used in car starter motors, fuel injectors, door locks, etc.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**1. Functional Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw: Input controller → Actuator types branch → Output: linear/rotary motion → Feedback sensor → back to controller)*

The 4 major actuator classes in mechatronics:
- Electromechanical (rotary motors, solenoids)
- Hydraulic (cylinder/motor driven by oil pressure)
- Pneumatic (cylinder/air motor driven by compressed air)
- Piezoelectric (crystal deformation under electric field)

**2. Comparison Table — Hydraulic vs Pneumatic (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Working Fluid | Incompressible oil (ISO VG 32/46) | Compressible air |
| Working Pressure | 70 – 700 bar | 6 – 8 bar |
| Force Output | **Very high (up to MN)** | Low to medium (up to 50 kN) |
| Speed | Medium (limited by oil flow) | **Very high** |
| Precision | **High** (less compressible) | Lower (air is springy) |
| Cleanliness | Can leak oil (not clean room) | **Very clean** (only exhaust air) |
| Cost | High (pump, tank, valves, oil) | Low (simple compressor) |
| Applications | Excavator, press, brake, jack | Pick-and-place, packaging, dental tools |

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):** Hydraulic = high force, precision. Pneumatic = clean, fast, low force.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the working of **(i) a hydraulic cylinder** and **(ii) a pneumatic cylinder**. State **Pascal's Law** and **derive the expression for force multiplication** in a hydraulic press.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **converse piezoelectric effect**? Derive the expression for the **displacement of a piezoelectric stack actuator**. List **four applications** of piezoelectric actuators.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Pascal's Law — Statement (1 mark):**
*"Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, acting perpendicular to the containing surfaces."*

**2. Hydraulic Cylinder — Working (2 marks):**
- A **double-acting cylinder** has ports on both sides of the piston.
- Pressurized oil from the pump enters the cap-end port → pushes the piston outward → extends the rod.
- To retract, oil is directed to the rod-end port, and the cap-end oil returns to the reservoir.
- Force generated: $F = P \cdot A$ where $A$ is the active piston area.

**3. Pneumatic Cylinder — Working (1.5 marks):**
- Operates on **compressed air** instead of oil.
- A **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** routes air to one side of the piston.
- Force: $F = \eta_m \cdot P \cdot A$ (where $\eta_m ≈ 0.9$ accounts for friction).
- Typically **single-acting** (spring return) or **double-acting** (air on both sides).

**4. Force-Multiplication Derivation (2.5 marks):**
*(Same derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes — step by step)*

**Result:** $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1$ — verified by substituting Pascal's Law.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**1. Converse Piezoelectric Effect — Definition (1 mark):**
When an **electric field** is applied across certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (Quartz, PZT, Barium Titanate), the crystal undergoes **mechanical strain** (deformation). This is the *converse* of the *direct* effect (where stress produces charge).

**2. Governing Equation (1 mark):**

$$
S = d \cdot E
$$

where $S$ = strain (dimensionless), $d$ = piezoelectric coefficient (m/V), $E$ = electric field (V/m).

**3. Derivation — Stack Displacement (3 marks):**
*(Same as Section 3.3 of these notes)*
- Single layer displacement: $\Delta L_{single} = d \cdot V$
- Stack of $n$ layers: $\boxed{\Delta L_{stack} = n \cdot d \cdot V}$
- **Insight:** Increasing $n$ or $V$ increases displacement.

**4. Numerical Demonstration (1 mark):**
For $n = 50$, $d = 400 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m/V}$, $V = 150\;\text{V}$:

$$
\Delta L = 50 \times 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 150 = 3 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} = 3\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**5. Four Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** — nanopositioning of probe
2. **Inkjet printer heads** — micro-droplet ejection
3. **Precision optical lens focus** in cameras and microscopes
4. **Microfluidic valves** in lab-on-chip devices

*(Alternates: ultrasonic cleaning, fuel injectors, vibration damping in smart structures)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] **Actuator** = energy conversion device that produces physical motion
- [ ] **Solenoid** = coil + movable ferromagnetic plunger → **linear force**
- [ ] **Relay** = electromagnetic switch → **opens/closes contacts**, isolates control from power
- [ ] **Pascal's Law** = pressure transmitted equally in confined fluid (basis of hydraulics)
- [ ] **Converse Piezoelectric Effect** = electric field → mechanical strain (Curie brothers, 1880)
- [ ] **PZT** = most common piezoelectric ceramic material

### ✅ Must-Know Formulas
- [ ] $F = BIL$ — Lorentz force (basis of motors)
- [ ] $F = P \cdot A$ — cylinder force (hydraulic & pneumatic)
- [ ] $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1$ — hydraulic force multiplication
- [ ] $B = \mu_0 N I / l$ — solenoid field
- [ ] $F = B^2 A / (2\mu_0)$ — solenoid plunger force
- [ ] $F \propto I^2$ — force is independent of current direction
- [ ] $\Delta L_{stack} = n \cdot d \cdot V$ — piezo stack displacement
- [ ] $S = d \cdot E$ — piezoelectric strain equation
- [ ] $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$ — hydraulic power
- [ ] $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$ — efficiency

### ✅ Selection Heuristics
- [ ] **High force + precise** → **Hydraulic**
- [ ] **Clean + fast + low force** → **Pneumatic**
- [ ] **Rotary continuous + speed control** → **DC motor**
- [ ] **Open-loop discrete steps** → **Stepper**
- [ ] **Closed-loop precision rotary** → **Servo**
- [ ] **Sub-micron displacement** → **Piezoelectric**
- [ ] **Switching high voltage from low voltage signal** → **Relay**
- [ ] **Linear push/pull mechanism** → **Solenoid**

### ✅ Numerical Constants to Memorize
- [ ] $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\;\text{H/m}$ (permeability of free space)
- [ ] $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ (copper resistivity)
- [ ] $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$ (universal gas constant)
- [ ] 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa
- [ ] $1.8°$ per step = standard hybrid stepper resolution
- [ ] Industrial pneumatics: **6 – 8 bar** working pressure
- [ ] Industrial hydraulics: **70 – 700 bar** working pressure
- [ ] PZT: $d_{33} \approx 300 - 600 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m/V}$

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse **relay** (a switch) with **solenoid** (an actuator)
- [ ] Don't forget **units** in every numerical answer
- [ ] Don't claim solenoids "work only on DC" — they work on AC too (force $\propto I^2$)
- [ ] Don't apply $F = P \cdot A$ without specifying **which side** (extension uses $A_p$, retraction uses annulus area $A_r$)
- [ ] Don't forget to draw a **labeled diagram** in descriptive questions
- [ ] Don't mix up **piezoelectric** (electric → strain) with **electrostrictive** (quadratic in E)
- [ ] In stepper motors, **open-loop** means no position feedback — lost steps can occur

### ✅ Key Application Trivia (KTU loves asking "Give an example of…")
- [ ] Solenoid → **Car starter motor engagement**
- [ ] Relay → **Automotive horn circuit** / **PLC output module**
- [ ] Hydraulic → **JCB backhoe / Excavator boom**
- [ ] Pneumatic → **Cinema seat recline / Dental drill**
- [ ] Piezoelectric → **AFM / Inkjet print head / Quartz watch oscillator**
- [ ] DC motor → **Conveyor belt / Fan / Pump**
- [ ] Stepper motor → **3D printer axis / CNC small machine**
- [ ] Servo motor → **RC airplane control surface / Robot joint**

### ✅ Final One-Line Summary
> *"**Actuators** are the muscles of mechatronics — converting electrical, fluid, or field energy into the mechanical work that moves the physical world; the right choice depends on force, speed, precision, and cleanliness demanded by the application.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Motors (Working Only) - DC, Stepper, and Servo Motors; Motor Control Techniques - PWM Control, H-Bridge Circuits, Motor Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Motors in Mechatronics — The Definitional Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical (rotational) energy using the interaction between magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors, governed by the **Lorentz Force Law** (F = BIL) and **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

In mechatronic systems, motors serve as the **final actuation element** (the "muscle" of the system), receiving low-power control signals from microcontrollers / PLCs and producing controlled mechanical motion at the output shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Motor Families Covered in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **DC Motors** — Continuous rotation, speed/torque controlled by voltage.
> 2. **Stepper Motors** — Discrete angular steps, open-loop position control.
> 3. **Servo Motors** — Closed-loop position/velocity control.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Motors as Water Wheels"

Imagine a water wheel. Water flow (current) into the wheel pushes paddles inside a magnetic field to produce rotation. In a DC motor, instead of water, we use electrons flowing through conductors placed between **permanent magnets** or **electromagnets**.

- **More voltage** → More electron pressure → Faster rotation.
- **More load** → More mechanical resistance → Motor draws more current to maintain torque.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the motor as a **translator**: it "translates" electrical commands (voltage/current) into mechanical language (rotation, torque, position).

---

## 1.3 The Three Motor Types — Quick Conceptual Overview

### 1.3.1 DC Motor (Brushed Permanent Magnet DC Motor)
> **Analogy:** A spinning merry-go-round pushed continuously by a stream of water — the harder you push (more voltage), the faster it spins.

- Uses **two brushes** and a **commutator** to reverse current direction every half-turn, ensuring unidirectional torque.
- Speed is roughly proportional to applied voltage.

### 1.3.2 Stepper Motor
> **Analogy:** A clock's second hand — instead of sweeping continuously, it jerks forward in tiny fixed **steps** (e.g., 1.8° per step).

- Rotates in **discrete angular increments** controlled by digital pulses.
- No feedback sensor required (**open-loop**).
- Holding torque maintains position even at standstill.

### 1.3.3 Servo Motor (RC/Hobby Servo used in Mechatronics)
> **Analogy:** A car's power steering — it constantly measures how far you turned the wheel and adjusts to reach the exact desired angle.

- Uses **closed-loop feedback** (internal potentiometer + comparator).
- Controlled by **PWM pulse width** (typically 1 ms → 0°, 1.5 ms → 90°, 2 ms → 180°).

---

## 1.4 Motor Control Techniques — The "How Do We Drive Them?" Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, etc.) outputs only **low-current logic signals** (e.g., 5 V, 20 mA). Motors need **high current** (often >1 A). So we need:
> 1. **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** — to vary average voltage → control speed.
> 2. **H-Bridge Circuit** — to reverse current direction → control direction.
> 3. **Motor Driver ICs** (e.g., L298N, L293D, DRV8871) — to amplify logic signals to motor-driving currents.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Back EMF constant (Kₑ)** | V·s/rad | DC motor |
| **Torque constant (Kₜ)** | N·m/A | DC motor |
| **Step angle (β)** | 1.8° (typical) | Stepper motor |
| **Servo PWM period** | **20 ms (50 Hz)** | RC Servo |
| **Servo pulse range** | **1 ms – 2 ms** | RC Servo |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DC Motor — Working Principle

A **Brushed Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Motor** consists of:
- **Stator:** Permanent magnets producing a radial magnetic field B.
- **Rotor (Armature):** A coil of wire wound on a slotted iron core, connected to a split-ring **commutator**.
- **Brushes:** Stationary carbon contacts sliding on the rotating commutator.

### Working Steps:
1. Current enters the armature coil via the brush-commutator pair.
2. According to **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, the current-carrying conductor in the magnetic field experiences a force **F = BIL**.
3. This force creates a **torque T = F × r** on the coil, producing rotation.
4. As the coil rotates past 180°, the commutator **reverses the current direction** so that torque remains unidirectional.
5. The rotating coil cuts magnetic flux lines, generating a **Back EMF** opposing the applied voltage (Lenz's Law).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Without the commutator, the coil would simply oscillate (not rotate) because torque would reverse every half cycle.

---

## 2.2 DC Motor — Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Back EMF
$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (RPM)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) = $\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

### 2.2.2 Armature Voltage Equation (KVL around armature loop)
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Where:
- $V$ = Applied armature voltage
- $I_a$ = Armature current
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance

### 2.2.3 Torque Equation
$$T = K_t \cdot I_a = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2 \pi \cdot A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In SI units:** $K_e = K_t$ (numerically equal when consistent units are used). This is fundamental to DC motor analysis.

### 2.2.4 Speed Equation (Rearranging KVL)
$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$$

---

## 2.3 Stepper Motor — Working Principle

A stepper motor rotates in **discrete angular steps**, with each step triggered by a digital pulse sequence on its coils. The most common types in mechatronics are:

### 2.3.1 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper
- Rotor is a permanent magnet.
- Stator has multiple salient poles energized sequentially.
- Aligns with the energized stator pole due to magnetic attraction.

### 2.3.2 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper
- Rotor is a soft-iron toothed wheel (NOT magnetized).
- Rotor rotates to **minimize magnetic reluctance** (path of least resistance to flux).

### 2.3.3 Hybrid Stepper (Most Common — combines PM + VR principles)
- Used in CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics.

### Step Angle Formula:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = Step angle
- $N_s$ = Number of stator teeth/poles
- $m$ = Number of phases

> **Example:** A motor with 50 stator teeth and 2 phases → $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{50 \times 2} = 3.6°$ per step.

For **Variable Reluctance** type:
$$\beta = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \times 360°$$

### Steps per Revolution:
$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{\beta}$$

---

## 2.4 Servo Motor (RC Servo) — Working Principle

An RC servo is a **closed-loop position control device** consisting of:
1. A small **DC motor**.
2. A **gear train** (reduces speed, increases torque).
3. A **potentiometer** (rotational position sensor on output shaft).
4. A **control circuit** (compares setpoint vs. actual position).

### Working:
1. Controller sends a **PWM pulse** every **20 ms** (50 Hz).
2. Pulse width determines target angle: **1.0 ms → 0°**, **1.5 ms → 90°**, **2.0 ms → 180°**.
3. The internal control circuit compares the desired position (pulse width) with actual position (potentiometer feedback).
4. Motor drives the output shaft via gears **until the error → 0**.
5. Position is **held** even against external disturbance (holding torque).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "**1-2 ms controls 0-180°**" — pulse width in milliseconds, position in degrees.

---

## 2.5 PWM Control of Motors

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is used to vary the **average voltage** applied to a DC motor, controlling its speed without dissipating excess energy as heat.

### Duty Cycle:
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

### Average Motor Voltage:
$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$$

Where $V_s$ = supply voltage (e.g., 12 V).

### Motor Speed Approximation:
$$N \approx K \cdot V_{avg} = K \cdot D \cdot V_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM does NOT change the supply voltage — it merely switches it ON and OFF rapidly**, so the motor "sees" a lower effective voltage. Switching frequency must be **high enough** (>1 kHz typically) to prevent motor cogging/jerking.

---

## 2.6 H-Bridge Circuit

An **H-Bridge** is a circuit with **4 switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an "H" shape around the motor, allowing bidirectional current flow.

### Switching Truth Table:

| S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON | OFF | OFF | ON | **Forward** |
| OFF | ON | ON | OFF | **Reverse** |
| ON | OFF | ON | OFF | **Brake (dynamic)** |
| OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | **Coast (free run)** |
| ON | ON | X | X | **SHOOT-THROUGH (short circuit — forbidden!)** |

> [!WARNING]
> Never turn ON both switches on the same side (S1+S2 or S3+S4 simultaneously) — this creates a direct short across the supply and **destroys the switches / power supply**.

---

## 2.7 Motor Driver ICs

A **motor driver** is an integrated package containing:
- An H-bridge (or two H-bridges for bidirectional dual-motor control).
- Logic-level input interfaces (compatible with 3.3 V / 5 V microcontrollers).
- Built-in flyback diodes for inductive load protection.

### Common Driver ICs in KTU Curriculum:

| IC | Max Current | Voltage | Channels |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L293D** | 600 mA per channel | 4.5 – 36 V | 2 (dual H-bridge) |
| **L298N** | 2 A per channel | 5 – 35 V | 2 (dual H-bridge) |
| **DRV8871** | 3.6 A | 6.5 – 45 V | 1 (single H-bridge) |
| **A4988** | 2 A | 8 – 35 V | Stepper driver |

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = K_e \omega$ | Back EMF | V |
| 2 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL | V |
| 3 | $T = K_t I_a$ | Motor torque | N·m |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | Speed equation | rad/s |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s m}$ | Step angle | degrees |
| 6 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | Steps per revolution | — |
| 7 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$ | PWM duty cycle | % |
| 8 | $V_{avg} = D V_s$ | Average motor voltage | V |
| 9 | $\theta_{servo} = \dfrac{(t_{pulse} - 1\text{ ms})}{1\text{ ms}} \times 90°$ | Servo angle | degrees |
| 10 | $K_e = K_t$ (SI units) | Motor constant equivalence | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in:
> - **Robotics** — selecting motor + driver for joint actuation.
> - **CNC machines** — stepper motor sizing for precision positioning.
> - **EVs** — DC motor torque/speed curves for traction.
> - **Drones** — BLDC motor PWM control for propeller RPM.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Back EMF in a DC Motor

### Starting Point:
When the armature rotates at speed $N$ (RPM), each conductor cuts magnetic flux. According to **Faraday's Law**, the induced EMF per conductor is:

$$e = \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution:
For a single conductor, the total flux cut in one complete revolution is:
$$\Phi_{total} = P \cdot \Phi$$

where $P$ = number of poles (because the conductor passes $P$ poles per revolution).

### Step 2 — Time Per Revolution:
$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{(seconds per revolution)}$$

### Step 3 — EMF Per Conductor:
$$e = \frac{P \Phi}{T_{rev}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

The units are volts when $\Phi$ is in Webbs and $N$ is in RPM.

### Step 4 — Total Back EMF:
There are $Z$ conductors in $A$ parallel paths, so conductors in **series** per path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

The total EMF (sum of series EMFs per path):
$$E_b = e \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$

### Step 5 — Express in Angular Velocity:
Since $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$, we have $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2 \pi}$.

Substituting:
$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z}{60 A} \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2 \pi} = \frac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A} \cdot \omega$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E_b = K_e \cdot \omega}$$

where:
$$K_e = \frac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque in a DC Motor

### Step 1 — Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor:
A conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I_c$ in a magnetic field $B$ experiences:
$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot L$$

Since $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}}$, and each conductor carries $I_c = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$:

$$F = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L$$

### Step 2 — Torque Per Conductor:
If the conductor is at radius $r$ from the shaft:
$$T_{cond} = F \cdot r = \frac{\Phi \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r}{A \cdot A_{pole}}$$

### Step 3 — Total Torque:
There are $Z$ conductors, and due to the way forces add constructively in a multi-pole machine, we get:
$$T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify:
Define the torque constant:
$$K_t = \frac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$$

Then:
$$\boxed{T = K_t \cdot I_a}$$

### Step 5 — Show $K_e = K_t$ in SI Units:
From the previous derivations:
- $K_e = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$ (units: V·s/rad)
- $K_t = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$ (units: N·m/A)

In **SI units**, V·s/rad is dimensionally equal to N·m/A, so:
$$K_e = K_t \quad \text{(numerically, with consistent SI units)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This equality is why one can use a single constant $K$ in the simplified equations $E_b = K\omega$ and $T = K I_a$ in SI units.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed-Torque Characteristic of DC Motor

Starting from the two governing equations:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$T = K_t I_a \implies I_a = \frac{T}{K_t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute $E_b = K_e \omega$:
$$V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $I_a = T / K_t$:
$$V = K_e \omega + \frac{T R_a}{K_t}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\omega$:
$$K_e \omega = V - \frac{T R_a}{K_t}$$

$$\omega = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} T$$

### Step 4 — Identify No-Load Speed and Slope:
**No-load speed** ($T = 0$):
$$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e}$$

**Slope** (drop in speed with torque):
$$\text{Slope} = -\frac{R_a}{K_e K_t}$$

### Step 5 — Final Linear Characteristic:
$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 - \left( \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \right) T}$$

This is a **straight line** with:
- y-intercept = no-load speed $\omega_0$.
- Negative slope = how quickly speed drops with load torque.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (DC Motor Speed)

**Problem:** A PMDC motor has $R_a = 1\,\Omega$, $K_e = 0.05\,\text{V·s/rad}$. Supply voltage $V = 24\,\text{V}$. Load torque $T = 2\,\text{N·m}$, $K_t = 0.05\,\text{N·m/A}$. Find the steady-state speed.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute armature current from torque:
$$I_a = \frac{T}{K_t} = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute back EMF from KVL:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 24 - (40)(1) = 24 - 40 = -16\,\text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Negative $E_b$ indicates the motor cannot drive this load — it will stall. To handle this load, the supply voltage must be increased, or a motor with higher $K_e$ (more back EMF per speed) must be used.

### Re-Design Check:
For $E_b \geq 0$, we need $V \geq I_a R_a = 40\,\text{V}$.

If we use $V = 60\,\text{V}$:
$$E_b = 60 - 40 = 20\,\text{V}$$
$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{20}{0.05} = 400\,\text{rad/s}$$
$$N = \frac{400 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 3820\,\text{RPM}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Stepper Motor Step Angle

**Problem:** A 4-phase hybrid stepper has 50 rotor teeth. Find step angle and steps per revolution.

### Solution:
**Step 1:** Apply the step angle formula for hybrid stepper:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8°\text{ per step}$$

**Step 2:** Steps per revolution:
$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200\,\text{steps/rev}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **1.8° per step → 200 steps/revolution** is the most common stepper specification in 3D printers and CNC routers.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — PWM Duty Cycle for Speed Control

**Problem:** A DC motor rated 12 V is to be run at half speed using PWM. Find duty cycle.

### Solution:
For half speed, average voltage = 6 V:
$$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_s} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

So the PWM signal should be ON for 50% of each period.

If switching frequency is 1 kHz → $T_{period} = 1\,\text{ms}$ → $T_{on} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Servo Angle from Pulse Width

**Problem:** A servo receives a pulse of width 1.6 ms. Find the target shaft angle.

### Solution:
Using the linear interpolation formula:
$$\theta = \frac{(t_{pulse} - 1\,\text{ms})}{1\,\text{ms}} \times 90°$$

$$\theta = \frac{(1.6 - 1.0)}{1.0} \times 90° = 0.6 \times 90° = 54°$$

So the servo rotates to **54° from 0° reference**.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Motor

| Selection Criterion | DC Motor | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position control precision** | Low (open loop) | Medium-High | Very High (closed loop) |
| **Speed range** | High (continuous) | Low-Medium | Medium |
| **Torque at low speed** | Low | **Highest (holding torque)** | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Control complexity** | Simple (PWM) | Medium (pulse sequences) | Medium (PWM + feedback) |
| **Feedback required?** | No | No | **Yes (built-in)** |
| **Best for** | Fans, pumps, wheels | 3D printers, CNC | Robot joints, steering |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention **holding torque** when comparing stepper vs servo — it's the stepper's biggest advantage in vertical / load-holding applications.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Brushed DC Motor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply V] --> B[Brush Pair Positive]
    A --> C[Brush Pair Negative]
    B --> D[Commutator Segment A]
    C --> E[Commutator Segment B]
    D --> F[Armature Coil Conductor 1]
    E --> G[Armature Coil Conductor 2]
    F --> H[Force F equals BIL]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Torque T on Shaft]
    I --> J[Mechanical Output Rotation]
    J --> K[Back EMF induced in coil]
    K --> L[Opposes applied voltage]
    L --> M[Net voltage V minus Eb drives Ia]
    M --> N[Stator Permanent Magnets Supply B Field]
    N --> O[Brush Commutator Reverses Current every 180 degrees]
    O --> F
    O --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Stepper Motor — Excitation Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Phase A ON, B OFF] --> R1[Rotor aligns with Phase A pole]
    R1 --> P2[Step 2: Phase B ON, A OFF]
    P2 --> R2[Rotor rotates by beta degrees to align with Phase B]
    R2 --> P3[Step 3: Phase A Reverse ON, B OFF]
    P3 --> R3[Rotor rotates by beta more]
    R3 --> P4[Step 4: Phase B Reverse ON, A OFF]
    P4 --> R4[Rotor rotates by beta more]
    R4 --> P5{Step direction required}
    P5 --> FWD[Forward sequence: A B Abar Bbar A]
    P5 --> REV[Reverse sequence: A Bbar Abar B A]
    FWD --> EN[Full step 200 steps per rev]
    REV --> EN
    EN --> HM[Half step mode: 400 steps per rev via interleaved excitation]
    EN --> MI[Microstepping: 1600 to 25600 steps per rev]
```

---

## 4.3 Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[Microcontroller] -->|PWM pulse 1 to 2 ms| CMP[Comparator in Servo]
    POT[Output Shaft Potentiometer] -->|Voltage proportional to angle| CMP
    CMP -->|Error signal| DRV[Motor Driver Circuit]
    DRV --> DCM[Internal DC Motor]
    DCM --> GR[Gear Train]
    GR --> SHF[Output Shaft]
    SHF --> MECH[Mechanical Load]
    SHF --> POT
    CMP -->|When error equals 0| STB[Servo stops motor holds position]
```

---

## 4.4 H-Bridge Circuit — Switch Configuration States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Motor Direction] --> FWD{Forward}
    START --> REV{Reverse}
    START --> BRK{Brake}
    START --> CST{Coast}
    FWD --> S1[S1 ON]
    FWD --> S4[S4 ON]
    FWD --> S2[S2 OFF]
    FWD --> S3[S3 OFF]
    S1 --> MOTOR[Current flows through motor top to bottom]
    S4 --> MOTOR
    REV --> S2[S2 ON]
    REV --> S3[S3 ON]
    REV --> S1[S1 OFF]
    REV --> S4[S4 OFF]
    S2 --> MOTOR2[Current flows bottom to top motor reverses]
    S3 --> MOTOR2
    BRK --> S1ON[S1 ON]
    BRK --> S3ON[S3 ON]
    BRK --> S2OFF[S2 OFF]
    BRK --> S4OFF[S4 OFF]
    S1ON --> BRKACT[Low side shorts motor terminals dynamic braking]
    S3ON --> BRKACT
    CST --> ALLOFF[All switches OFF motor coasts]
    START -.FORBIDDEN.-> X[NEVER S1 plus S2 or S3 plus S4 short circuit]
```

---

## 4.5 Motor Driver IC — Internal Block Architecture (L298N Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[Logic Supply 5V] --> LOGIC[Logic Interface Level Shifter]
    VIN[Motor Supply up to 35V] --> POWER[Power Stage H-Bridge A]
    VIN --> POWERB[Power Stage H-Bridge B]
    LOGIC --> IN1[Input IN1]
    LOGIC --> IN2[Input IN2]
    LOGIC --> IN3[Input IN3]
    LOGIC --> IN4[Input IN4]
    IN1 --> POWER
    IN2 --> POWER
    IN3 --> POWERB
    IN4 --> POWERB
    POWER --> OUTA[Motor A Terminals OUT1 OUT2]
    POWERB --> OUTB[Motor B Terminals OUT3 OUT4]
    OUTA --> MOTORA[DC Motor 1 or Stepper Coil A]
    OUTB --> MOTORB[DC Motor 2 or Stepper Coil B]
    POWER --> DIODE[Internal Flyback Diodes for back EMF protection]
    POWERB --> DIODE
    ENA[Enable A PWM Input] --> POWER
    ENB[Enable B PWM Input] --> POWERB
```

---

## 4.6 PWM Signal — Conceptual Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OFF[Logic LOW 0V] --> ON[Logic HIGH Vcc]
    ON --> OFF2[Logic LOW 0V]
    OFF2 --> ON2[Logic HIGH Vcc]
    ON2 --> OFF3[Logic LOW 0V]
    OFF3 --> ON3[Logic HIGH Vcc]
    ON3 -.Period T repeats.-> CYCLE[Period T equals 20 ms for servo or 1 ms for DC motor PWM]
```

> **Visual Reference:**
> - **Duty Cycle 25%** → ON for short time, average V is low → slow motor.
> - **Duty Cycle 75%** → ON for long time, average V is high → fast motor.
> - **Duty Cycle 100%** → Fully ON → maximum speed.

---

## 4.7 Complete Mechatronic Motor Control System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensor Input] --> MCU[Microcontroller]
    USER[User Command] --> MCU
    MCU -->|PWM signal| DRV[Motor Driver IC]
    MCU -->|Direction Logic IN1 IN2| DRV
    DRV -->|High current to motor| MOT[DC or Stepper or Servo Motor]
    MOT --> MECH[Mechanical Load]
    MECH --> FB[Feedback Sensor]
    FB --> SENS
    MOT -.->|Encoders for servo feedback| SENS
```
