# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a brushed DC motor with a neat diagram. State the role of the commutator.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
A brushed DC motor consists of a **stator** (permanent magnets or field windings) and a **rotor/armature** (current-carrying coil) connected to a **split-ring commutator** and **brushes**.

**Working:**
1. When DC supply is given, current flows through the armature coil via brushes.
2. According to **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, the current-carrying conductor in the magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL$, producing a torque on the coil.
3. The coil rotates, and as it passes the vertical position, the **commutator reverses the current direction**.
4. This reversal ensures that torque remains unidirectional throughout the rotation.

**Role of Commutator (1 mark):** It acts as a mechanical switch that reverses the armature current every half cycle, converting the alternating EMF induced in the coil into unidirectional (DC) output torque. Without it, the motor would not rotate continuously — it would just oscillate.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a stepper motor? Why is it called a "stepper" motor?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
A **stepper motor** is a brushless DC motor that divides a full rotation into a number of equal steps. The motor's position can be controlled precisely without any feedback sensor (**open-loop control**) by applying a sequence of digital pulses to its stator windings.

**Why "stepper"? (1 mark):** It is called a "stepper" because the rotor moves in **discrete angular increments (steps)** — e.g., 1.8° per step — rather than rotating continuously. Each input pulse advances the rotor by one step, hence the name.

**Step angle formula:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$

**Applications:** 3D printers, CNC machines, robotics, camera lens focusing.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expressions for **back EMF** and **torque**. Show that $K_e = K_t$ in SI units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.5\,\Omega$ and a field winding resistance of $220\,\Omega$. When running at no load, it draws 5 A from the supply and runs at 1500 RPM. Calculate:
   (i) The back EMF at no load.
   (ii) The no-load speed if armature reaction is neglected.
   (iii) The armature current and torque when the motor draws 30 A total current. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) PMDC Motor — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Permanent magnets (Alnico / Ferrite) producing radial flux $\Phi$.
- **Rotor:** Laminated silicon steel armature with slots carrying the winding.
- **Commutator:** Split copper ring segments connected to the armature coil ends.
- **Brushes:** Carbon/graphite contacts that slide on the commutator.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Current enters via positive brush → commutator segment → armature coil → negative brush.
- Lorentz force $F = BIL$ acts tangentially on conductors, producing torque $T = F \times r$.
- After 180° rotation, the commutator reverses current direction, ensuring continuous unidirectional rotation.
- The rotating coil also generates a back EMF $E_b$ opposing the supply voltage (Lenz's Law).

**Derivation of Back EMF (2 marks):**
$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = K_e \omega$$
where $K_e = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$.

**Derivation of Torque (1 mark):**
$$T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = K_t I_a$$
where $K_t = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A}$.

Since $K_e$ and $K_t$ have the same algebraic form and consistent SI units (V·s/rad ≡ N·m/A), we conclude:
$$\boxed{K_e = K_t \quad \text{in SI units}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V = 220\,\text{V}$
- $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 220\,\Omega$
- $I_L = 5\,\text{A}$ (no load), $N_0 = 1500\,\text{RPM}$

**Step 1 — Find shunt field current at no load:**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Find armature current at no load:**
$$I_{a0} = I_L - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4\,\text{A}$$

**(i) Back EMF at no load (2 marks):**
$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (4)(0.5) = 220 - 2 = 218\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) No-load speed (1 mark):**
Since armature reaction is neglected, $\Phi$ is constant, so $E_b \propto N$.
$$N_0 = 1500\,\text{RPM}$$
$$E_{b0} = 218\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — At full load, total current $I_L = 30\,\text{A}$:**
$$I_{sh} = 1\,\text{A} \text{ (unchanged, since V and } R_{sh} \text{ constant)}$$
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Armature current at load: $I_a = 29\,\text{A}$ (1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Back EMF at full load (1 mark):**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (29)(0.5) = 220 - 14.5 = 205.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Full load speed:**
$$\frac{N}{N_0} = \frac{E_b}{E_{b0}}$$
$$N = 1500 \times \frac{205.5}{218} = 1500 \times 0.9427 = 1414\,\text{RPM}$$

**Step 6 — Torque at full load (2 marks):**
Power developed in armature:
$$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 205.5 \times 29 = 5959.5\,\text{W}$$

Torque:
$$T = \frac{P_{dev}}{\omega} = \frac{5959.5}{2\pi \times 1414/60} = \frac{5959.5}{148.1} \approx 40.24\,\text{N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $E_{b0} = 218\,\text{V}$
- (ii) $N_0 = 1500\,\text{RPM}$
- (iii) $I_a = 29\,\text{A}$, $T \approx 40.24\,\text{N·m}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of an RC servo motor**. How is the shaft angle controlled using PWM? List two applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **H-Bridge circuit** for DC motor direction control with a clear truth table. Why is it called an "H-bridge"? What is **shoot-through fault** and how is it prevented? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) RC Servo Motor (7 marks)**

**Construction / Block Diagram (2 marks):**
An RC servo motor consists of:
1. **Small DC motor** — provides the rotational power.
2. **Gear train** — reduces speed and increases torque.
3. **Potentiometer** — mounted on the output shaft to sense position.
4. **Control electronics** — comparator + driver circuit.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The microcontroller sends a **PWM control pulse** every **20 ms (50 Hz)**.
- The pulse width (typically **1 ms to 2 ms**) encodes the desired shaft angle:
  - **1.0 ms pulse → 0°** (one extreme position)
  - **1.5 ms pulse → 90°** (center position)
  - **2.0 ms pulse → 180°** (other extreme position)
- The internal **comparator** compares the setpoint (from PWM) with the actual position (from potentiometer).
- An **error signal** drives the motor until the error → 0.
- Once the desired angle is reached, the motor stops but maintains **holding torque** to resist disturbance.

**Position formula:**
$$\theta = \frac{(t_{pulse} - 1\,\text{ms})}{1\,\text{ms}} \times 90°$$

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. Robotic arms and grippers.
2. RC vehicles (steering).
3. Camera gimbals / drone flight controllers.
4. CNC tool changers.

---

**(b) H-Bridge Circuit (7 marks)**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
An H-Bridge consists of **four switches** (transistors / MOSFETs / relays) — typically labeled **S1, S2 (high side)** and **S3, S4 (low side)** — connected around the DC motor in an "H" shape. Each arm of the H contains one switch.

**Why "H-bridge" name? (1 mark):**
The four switches form the vertical arms of the letter "H", while the motor forms the horizontal bar across the middle. Hence the name.

**Switching Truth Table (2 marks):**

| S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Forward |
| OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Reverse |
| ON | OFF | ON | OFF | Dynamic Brake |
| OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Coast (free-run) |

**Shoot-Through Fault (1 mark):**
If both high-side (S1 & S2) or both low-side (S3 & S4) switches are turned ON **simultaneously**, it creates a direct short circuit across the supply rail. This is called **shoot-through**, which can draw enormous current and instantly destroy the switches and power supply.

**Prevention:**
- Use a **dead-time** delay between switching one pair OFF and the complementary pair ON.
- Use **gate driver ICs** with built-in shoot-through protection (e.g., IR2104, DRV8701).
- Use **logic gates** to enforce mutually exclusive inputs.

**Why use H-bridge? (1 mark):**
- Bidirectional control of DC motor (forward + reverse).
- Combined with PWM on enable pins, allows **variable speed + variable direction** control.
- Essential for differential drive robots and any application requiring direction reversal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Back EMF with Supply Voltage** — Students often write $V = I_a R_a$ without including $E_b$. Always start with the KVL equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Stepper Types** — Don't say "stepper motor has permanent magnet rotor" generically. Specify the type: PM, VR, or Hybrid.
>
> 3. **H-Bridge Shoot-Through** — Many students forget to mention the dead-time requirement. This is a 1-mark loss in 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Servo Pulse Width Range** — Always state **1 ms to 2 ms**, not just "PWM". Examiners are strict on the 20 ms period.
>
> 5. **Units in Motor Constants** — $K_e$ should have units of **V·s/rad** and $K_t$ in **N·m/A**, not just numerical values.
>
> 6. **Step Angle Derivation** — Don't just state the formula; mention that it comes from the geometry of stator/rotor tooth alignment and number of phases.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔹 DC Motor Essentials
- [ ] **Working principle:** Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field.
- [ ] **Role of commutator:** Mechanical rectifier → reverses current every 180° for unidirectional torque.
- [ ] **Back EMF formula:** $E_b = K_e \omega = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$
- [ ] **Armature KVL:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
- [ ] **Torque formula:** $T = K_t I_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$
- [ ] **$K_e = K_t$** in SI units (V·s/rad = N·m/A).
- [ ] **Speed-torque characteristic** is linear with negative slope.

### 🔹 Stepper Motor Essentials
- [ ] **Open-loop position control** — no feedback sensor needed.
- [ ] **Three types:** Permanent Magnet (PM), Variable Reluctance (VR), Hybrid.
- [ ] **Step angle:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$
- [ ] **Common values:** 1.8°/step (200 steps/rev), 0.9°/step (400 steps/rev).
- [ ] **Driving modes:** Full-step, half-step, microstepping.
- [ ] **Holding torque** maintains position at standstill — key advantage.

### 🔹 Servo Motor Essentials
- [ ] **Closed-loop** — internal potentiometer provides feedback.
- [ ] **PWM control:** pulse every **20 ms**, width **1 ms – 2 ms** for **0° – 180°**.
- [ ] **Formula:** $\theta = \dfrac{(t_{pulse} - 1)}{1} \times 90°$
- [ ] **Holding torque** even when stationary.
- [ ] Limited rotation angle (typically 180° or 270°) — NOT continuous rotation (unless modified).

### 🔹 PWM Control Essentials
- [ ] **Duty cycle:** $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$
- [ ] **Average voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$
- [ ] Higher frequency → smoother motor operation (typical >1 kHz).
- [ ] PWM frequency for servo is **fixed at 50 Hz**.

### 🔹 H-Bridge Essentials
- [ ] **Four switches** arranged in H-shape around motor.
- [ ] Enables **bidirectional** control.
- [ ] **Shoot-through fault** = same-side switches ON simultaneously → short circuit → damage.
- [ ] Use **dead-time** + gate driver ICs for protection.

### 🔹 Motor Driver ICs Essentials
- [ ] **L293D:** 600 mA, 2 channels (dual H-bridge).
- [ ] **L298N:** 2 A, 2 channels.
- [ ] **DRV8871:** 3.6 A, single channel.
- [ ] **A4988 / DRV8825:** Stepper motor drivers with microstepping.
- [ ] Always check **continuous current rating** vs **peak current** rating.

### 🔹 Key Numerical Relationships
- [ ] $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ (rad/s to RPM conversion)
- [ ] $N = \dfrac{60 f}{P}$ (synchronous speed for AC, not DC — useful sanity check)
- [ ] Power developed $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ (electromagnetic power)
- [ ] Output power $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems in Mechatronic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems in Mechatronic Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Mechatronic system design, the **Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems** refers to the systematic engineering methodology used to identify the optimal energy conversion device (actuator) and mechanical power transmission element (drive) that satisfies a given set of design specifications, including load, speed, precision, duty cycle, environmental conditions, and economic constraints.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

- **Actuator**: A device that converts a control signal (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or thermal energy) into a physical motion (rotational or translational) to perform mechanical work on a load.
- **Drive System (Power Transmission System)**: A mechanical linkage or coupling mechanism (gears, belts, lead screws, cams, rack-pinion, chains) that transmits motion and power from the actuator to the load, often modifying speed, torque, or motion type in the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of *OEAET831 – Introduction to Mechatronics* emphasizes that actuator and drive selection is NOT a standalone decision. It is coupled with the **sensor selection**, **controller design**, and **mechanical structure**. The correct approach is **systematic, performance-driven, and multi-objective**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **robotic arm in a car assembly line** that must lift a heavy door panel (load = **15 kg**), move it slowly and precisely into position (speed = **0.1 m/s**, accuracy = **±0.5 mm**), and operate safely near flammable paint fumes.

If you chose:
- A **pneumatic cylinder** → cheap and fast, but lacks precision → **REJECTED**
- A **hydraulic actuator** → strong enough, but oil leaks near paint = fire hazard → **REJECTED**
- A **stepper motor with a harmonic drive gear** → precise, clean, no leakage → **ACCEPTED**

> This is the heart of selection criteria: matching the **actuator's intrinsic physical behavior** with the **application's demands**, mediated by a **drive system** that refines speed/torque to match the load.

---

## 1.3 Why Selection Criteria Matter

| Aspect | Consequence of Poor Selection |
|---|---|
| **Torque/Force Mismatch** | Stalling, overheating, system failure |
| **Speed Mismatch** | Production bottleneck or inertial overshoot |
| **Precision Mismatch** | Scrap, quality defects |
| **Environment Mismatch** | Corrosion, leakage, explosion |
| **Cost Mismatch** | Non-competitive product |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Mechatronics Metric:** The ratio of useful mechanical power output to electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic power input is called **actuator efficiency** (η). Typical ranges:
> - **Electrical actuators (DC motors)** : 60% – 90%
> - **Hydraulic actuators** : 70% – 85%
> - **Pneumatic actuators** : 30% – 50% (due to air compression losses)

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview

### A. Actuators (by energy domain)

1. **Electrical Actuators**: DC motors, Stepper motors, Servo motors, Solenoids, Piezoelectric actuators
2. **Hydraulic Actuators**: Cylinders, Rotary hydraulic motors
3. **Pneumatic Actuators**: Cylinders, Rotary pneumatic motors, Air muscles
4. **Non-conventional**: Shape memory alloys (SMA), Magnetostrictive, Electroactive polymers (EAP)

### B. Drive Systems (Power Transmission)

1. **Rotational Drives**: Spur/Bevel/Helical/Worm gears, Belt-pulley, Chain drives, Harmonic drives
2. **Translational Drives**: Lead screws, Ball screws, Rack and pinion, Belt-driven linear actuators
3. **Special Motion**: Cam-follower, Geneva mechanism, Four-bar linkages, Rack-clutch mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic directly supports **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Select appropriate sensors, actuators, and drive systems for mechatronic applications."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Selection Methodology (Step-by-Step Logic)

The professional selection of an actuator-drive pair follows a **top-down hierarchical funnel**:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. DEFINE LOAD & MISSION PROFILE       │
│    (Force/Torque, Speed, Stroke, Duty) │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 2. DECIDE ENERGY DOMAIN                │
│    (Electrical / Hydraulic / Pneumatic)│
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 3. PICK ACTUATOR FAMILY & QUANTITY     │
│    (Motor type, Cylinder bore, etc.)   │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 4. CHOOSE DRIVE / TRANSMISSION        │
│    (Gear ratio, Screw pitch, etc.)     │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  ↓
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 5. VALIDATE VIA DYNAMICS & THERMAL     │
│    (Torque-speed curve, Heat sink)     │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 2.2 The Eight Critical Selection Criteria

### Criterion 1: Load Requirements (Force / Torque)
- **Translational:** $F_{load}$ (N)
- **Rotational:** $T_{load}$ (N·m)

The actuator must satisfy:

$$
F_{actuator} \geq F_{load} \cdot S_{factor}
$$

where the **Service Factor** $S_{factor} = 1.2 \text{ to } 2.0$ accounts for friction, inertia, shock, and safety margin.

> The **"Why"**: Real systems experience transient peaks (acceleration, deceleration) that exceed steady-state loads. Selecting too close to the limit causes fatigue failure.

---

### Criterion 2: Speed (Velocity / RPM)
- Rotational: $N$ (rpm) or $\omega$ (rad/s)
- Translational: $v$ (m/s)

Conversion:

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

> The **"How"**: Speed determines whether a direct-drive motor or a geared system is needed. High speeds typically mean small motors with high gear ratios.

---

### Criterion 3: Power and Energy
The required **mechanical power** at the load:

$$
P_{load} = T_{load} \cdot \omega \quad [\text{W}]
$$

Or for linear motion:

$$
P_{load} = F_{load} \cdot v \quad [\text{W}]
$$

Required **input power** at the actuator:

$$
P_{input} = \frac{P_{load}}{\eta_{actuator} \cdot \eta_{drive}}
$$

> **Real-world utility:** In battery-powered mobile robots (e.g., warehouse AGVs), every Watt-hour of extra actuator inefficiency reduces mission duration by minutes. Hence the drive's efficiency (often **90–95%** for harmonic gears, but only **60–70%** for worm gears) is critical.

---

### Criterion 4: Accuracy and Resolution
- **Resolution:** Smallest incremental motion achievable.
- **Repeatability:** Ability to return to a position consistently.
- **Accuracy:** Difference between commanded and actual position.

For a stepper motor with **microstepping**:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p}{N_{microsteps} \cdot N_{gear}}
$$

where $p$ = lead screw pitch, $N_{microsteps}$ = microsteps per full step, $N_{gear}$ = gear ratio.

---

### Criterion 5: Stroke Length / Angular Range
- Linear actuators: stroke $L$ (mm)
- Rotary actuators: angular travel $\theta_{max}$ (rad or deg)

> Must be selected such that **actuator stroke ≥ required stroke + safety margin**.

---

### Criterion 6: Duty Cycle and Dynamic Response
Defined by:

$$
DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%
$$

> **Why it matters:** Continuous-duty applications (e.g., CNC spindle) require rated continuous torque, while intermittent-duty applications (e.g., pick-and-place) can use peak torque briefly.

Dynamic response — system bandwidth:

$$
f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{K}{J_{eq}}}
$$

where $K$ = torsional stiffness (N·m/rad), $J_{eq}$ = equivalent inertia reflected at the motor shaft.

---

### Criterion 7: Environmental and Operating Conditions

| Condition | Recommended Actuator |
|---|---|
| **Clean room, low noise** | Piezoelectric, Stepper + Harmonic drive |
| **Explosive atmosphere (ATEX)** | Pneumatic (intrinsically safe) |
| **Wet / Marine / Corrosive** | Stainless pneumatic, Sealed servo |
| **High force in compact size** | Hydraulic |
| **Temperature > 200 °C** | Pneumatic / Hydraulic (away from electronics) |

---

### Criterion 8: Cost, Weight, Maintenance, and Availability

$$
C_{total} = C_{acquisition} + C_{installation} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_{maintenance,i}}{(1+r)^i}
$$

> This is the **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** — preferred over initial price alone.

---

## 2.3 Drive System Selection Criteria

| Drive Type | Speed Reduction | Torque Multiplication | Backlash | Efficiency | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spur Gear** | $N_1/N_2$ | $N_2/N_1$ | Low–Medium | **98%** | Parallel shafts, low noise |
| **Worm Gear** | High in single stage | High | Low (self-locking) | **40–70%** | Right-angle drive, holding load |
| **Harmonic Drive** | High (50:1 to 100:1) | High | **Near zero** | **80–90%** | Robotics (precision) |
| **Belt-Pulley** | Variable | Variable | Low (slip possible) | **95%** | Long-distance, light load |
| **Lead Screw** | $v = N \cdot p$ | $T = \frac{F \cdot p}{2 \pi \eta}$ | Medium | **30–70%** | Slow linear motion |
| **Ball Screw** | Same as lead screw | $T = \frac{F \cdot p}{2 \pi \eta}$ | Very low | **90–95%** | CNC, high precision linear |
| **Rack & Pinion** | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | $F = T/r$ | Medium | **85%** | Long strokes |
| **Cam-Follower** | Programmed | Programmed | Zero (contact) | **60%** | Specific motion profiles |

> **Critical Decision Rule:**
> If precision > 5 µm → **Ball screw + Servo + Encoder**
> If precision ~50 µm → **Lead screw + Stepper**
> If force > 10 kN → **Hydraulic cylinder + valve**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = T \omega$ | Rotational power | W |
| 2 | $P = F v$ | Linear power | W |
| 3 | $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 4 | $v = N \cdot p$ | Lead screw linear speed | m/s |
| 5 | $T_{screw} = \frac{F p}{2 \pi \eta}$ | Lead/ball screw torque | N·m |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{drive}$ | Combined efficiency | – |
| 7 | $S_{factor} = F_{act}/F_{load}$ | Safety/service factor | – |
| 8 | $J_{eq} = J_{m} + J_{L}/N^2$ | Reflected inertia | kg·m² |
| 9 | $f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/J_{eq}}$ | Mechanical bandwidth | Hz |
| 10 | $DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Duty cycle | % |

> **Note:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` when writing absolute value inside tables to avoid breaking markdown.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robots:** Typically use **AC servo motors + Harmonic drives (RV reducers)** for the wrist joints and **planetary gearheads** for the base.
- **CNC Machines:** Use **ball screws + linear encoders + servo motors** for sub-micron positioning.
- **Aircraft Flight Control (Fly-by-Wire):** Use **electrohydraulic actuators (EHA)** for high force-to-weight ratio.
- **Soft Robotics (Medical):** Use **pneumatic McKibben muscles** for compliance.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** Use **voice-coil actuator + flexure bearing** for nanometer precision.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reflected Inertia Through a Gear Train

When a load inertia $J_L$ is connected to a motor through a gear train with ratio $N = N_{in}/N_{out}$, the equivalent inertia seen at the motor shaft is derived from **energy conservation**.

### Step 1: Express kinetic energy at the load
The kinetic energy stored in a rotating load at angular velocity $\omega_L$:

$$
KE_{load} = \frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2
$$

### Step 2: Relate load velocity to motor velocity
Because gears preserve the **pitch-line velocity**:

$$
\omega_m \cdot r_m = \omega_L \cdot r_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_L = \frac{N_m}{N_L} \omega_m
$$

Defining gear ratio $N_g = N_L / N_m$:

$$
\omega_L = \frac{\omega_m}{N_g}
$$

### Step 3: Equate kinetic energies
The kinetic energy of an equivalent inertia $J_{eq}$ at the motor shaft:

$$
KE_{eq} = \frac{1}{2} J_{eq} \omega_m^2
$$

Setting $KE_{eq} = KE_{load}$:

$$
\frac{1}{2} J_{eq} \omega_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_L \left(\frac{\omega_m}{N_g}\right)^2
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $J_{eq}$
Cancel $\frac{1}{2}\omega_m^2$ from both sides:

$$
J_{eq} = \frac{J_L}{N_g^2}
$$

### Step 5: Add motor inertia
The total inertia at motor shaft:

$$
\boxed{J_{total} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N_g^2}}
$$

> **Why this matters in selection:** Larger gear ratio $N_g$ makes the load "appear lighter" to the motor — improving acceleration capability. However, friction in the gearbox multiplies back as $T_{fric} \cdot N_g$ at the motor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lead Screw / Ball Screw Drive Torque

For a linear load $F$ moved by a rotating screw of pitch $p$:

### Step 1: Mechanical work done per revolution
Linear distance per revolution $= p$. Work done by load:

$$
W = F \cdot p
$$

### Step 2: Work done at the screw shaft
A torque $T$ applied through one full revolution ($2\pi$ rad) does work:

$$
W = T \cdot 2\pi
$$

### Step 3: Account for efficiency
With efficiency $\eta$, the input torque must overcome both load and losses:

$$
T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \eta = F \cdot p
$$

### Step 4: Solve for torque

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{F \cdot p}{2 \pi \cdot \eta}}
$$

For a ball screw (η ≈ 0.9) versus a lead screw (η ≈ 0.4), the required torque differs by **more than 2×** for the same load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Selection of DC Motor for a Conveyor Belt

**Problem statement:** A conveyor must move a $25\,\text{kg}$ load at $0.5\,\text{m/s}$ against friction ($\mu = 0.3$). The drive uses a pulley of diameter $D = 0.2\,\text{m}$. Gear ratio $N_g = 10$, motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.85$, drive efficiency $\eta_d = 0.9$, service factor $S_f = 1.5$. Find the required motor torque and power.

### Step 1: Compute linear force on the belt
$$
F = \mu \cdot m \cdot g = 0.3 \times 25 \times 9.81 = 73.575\,\text{N}
$$

### Step 2: Apply safety factor
$$
F_{design} = F \cdot S_f = 73.575 \times 1.5 = 110.36\,\text{N}
$$

### Step 3: Compute linear power at load
$$
P_{load} = F_{design} \cdot v = 110.36 \times 0.5 = 55.18\,\text{W}
$$

### Step 4: Compute input power at motor (accounting for losses)
$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_d = 0.85 \times 0.9 = 0.765
$$

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{load}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{55.18}{0.765} \approx 72.13\,\text{W}
$$

### Step 5: Compute required motor torque
Pulley radius: $r = D/2 = 0.1\,\text{m}$. Load-side torque:

$$
T_{load} = F_{design} \cdot r = 110.36 \times 0.1 = 11.036\,\text{N·m}
$$

Reflected to motor through $N_g = 10$:

$$
T_{motor,ideal} = \frac{T_{load}}{N_g} = 1.1036\,\text{N·m}
$$

Including drive losses:

$$
T_{motor} = \frac{T_{load}}{N_g \cdot \eta_d} = \frac{11.036}{10 \times 0.9} \approx 1.226\,\text{N·m}
$$

### Step 6: Verify against speed
Belt speed $v = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$ → pulley $\omega = v/r = 5\,\text{rad/s}$ → motor speed $= N_g \cdot \omega = 50\,\text{rad/s} \approx 477\,\text{rpm}$.

$$
P_{check} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_m = 1.226 \times 50 = 61.3\,\text{W}
$$

The 13% difference from Step 4 reflects friction/loss modeling differences; designers typically use the **larger of the two values** as the safe motor rating → select a **≥ 80 W DC motor**.

> **Result Summary:**
> - **Required Motor Torque:** $T_m \approx 1.23\,\text{N·m}$
> - **Required Motor Speed:** $N_m \approx 477\,\text{rpm}$
> - **Required Motor Power:** $P_m \geq 72\,\text{W}$ → **Standard off-the-shelf choice: 80 W DC gearmotor**

---

## 3.4 Selection Decision Matrix (Multi-Criteria Scoring)

| Criterion | Weight | Stepper | Servo | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Precision | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 2 |
| Speed | 0.15 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| Force/Torque density | 0.20 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Cleanliness | 0.10 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 1 |
| Cost | 0.15 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| Maintenance | 0.10 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 1 |
| Control simplicity | 0.10 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| **Weighted Score** | 1.00 | **4.25** | **4.15** | **3.40** | **2.65** |

**Conclusion:** **Stepper motor is the best overall choice** in this scenario — a classic KTU-style decision-making exercise.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechatronic System-Level Block Diagram

The placement of actuators and drives within the broader mechatronic system:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[Power Supply] --> actuatorA[Actuator]
    ctrlA[Controller PLC Microcontroller] --> driveA[Drive Power Amplifier]
    driveA --> actuatorA
    actuatorA --> driveSys[Drive System Gear Belt Screw]
    driveSys --> loadA[Mechanical Load]
    loadA --> sensorA[Sensor Encoder LVDT]
    sensorA --> ctrlA
    envA[Environment] -.-> actuatorA
    envA -.-> sensorA

    classDef power fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#386641,color:#000
    classDef motion fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#386641,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#bee9e8,stroke:#1d7874,color:#000
    classDef env fill:#f1c0e8,stroke:#7d4f9b,color:#000

    class sourceA,actuatorA power
    class ctrlA,driveA ctrl
    class driveSys,loadA motion
    class sensorA sense
    class envA env
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = power/signal flow direction.
> - **Dotted arrow from Environment** = disturbances (temperature, dust, vibration).
> - **Feedback loop**: Sensor → Controller → Drive → Actuator is the **closed-loop mechatronic chain**.

---

## 4.2 Actuator Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Define Load Requirements] --> q1{Need linear or rotary motion}
    q1 -->|Linear| q2L{Stroke length mm}
    q1 -->|Rotary| q2R{Required torque N m}

    q2L -->|lt 50| piezoA[Piezoelectric Stack]
    q2L -->|50 to 500| screwA[Solenoid or Lead Screw + Stepper]
    q2L -->|gt 500| hydpA[Hydraulic Cylinder or Long Pneumatic]

    q2R -->|lt 0.1| motA[Small DC Servo Direct Drive]
    q2R -->|0.1 to 10| gearA[Servo + Planetary Gear]
    q2R -->|10 to 100| hydmA[Hydraulic Motor or Servo + Harmonic]
    q2R -->|gt 100| bigA[Industrial Servo + Heavy Gearbox]

    piezoA --> envQ{Environment constraint}
    screwA --> envQ
    hydpA --> envQ
    motA --> envQ
    gearA --> envQ
    hydmA --> envQ
    bigA --> envQ

    envQ -->|Clean dry| elecA[Electrical Solution]
    envQ -->|Wet explosive| pneuA[Pneumatic Solution]
    envQ -->|Heavy load slow| hydrA[Hydraulic Solution]

    elecA --> endA[Select specific model and validate]
    pneuA --> endA
    hydrA --> endA

    classDef decision fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef choice fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1d4e89,color:#000
    classDef start fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000

    class startA,envQ,endA start
    class q1,q2L,q2R decision
    class piezoA,screwA,hydpA,motA,gearA,hydmA,bigA,elecA,pneuA,hydrA choice
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Drive System Mapping

For a **linear positioning stage** (a KTU-favorite problem), the functional architecture mapping is:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA[Stepper Motor Pulse Generator]
    stepA --> stepB[Stepper Driver Microstepping]
    stepB --> stepC[Motor Shaft Coupling]
    stepC --> stepD[Gear Reducer Ratio N]
    stepD --> stepE[Drive Pulley or Pinion]
    stepE --> stepF[Transmission Belt or Rack]
    stepF --> stepG[Linear Carriage or Table]
    stepG --> stepH[Load Workpiece]
    stepH --> fb1[Linear Encoder Position Feedback]
    fb1 --> fb2[Controller Closed Loop]
    fb2 --> stepB

    classDef motor fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#bc4749,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#bc6c25,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#caffbf,stroke:#386641,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#04619f,color:#000

    class stepA,stepB stepA
    class stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG motor
    class stepH trans
    class fb1,fb2 load
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Actuator Energy Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eA[Electrical Energy] --> eM[DC Motor Stepper Servo Solenoid Piezo]
    hA[Hydraulic Energy] --> hM[Cylinder Rotary Motor]
    pA[Pneumatic Energy] --> pM[Cylinder Air Muscle Rotary Vane]

    eM --> oA[Motion Output Linear or Rotary]
    hM --> oA
    pM --> oA

    oA --> dr[Drive System Gear Screw Belt Rack]
    dr --> ld[Final Load]

    classDef eClass fill:#e0aaff,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000
    classDef hClass fill:#a2d2ff,stroke:#023e8a,color:#000
    classDef pClass fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#023e8a,color:#000
    classDef outClass fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#bc4749,color:#000
    classDef drClass fill:#caffbf,stroke:#386641,color:#000

    class eA,eM eClass
    class hA,hM hClass
    class pA,pM pClass
    class oA,ld outClass
    class dr drClass
```

> **Visualization aid:** This diagram helps students remember the **three energy domains** that converge into a common **motion output**, then pass through a drive to the load — the universal architecture of any actuated mechatronic system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE important criteria for the selection of an actuator in a mechatronic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Load Requirement (Torque/Force):** The actuator must produce a force or torque greater than the load demand multiplied by a safety factor ($S_f = 1.2$ to $2.0$). Under-specification leads to stalling or overheating.

2. **Speed and Dynamic Response:** The actuator's rated speed (RPM or m/s) and bandwidth must match the application's cycle time. Stepper motors suit low-speed, while servo motors handle high-bandwidth tasks.

3. **Environmental Suitability:** The actuator must withstand the operating environment. For example, pneumatic actuators are preferred in explosive atmospheres, while electrical actuators suit clean rooms.

> *Awarding scheme: 1 mark per correctly stated criterion with brief justification.*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a lead screw and a ball screw in terms of working principle and selection preference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Lead Screw:** Uses sliding friction between the nut and screw threads. Efficiency is low (30–50%). Preferred when cost is a priority, load is light, and precision needs are moderate.
- **Ball Screw:** Uses recirculating ball bearings between nut and screw, converting sliding friction to rolling friction. Efficiency is high (90–95%). Preferred for high-speed, high-precision, high-duty-cycle applications like CNC machines and robotics.

| Aspect | Lead Screw | Ball Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Friction type | Sliding | Rolling |
| Efficiency | 30–50% | 90–95% |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Life | Limited | Long |

> *Awarding scheme: 1 mark working principle difference, 1 mark efficiency/numerical, 1 mark selection preference.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the role of actuators and drive systems in a typical mechatronic system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pick-and-place mechanism is required to lift a 1.5 kg component, move it 200 mm horizontally in 1.5 s, and place it. The horizontal motion is achieved through a rack-and-pinion driven by a DC motor via a 5:1 gear reducer. Pinion diameter = 30 mm, motor efficiency = 0.85, gear efficiency = 0.9, coefficient of friction = 0.2 between rack and load, and a service factor of 1.5 must be applied. Determine:
   1. The required linear velocity and acceleration.
   2. The horizontal force required.
   3. The required motor torque and power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A mechatronic system consists of a **central controller (microcontroller/PLC)**, **sensors** for feedback, **actuators** that convert controller signals into motion, and **drive systems** that transmit/modify this motion to the load.

```
[Controller] → [Drive/Power Amp] → [Actuator] → [Drive System] → [Load]
       ↑                                                                  |
       └──────────── [Sensor] ←──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Actuator's role:** Convert electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic energy into mechanical motion (rotary or linear) with controlled force/torque.
- **Drive system's role:** Modify the motion (speed reduction, torque amplification, conversion between linear and rotary) to suit the load.
- **Examples:** DC motor + worm gear for a conveyor; Stepper motor + lead screw for a linear stage; Servo motor + harmonic drive for a robot joint.

> *Awarding scheme: Diagram 3 marks, explanation of actuator role 2 marks, drive system role 2 marks.*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 1.5\,\text{kg}$
- Distance $s = 0.2\,\text{m}$, time $t = 1.5\,\text{s}$
- Gear ratio $N_g = 5$
- Pinion diameter $D = 0.03\,\text{m}$ → radius $r = 0.015\,\text{m}$
- $\eta_m = 0.85$, $\eta_g = 0.9$, $\mu = 0.2$, $S_f = 1.5$

**Step 1: Velocity & acceleration (1.5 marks)**

Assuming constant acceleration (trapezoidal profile):

$$
v = \frac{s}{t} = \frac{0.2}{1.5} \approx 0.133\,\text{m/s}
$$

Acceleration time $\approx t/2 = 0.75\,\text{s}$:

$$
a = \frac{v}{0.75} \approx 0.178\,\text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2: Horizontal force (2 marks)**

Friction force:

$$
F_{fric} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g = 0.2 \times 1.5 \times 9.81 = 2.943\,\text{N}
$$

Inertial force:

$$
F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 1.5 \times 0.178 = 0.267\,\text{N}
$$

Total load force:

$$
F_{load} = F_{fric} + F_{inertia} = 2.943 + 0.267 = 3.21\,\text{N}
$$

With safety factor:

$$
F_{design} = F_{load} \cdot S_f = 3.21 \times 1.5 = 4.815\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3: Motor torque & power (3.5 marks)**

Load-side torque at pinion:

$$
T_{load} = F_{design} \cdot r = 4.815 \times 0.015 = 0.0722\,\text{N·m}
$$

Reflected at motor (ideal):

$$
T_{motor,ideal} = \frac{T_{load}}{N_g} = \frac{0.0722}{5} = 0.01444\,\text{N·m}
$$

Including gear losses:

$$
T_{motor} = \frac{T_{load}}{N_g \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{0.0722}{5 \times 0.9} \approx 0.01604\,\text{N·m}
$$

Motor speed: pinion $\omega = v/r = 0.133/0.015 \approx 8.87\,\text{rad/s}$. Motor $\omega_m = N_g \cdot \omega = 5 \times 8.87 = 44.36\,\text{rad/s}$ → $N_m \approx 423\,\text{rpm}$.

Required motor mechanical power:

$$
P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_m = 0.01604 \times 44.36 \approx 0.711\,\text{W}
$$

Including motor electrical efficiency:

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta_m} = \frac{0.711}{0.85} \approx 0.836\,\text{W}
$$

**Result:** A standard **1–2 W DC gearmotor** with 5:1 reduction is suitable.

> *Awarding scheme: 1.5 marks velocity/acceleration, 2 marks force, 3.5 marks torque and power derivation.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Compare the suitability of electrical, pneumatic, and hydraulic actuators for the following mechatronic applications:
  1. Pick-and-place robotic arm in a clean room.
  2. Heavy-duty earth-moving excavator.
  3. Underground coal mine conveyor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with sketches the working of a **Harmonic Drive** system. Justify why it is preferred over a conventional spur gear train in robotic wrist joints. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Application | Best Actuator | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **(1) Clean-room robotic arm** | **Electrical (Servo + Harmonic)** | No oil contamination; high precision; clean operation; easily sealed for ISO class 3–5. |
| **(2) Earth-moving excavator** | **Hydraulic** | Highest force/torque density; ability to handle multi-tonne loads; robust against dust and shock. |
| **(3) Underground coal mine** | **Pneumatic or Hydraulic (intrinsically safe)** | No electrical sparking → explosion-safe. Pneumatic preferred for simplicity; hydraulic for high loads. |

> *Awarding scheme: 2 marks per application (1 mark for choice, 1 mark for justification).*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A **Harmonic Drive** (also called strain-wave gear) consists of three components:

1. **Wave Generator (WG):** Elliptical input cam with a thin ball bearing on its outer rim.
2. **Flexspline (FS):** Thin, flexible cup-shaped gear with external teeth.
3. **Circular Spline (CS):** Rigid ring with internal teeth (typically 2 more teeth than FS).

**Working:**
- The Wave Generator is connected to the input shaft (motor).
- As the WG rotates, the FS flexes into an elliptical shape.
- The external teeth of the FS engage with the internal teeth of the CS at the two contact zones on the ellipse's major axis.
- For every rotation of the WG, the FS "rotates" backward by 2 teeth relative to the CS.
- If the FS is fixed (input) and CS is output, gear ratio is **100:1** or more in a single stage.

**Sketch (functional representation):**

```
       [Wave Generator (elliptical)]
            ↓ flexes
   [Flexible Spline FS (external teeth)]
            ↓ engages
   [Circular Spline CS (internal teeth, fixed/output)]
```

**Why preferred in robotic wrist joints:**

| Criterion | Harmonic Drive | Spur Gear Train |
|---|---|---|
| Gear ratio (single stage) | 50:1 to 100:1 | Up to 10:1 (needs multiple stages) |
| Backlash | **Near zero (< 1 arc-min)** | Noticeable (10–30 arc-min) |
| Weight & Size | Compact, lightweight | Bulky for high ratios |
| Efficiency | 80–90% | ~98% per stage |
| Torque capacity | Moderate to high | High |

> *Awarding scheme: 2 marks components, 2 marks working principle, 1 mark sketch, 2 marks comparison/justification.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Service Factor (S_f):** A very common error. Students compute force without multiplying by 1.5, leading to a 33% under-specification — instant deduction of **1 mark** in problems.
> 2. **Reflecting inertia, but NOT friction:** When computing motor torque through a gear train, students often divide the load torque by $N_g$ but forget to divide the **gear friction torque** by $N_g$ as well (it actually increases at the motor side).
> 3. **Mixing up `N = N_in/N_out` vs `N_out/N_in`:** Always clearly state what the gear ratio represents. KTU examiners explicitly check this.
> 4. **Using `ω = 2πN/60` with N in rps instead of rpm:** This leads to a factor of 60 error — costly.
> 5. **Ignoring drive efficiency:** A worm gear with 50% efficiency means the motor must deliver **double** the load torque. This is a frequent oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Selection is a hierarchical funnel:** Define load → Choose energy domain → Pick actuator → Pick drive → Validate.
- [x] **Eight critical selection criteria:** Load, Speed, Power, Accuracy, Stroke, Duty cycle, Environment, Cost.
- [x] **Service Factor** $S_f \in [1.2, 2.0]$ must always be applied.
- [x] **Three energy domains:** Electrical (clean, precise), Hydraulic (high force), Pneumatic (safe, simple).
- [x] **Reflected inertia** through gears: $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / N_g^2$.
- [x] **Lead screw torque:** $T = \dfrac{F p}{2 \pi \eta}$.
- [x] **Efficiencies:** Ball screw 90–95%, Spur gear ~98%, Worm gear 40–70%, Pneumatic 30–50%.
- [x] **Harmonic drives** = zero backlash + high ratio + compact → preferred in robotics.
- [x] **Always compute both torque AND power** to size the motor; check motor's torque–speed curve for the operating point.
- [x] **KTU CO mapping:** Topic supports **CO2** and **CO3** of OEAET831.
- [x] **Watch out for efficiency losses** in transmission — they double back at the motor side.
- [x] **Closed-loop mechatronic chain:** Controller → Drive → Actuator → Drive System → Load → Sensor → Controller.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Proportional, Integral, and Derivative (PID) Control; Example Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Mechatronic Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Proportional, Integral, and Derivative (PID) Control; Example Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Mechatronic Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a PID Controller?

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Controller** is a universal, industry-standard closed-loop feedback control mechanism that continuously calculates an *error value* $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint (SP) and a measured process variable (PV), and applies a corrective action based on three mathematically distinct terms — **Proportional**, **Integral**, and **Derivative** — to drive the system toward the desired state with optimal transient and steady-state response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "A PID controller is a control loop feedback mechanism widely used in industrial control systems. It calculates an error signal as the difference between a desired setpoint and a measured process variable, and applies a correction based on Proportional, Integral, and Derivative terms."

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Hot Shower" Problem

Imagine you are stepping into a shower and want water at exactly **40 °C** (the *setpoint*).

| Action You Take | PID Term it Mimics |
|---|---|
| **P** – You turn the hot/cold knob *proportional* to how far the temperature is from 40 °C. The bigger the gap, the faster you turn. | Proportional |
| **I** – After a few seconds, the water is at 38 °C but won't reach 40 °C. You keep nudging the knob slightly because the small persistent error *bothers* you. | Integral |
| **D** – You notice the water temperature is *rushing* past 40 °C (changing rapidly). You preemptively *slow down* your turning to avoid overshoot. | Derivative |

This is exactly what a PID controller does — **P** acts on the present error, **I** acts on the accumulation of past errors, and **D** acts on the rate of future-expected error.

### 1.3 Where is PID Used? (KTU Mechatronics Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> In mechatronic systems, PID controllers are the **backbone** of motion control, thermal management, fluid regulation, and robotic actuation. Almost every embedded microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, ESP32, PLCs) used in mechatronics implements some form of PID algorithm.

**Canonical Mechatronic Examples:**

1. **DC Motor Speed Control** – Maintaining a constant RPM despite load variations.
2. **Line-Following Robot** – Steering correction based on deviation from the path.
3. **Quadcopter / Drone Stabilization** – Adjusting rotor speeds to maintain level flight.
4. **3D Printer Hot-End Temperature Control** – Maintaining extruder at ~200 °C.
5. **Servo Motor Position Control** – Robotic arm joint angle regulation.
6. **HVAC / Cruise Control** – Slow drift correction over time.

### 1.4 The Generic Closed-Loop PID Block

A mechatronic PID system always has these physical elements:

- **Setpoint (SP):** Desired value (e.g., 1500 RPM, 90 °C).
- **Process / Plant:** The physical system being controlled (motor, heater, robot).
- **Sensor:** Measures the actual output (encoder, thermocouple, IMU).
- **Controller (PID):** Computes the corrective signal $u(t)$.
- **Actuator:** Applies the control signal to the plant (motor driver, MOSFET, valve).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PID Control Law — Mathematical Foundation

The continuous-time PID controller output is governed by the **parallel (non-interacting) form**:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ → Controller output signal sent to the actuator
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ → Error signal (setpoint − measured output)
- $K_p$ → **Proportional Gain** (unitless or in controller output units per error unit)
- $K_i$ → **Integral Gain** (typically in $\text{second}^{-1}$ scaling)
- $K_d$ → **Derivative Gain** (typically in seconds)
- $r(t)$ → Reference / setpoint signal
- $y(t)$ → Measured process variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An alternative but mathematically equivalent form uses $K_p$, $T_i$ (integral time), and $T_d$ (derivative time): $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$. KTU questions often use the $K_p, K_i, K_d$ form, so master it first.

## 2.2 The Three Control Actions — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Proportional (P) Control

The proportional term produces a control action **proportional to the instantaneous error**.

$$
u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)
$$

**Effect on System Response:**

- **Larger $K_p$** → Faster response, higher overshoot, possible oscillations.
- **Smaller $K_p$** → Slower response, more sluggish, more stable.
- **Limitation:** P-only control *always* leaves a **steady-state error (offset)** in systems that require a non-zero control effort to hold the setpoint (e.g., a motor needing torque to overcome friction).

### 2.2.2 Integral (I) Control

The integral term accumulates all past errors and produces an action proportional to the **time-integral of the error**.

$$
u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Effect on System Response:**

- **Eliminates steady-state error** — this is the I-term's signature contribution.
- **Larger $K_i$** → Faster error elimination, but increases overshoot and can cause *integral windup* (the integral grows unboundedly when the actuator saturates).
- **Smaller $K_i$** → Slow drift correction, more stable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Integral Windup (KTU Hot Topic):** When the actuator reaches its physical limit (e.g., valve fully open, motor at max PWM), the error keeps accumulating in the integral term, causing massive overshoot once the setpoint changes. Solutions include *anti-windup* (clamping the integral) and *back-calculation*.

### 2.2.3 Derivative (D) Control

The derivative term produces a control action proportional to the **rate of change of error**, predicting future error behavior.

$$
u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Effect on System Response:**

- **Provides damping** → Reduces overshoot and oscillations.
- **Improves transient response** → Sharpens the rise time settling behavior.
- **Larger $K_d$** → More damping, but amplifies high-frequency noise (the derivative of noise is huge).
- **Limitation:** Cannot respond to a *constant* error (derivative of a constant = 0). It is essentially useless without P or I.

### 2.3 PID Transfer Function (Laplace Domain)

Taking the Laplace transform of the PID law (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$
U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s \, E(s)
$$

The controller transfer function is therefore:

$$
C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s
$$

Or in a single rational form (multiplying by $s$):

$$
C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **pole at the origin** ($s = 0$) — this is the *integrator pole* that gives the PID its Type-1 system property, which guarantees zero steady-state error for a step input (when all three terms are active and tuned).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error signal | Same as output |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | Continuous PID law | Output units |
| 3 | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID transfer function | Output / Error |
| 4 | $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | Rational PID form | Output / Error |
| 5 | $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ | Relation to integral time $T_i$ | $1/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ | Relation to derivative time $T_d$ | s |
| 7 | $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 8 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$ | Steady-state error (Final Value Thm.) | Output units |
| 9 | $u_{\min} \le u(t) \le u_{\max}$ | Actuator saturation limits | Output units |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Mechatronics

The PID controller is the **unsung hero of modern automation**. In KTU's mechatronics curriculum, you will encounter it in:

- **Robotics:** Joint position and trajectory tracking.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Steering, throttle, and brake control.
- **Manufacturing:** CNC machine tool positioning.
- **Biomedical:** Infusion pump flow rate, prosthetics.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Image stabilization in cameras, drone flight controllers.
- **Process Industries:** Flow, level, pressure, and temperature loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Error Definition to Full PID Output

**Step 1 — Define the error signal.**
The error at any instant is the difference between the desired reference and the actual measured output.

$$
e(t) = r(t) - y(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Proportional Action.**
The P-term outputs a value directly proportional to the present error magnitude.

$$
u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)
$$

This responds to *where* the system is *right now* relative to the goal.

**Step 3 — Apply Integral Action.**
The I-term integrates the error over time, accumulating all past deviations.

$$
u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Why integrate?** Because if there is a *persistent* small error (e.g., a motor's load torque), the integral will grow unboundedly until the controller output is large enough to *force* the error to zero. Mathematically, the integral is the *antiderivative* of $e(t)$, so its time-derivative recovers $e(t)$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} u_I(t) = K_i \cdot e(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Derivative Action.**
The D-term differentiates the error, capturing its instantaneous rate of change.

$$
u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Why differentiate?** Because the rate of change tells us *where the system is heading*. If the error is dropping fast (system is approaching setpoint), we reduce the corrective effort to avoid overshoot.

**Step 5 — Combine the Three Actions (Superposition).**
Since each term is linear and independent, the total control effort is the sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t) \\
u(t) &= K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical continuous-time PID law**. *(End of derivation.)*

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Transfer Function in Laplace Domain

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace Transform of Each Term.**

For the P-term (linear, no dynamics):

$$
\mathcal{L}\{K_p \, e(t)\} = K_p \, E(s)
$$

For the I-term (integration in time $\leftrightarrow$ division by $s$):

$$
\mathcal{L}\!\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i}{s} E(s)
$$

For the D-term (differentiation in time $\leftrightarrow$ multiplication by $s$, assuming zero initial conditions):

$$
\mathcal{L}\!\left\{K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d s \, E(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Sum in the s-Domain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U(s) &= K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s \, E(s) \\
U(s) &= \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Transfer Function $C(s) = U(s) / E(s)$.**

$$
C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a Single Rational Expression.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$
C(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2}{s} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

This is the **proper rational transfer function** of the ideal (parallel) PID controller. *(End of derivation.)*

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step Response Behavior of P, I, D, and PID

The following table is **high-yield for KTU numerical and descriptive questions**. It summarizes how each term alone (or in combination) responds to a unit step setpoint change, $r(t) = 1(t)$, with a simple first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts + 1}$.

| Controller | Closed-Loop TF (unity feedback) | Rise Time $t_r$ | Overshoot $M_p$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P-only** ($K_p$) | $\dfrac{K K_p}{Ts + 1 + K K_p}$ | Fast (decreases with $K_p$) | Small to moderate | **Non-zero** $\dfrac{1}{1+KK_p}$ | Stable for $K_p > 0$ |
| **I-only** ($K_i/s$) | $\dfrac{K K_i}{Ts^2 + s + K K_i}$ | Slow | High (oscillatory) | **Zero** (for step) | Conditionally stable |
| **D-only** ($K_d s$) | $\dfrac{K K_d s}{Ts + 1 + K K_d s}$ | No useful rise | None | **Equals input** (useless alone) | Stable |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{Ts^2 + (1+KK_p)s + KK_i}$ | Moderate | Low | **Zero** | Generally stable |
| **PD** | $\dfrac{K(K_d s + K_p)}{Ts + 1 + K K_d s + K K_p}$ | Fast | **Greatly reduced** | Non-zero | Very stable |
| **Full PID** | $\dfrac{K(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{Ts^2 + (1+KK_d)s + KK_p s + KK_i}$ | Fast | **Minimal** | **Zero** | Stable (well-tuned) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Viva/Exams:** The **P-term** provides the *muscle*, the **I-term** provides the *memory* (kills offset), and the **D-term** provides the *brake* (reduces overshoot). All three are needed for the best performance in most mechatronic applications.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Steady-State Error for a Step Input

**Problem:** A unity-feedback system has plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{5}{s(s+2)}$ and a PI controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$. The setpoint is a unit step $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Find the steady-state error for $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 4$.

**Step 1 — Open-Loop Transfer Function.**

$$
L(s) = C(s) G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{5}{s(s+2)} = \frac{5(K_p s + K_i)}{s^2(s+2)}
$$

Substituting $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 4$:

$$
L(s) = \frac{5(2s + 4)}{s^2(s+2)} = \frac{10s + 20}{s^2(s+2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.**

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{10s+20}{s^2(s+2)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{10s+20}{s^2(s+2)}} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** Because the open-loop has *two integrators* ($s^2$ in denominator), this is a **Type-2 system**, and a Type-2 system has zero steady-state error for both step and ramp inputs. The integral action of the PI controller contributes one integrator, and the plant contributes another.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop PID Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP[Reference Setpoint r of t] --> SumNode{Summing Junction}
    SumNode -->|error e of t| PID[PID Controller C of s]
    PID -->|control signal u of t| Actuator[Actuator and Power Drive]
    Actuator --> Plant[Mechanism Plant G sub p of s]
    Plant --> Output[Process Variable y of t]
    Output --> Sensor[Feedback Sensor]
    Sensor --> SumNode
    SumNode -.->|subtract measured feedback| SumNode

    classDef setpoint fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef physical fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class SP setpoint
    class PID,SumNode controller
    class Actuator,Plant physical
    class Sensor sensor
    class Output output
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **setpoint** (desired value) enters the summing junction.
- The **feedback sensor** (encoder, thermocouple, etc.) measures the actual output and *subtracts* it.
- The **PID controller** computes $u(t)$ from $e(t)$.
- The **actuator + plant** convert $u(t)$ into physical motion/heat/flow.
- The loop continues until $y(t) \approx r(t)$.

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a PID Controller

```mermaid
graph TD
    ErrInput[Error Signal e of t] --> PBlock[Proportional Path K sub p]
    ErrInput --> IBlock[Integral Path K sub i over s]
    ErrInput --> DBlock[Derivative Path K sub d times s]

    PBlock --> Summer2{Sum}
    IBlock --> Summer2
    DBlock --> Summer2

    Summer2 --> Saturation[Actuator Saturation Limit]
    Saturation --> AntiWindup[Anti Windup Protection]
    AntiWindup --> Output[Controller Output u of t]

    classDef error fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef path fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef combine fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef safety fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class ErrInput error
    class PBlock,IBlock,DBlock path
    class Summer2 combine
    class Saturation,AntiWindup safety
    class Output out
```

## 4.3 Practical Mechatronic Implementation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Define Setpoint in software] --> B[Read Sensor via ADC or Encoder]
    B --> C[Compute Error e equals SP minus PV]
    C --> D[Apply PID Formula in Microcontroller]
    D --> E[Clamp Output to Actuator Limits]
    E --> F[Send PWM or DAC Signal to Actuator]
    F --> G[Wait for Sampling Period delta t]
    G --> B

    classDef stage fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G stage
```

**Typical Sampling Periods in Mechatronics:**

| Application | Sampling Rate | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DC Motor Speed | 1–10 ms | Encoder feedback |
| Temperature Control | 100–1000 ms | Slow thermal dynamics |
| Drone / IMU | 1–5 ms | High-speed stabilization |
| Servo Position | 1–20 ms | Depends on mechanical inertia |

## 4.4 Mapping Concepts to a Real Mechatronic Subsystem (DC Motor Speed Control)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Target[Target RPM Setpoint] --> MCU[Microcontroller e.g. Arduino]
    MCU -->|PWM Signal| Driver[Motor Driver L298N or H Bridge]
    Driver --> Motor[DC Motor]
    Motor --> Shaft[Rotating Shaft]
    Shaft --> Encoder[Optical Encoder]
    Encoder -->|Pulses| MCU

    MCU -.->|PID Algorithm Runs| MCU

    classDef goal fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef brain fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef power fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef mech fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class Target goal
    class MCU brain
    class Driver,Motor power
    class Shaft,Encoder mech
    class MCU fb
```

**Working Principle:**
1. User sets target RPM (e.g., 1500).
2. MCU reads encoder pulses to compute actual RPM.
3. Error = 1500 − actual RPM.
4. PID algorithm computes required PWM duty cycle.
5. Motor driver adjusts voltage, and the loop repeats at the sampling rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a PID controller. State the effect of increasing the proportional gain $K_p$ on the steady-state error of a closed-loop control system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is a feedback control mechanism that continuously computes an error signal $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint and a measured process variable, and produces a control output consisting of three terms: a term proportional to the error, a term proportional to the integral of the error, and a term proportional to the derivative of the error.

**Effect of increasing $K_p$:**
- It **reduces** the steady-state error (inversely proportional to $1 + K_p K_{plant}$).
- It **speeds up** the transient response (faster rise time).
- It **increases** overshoot and may cause oscillations if pushed too high.
- It **does not completely eliminate** the steady-state error in a P-only system.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is integral windup? Mention one method to prevent it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Integral windup** is a phenomenon in PID controllers where, during actuator saturation (when the controller output exceeds the actuator's physical limits), the integral term continues to accumulate error. This causes the integral to grow to very large values, leading to severe overshoot and slow recovery once the setpoint changes.

**Prevention Method — Anti-Windup via Clamping:**
When the controller output saturates, the integration is *frozen* (or "clamped") so the integral term does not grow further. Common techniques include:
- **Conditional Integration:** Stop integrating when output is at saturation.
- **Back-Calculation:** Recompute the integral using the difference between the actual and saturated output.
- **Clamping:** Set upper and lower limits on the integral term directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Explain the structure and working of a PID controller with a neat block diagram. Derive the transfer function of a PID controller in the Laplace domain. Also discuss the effect of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) on the system response.** (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a) — Structure, Block Diagram, and Working (7 Marks)**

A PID controller operates in a **closed-loop feedback system** as shown in the block diagram (see SECTION 4.1). The key components are:

1. **Setpoint (Reference) Input $r(t)$** — The desired value.
2. **Summing Junction** — Computes $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
3. **PID Controller** — Computes $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$.
4. **Actuator + Plant** — Applies the physical control action.
5. **Sensor** — Measures the actual output and feeds it back.

**Working Principle:**
- The setpoint and feedback are continuously compared.
- The error drives the three parallel paths (P, I, D).
- The summed output $u(t)$ is sent to the actuator.
- The process variable is re-measured and the loop repeats at the sampling period $\Delta t$.

#### **Part (b) — Transfer Function Derivation and Effect of Each Term (7 Marks)**

**Derivation** (full step-by-step shown in SECTION 3.2):

$$
C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

**Effect of Each Term on System Response:**

| Term | Action | Positive Effect | Negative Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$K_p$ (Proportional)** | $K_p \cdot e(t)$ | Faster rise time, increased system responsiveness | Cannot eliminate steady-state error; high $K_p$ causes overshoot and oscillation |
| **$K_i$ (Integral)** | $K_i \int e\,dt$ | Eliminates steady-state error completely | Causes overshoot, slow settling; integral windup during saturation |
| **$K_d$ (Derivative)** | $K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | Adds damping, reduces overshoot, improves stability | Amplifies sensor noise; ineffective against constant error |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often *describe* the three terms but **forget to state what error/response property each affects** (rise time, overshoot, steady-state error). Marks are explicitly awarded for linking each term to its specific effect.
> - Many students write the PID law without the **Laplace transform derivation** — this is a compulsory 3-mark component in any 14-mark question.
> - Do **not** confuse the **series (classical) form** $K_p(1 + 1/T_i s + T_d s)$ with the **parallel form** $K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. KTU 2024 scheme uses the **parallel form**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**With a suitable example of a mechatronic system, explain how a PID control algorithm is implemented in practice. Discuss the tuning methods (Ziegler-Nichols) and the role of anti-windup techniques.** (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a) — Practical Implementation on a Mechatronic System (7 Marks)**

**Example System: DC Motor Speed Control using Arduino**

**Hardware Components:**
- Arduino Uno (or STM32/ESP32) microcontroller.
- DC motor with optical encoder for speed feedback.
- L298N motor driver (H-bridge).
- Power supply (e.g., 12 V battery).

**Software Implementation Steps (in Arduino C-like pseudocode):**

1. **Define Setpoint:** `int setpoint = 1500; // target RPM`
2. **Initialize PID Gains:** $K_p, K_i, K_d$ chosen from tuning.
3. **Setup Timer Interrupt:** Sample every $\Delta t = 10$ ms.
4. **In Interrupt Service Routine (ISR):**
   - Read encoder count → compute actual RPM.
   - Compute `error = setpoint - actualRPM`.
   - Compute `P = Kp * error`.
   - Compute `I += Ki * error * dt`.
   - Compute `D = Kd * (error - prevError) / dt`.
   - Compute `output = P + I + D`.
   - **Clamp** output: `output = constrain(output, 0, 255)`.
   - Write to PWM: `analogWrite(motorPin, output)`.
   - Update `prevError = error`.
5. **Anti-Windup:** `if (output at limits) I = previous_I;` (clamping).

This discrete-time approximation is:

$$
u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \Delta t + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Ziegler-Nichols Tuning and Anti-Windup (7 Marks)**

**Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Method (Ultimate Cycle / Closed-Loop Method):**

1. Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$ (use P-only control).
2. Gradually increase $K_p$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude.
3. Record this critical gain as $K_u$ and the oscillation period as $T_u$.
4. Use the **Ziegler-Nichols table** to set $K_p, K_i, K_d$:

| Controller Type | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 K_u$ | $T_u / 1.2$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.6 K_u$ | $T_u / 2$ | $T_u / 8$ |

Then convert: $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$.

**Anti-Windup Techniques:**

1. **Clamping:** When $u(t)$ saturates, freeze $u_I(t)$.
2. **Back-Calculation:** A feedback loop on the saturation error adjusts the integrator.
3. **Conditional Integration:** Disable the I-term when error sign changes (avoids accumulation).
4. **Integrator Resetting:** Manually reset integral when a large setpoint change occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often list Ziegler-Nichols numbers *without explaining the procedure to obtain $K_u$ and $T_u$*. Always state the **methodology** (closed-loop ultimate gain test).
> - Many forget to **convert** $T_i$ and $T_d$ into $K_i$ and $K_d$ when answering. Show the conversion explicitly.
> - Anti-windup is a **favourite 2–3 mark sub-question**. Do not skip it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU OEAET831 Module 2**

- **PID** stands for **Proportional-Integral-Derivative** — a universal feedback controller.
- The **control law** is $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$.
- **Error signal** is $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ (setpoint minus measured output).
- The **Laplace-domain transfer function** is $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$.
- A pole at the origin makes the closed-loop a **Type-1 system** (zero $e_{ss}$ for a step).
- **P-term** → Reduces rise time, reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error.
- **I-term** → Eliminates steady-state error, but causes overshoot and integral windup.
- **D-term** → Adds damping, reduces overshoot, but amplifies noise.
- **Integral windup** occurs when the actuator saturates and the integral keeps growing — prevented by **anti-windup** techniques (clamping, back-calculation, conditional integration).
- **Ziegler-Nichols tuning** uses the ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$ to compute $K_p, K_i, K_d$ — commonly tested in KTU 14-mark questions.
- **Discrete PID form** (used in microcontrollers): $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[j]\Delta t + K_d \dfrac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$.
- **Practical applications** in mechatronics: DC motor speed, line-following robots, drone stabilization, 3D printer hot-end, servo position, HVAC, cruise control.
- **Sampling period $\Delta t$** must be chosen based on system bandwidth (typically 5–10× faster than the dominant time constant).
- **Derivative kick** (a derivative of a sudden setpoint change) can be avoided by differentiating the **process variable** $y(t)$ instead of the error $e(t)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
