<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuation System?

In Mechatronics, an **Actuator** is the "muscle" of the system. It is the energy-conversion device that takes a control signal (electrical, electronic, or digital) from the controller and produces a corresponding **mechanical output** — a force, torque, displacement, or rotational motion — to act upon the physical environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** An actuator is a component of a mechatronic system that converts a control input signal (from the controller) into a physical, mechanical action such as linear motion, rotary motion, force, or torque.

The classification of actuators relevant to this module is:

| Family | Energy Domain | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical | Electric → Mechanical | DC/Stepper/Servo Motors, Solenoids |
| **Hydraulic** | **Hydraulic (Fluid) → Mechanical** | **Hydraulic Cylinders, Hydraulic Motors** |
| **Pneumatic** | **Pneumatic (Air) → Mechanical** | **Pneumatic Cylinders, Pneumatic Motors** |
| Thermal / Piezo | Heat / Crystal strain | Shape memory alloys, Piezo stacks |

---

## 1.2 Hydraulic Actuation Systems

A **hydraulic actuation system** uses a **pressurized incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil, water-glycol mix, or synthetic fluid) to transmit power from a prime mover to an actuator.

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **hydraulic actuation system** is a power-transmission system in which a pressurized, incompressible liquid (oil) confined in a closed circuit is used to generate, transmit, and control linear or rotary mechanical power. The system strictly follows **Pascal's Law** for pressure propagation.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Syringe & Tube" Model
Imagine two connected syringes of different sizes, completely filled with water, with no air bubbles, and connected by a thin tube. If you push the small piston down with a force of just **10 N**, the water transmits the pressure equally to the large piston. Because the larger piston has a much bigger area, the *force coming out* on the large side becomes hundreds of Newtons — but it moves a much *shorter distance*. That is **Pascal's Law** in action — and that is exactly how a hydraulic press or hydraulic cylinder works.

### Key Properties of Hydraulic Systems
- **Working fluid:** Oil (incompressible, density ≈ **850–900 kg/m³**)
- **Operating pressure range:** Typically **50 bar to 350 bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)
- **Power source:** Electric motor coupled to a hydraulic pump
- **Force per unit pressure:** Very high (because of incompressibility)
- **Stiffness:** Very stiff (almost no spring-back under load)
- **Speed control:** Excellent (using flow control valves)
- **Position accuracy:** ±0.1 mm or better (with servo valves)

---

## 1.3 Pneumatic Actuation Systems

A **pneumatic actuation system** uses **pressurized compressible gas** (almost always filtered, dried atmospheric air) to transmit power.

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **pneumatic actuation system** is a power-transmission system that uses a **compressible gas (air)** under pressure to generate and control linear or rotary mechanical motion. The system operates on the principle of **pressure-induced expansion** of the working medium.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Balloon & Syringe" Model
Take a sealed syringe and pull the plunger back — air rushes in. Now push the plunger in while blocking the outlet with your finger. The trapped air **compresses** (unlike water, which won't compress) and pushes the plunger back out. That springiness is the behaviour of air. Pneumatic systems exploit this compressibility: air is cheap, abundant, safe, and the "spring-back" doesn't damage the system. The trade-off is that you **cannot** position a pneumatic cylinder precisely without extra mechanical brakes, because air gives a little under load.

### Key Properties of Pneumatic Systems
- **Working fluid:** Atmospheric air (compressible, density ≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP)
- **Operating pressure range:** Typically **2 bar to 10 bar** (commonly **6 bar**)
- **Power source:** Electric motor + air compressor + receiver tank
- **Force per unit pressure:** Low compared to hydraulics
- **Stiffness:** Compliant (air compresses)
- **Speed control:** Excellent
- **Position accuracy:** Poor without external damping / braking
- **Safety:** Inherently safe (no fire hazard from leaks)

---

## 1.4 Head-to-Head Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question** (Module 3, ESE pattern).

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working medium | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Operating pressure | 50 – 350 bar | 2 – 10 bar |
| Force / Torque output | **Very high** | **Moderate** |
| Speed range | 0.01 – 1 m/s | 0.05 – 10 m/s |
| Position accuracy | Excellent | Poor (compliant) |
| Stiffness | High | Low |
| Cost | Expensive | Cheap |
| Cleanliness | Can leak oil | Clean |
| Fire hazard | Possible (mineral oil) | None |
| Typical use | Press, CNC, Injection moulding, Aircraft | Packaging, Pick-and-place, Clamping, Tools |

---

## 1.5 Foundational Law — Pascal's Law (Cradle of Fluid Power)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** *Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished and equally in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.*

Mathematically:
$$P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \cdots = P$$

For a fluid-power machine, this means the **force produced** depends *only* on the **pressure** and the **area of the exposed piston face**:
$$\boxed{F = P \times A}$$

This single equation is the basis of every cylinder and rotary actuator calculation in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Architecture of a Fluid-Power System

Whether hydraulic or pneumatic, every system has the same five functional blocks:

1. **Energy Source** — Prime mover (electric motor / engine)
2. **Supply Unit** — Pump (hydraulic) or Compressor + Receiver (pneumatic)
3. **Control Elements** — Directional / Pressure / Flow control valves
4. **Conditioning & Delivery** — Pipes, hoses, filters, regulators, lubricators
5. **Actuator** — Cylinder or motor (the muscle)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hydraulic Power Pack** and the **Pneumatic Service Unit (FRL — Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** are compulsory sub-units. Skipping the FRL in a pneumatic circuit is a guaranteed **valuation-penalty point** in KTU board exams.

---

## 2.2 Reciprocating Cylinders — The Workhorses of Mechatronics

A **reciprocating (linear) cylinder** converts fluid pressure into straight-line pushing/pulling force. It is the most common hydraulic and pneumatic actuator.

### 2.2.1 Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)

- Fluid pressure acts on **only one side** of the piston.
- The return stroke is achieved by an **external spring** (spring-return type) or by a **load (gravity) itself** (gravity-return type).
- Used in clamping, lifting, stamping, tool-locking.
- Force is produced in **one direction only**.

**Extension (working) stroke force:**
$$F_{ext} = P \times A_1 - F_{friction} - F_{spring}$$
where $A_1$ is the full piston area (piston side), and the retraction is passive.

### 2.2.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)

- Fluid pressure is applied **alternately to both sides** of the piston.
- Force is available in **both directions** — no spring, no gravity dependency.
- Used in **90 % of industrial mechatronic applications** (robots, pick-and-place, CNC slides, AGVs).

**Extension stroke force:**
$$F_{ext} = P_1 \times A_1 - P_2 \times A_2 - F_{friction}$$

**Retraction stroke force:**
$$F_{ret} = P_2 \times A_2 - P_1 \times A_1' - F_{friction}$$

where:
- $A_1$ = full piston area = $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- $A_2$ = annular (rod-side) area = $\dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$
- $D$ = piston diameter, $d$ = piston rod diameter
- $P_1$ = cap-end pressure, $P_2$ = rod-end pressure

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** The retraction force is *always less* than the extension force for the same supply pressure, because $A_2 < A_1$ (the rod steals area from the piston face). Many students incorrectly write $F_{ext} = F_{ret}$.

### 2.2.3 Cylinder Variants (commonly asked in 3-markers)

- **Tandem cylinder** — Two pistons in series, **double output force** for same pressure.
- **Telescopic cylinder** — Nested sleeves, **long stroke from short installed length** (used in dump trucks, cranes).
- **Double-rod (through-rod) cylinder** — Rod on both sides, **equal force both ways** (good for precision slides).
- **Bellows / Diaphragm cylinder** — No piston seal leakage; used in food/pharma.

---

## 2.3 Rotary Actuators (Fluid Motors)

A **rotary actuator** converts fluid pressure into **continuous (or limited-rotation) torque** and angular motion. They are the "fluid equivalent" of an electric motor.

Three rotary types are in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### 2.3.1 Gear-Type Rotary Actuator (Gear Motor)

- A pair of **meshing external (or internal) gears** is housed in a sealed casing.
- Pressurized fluid enters one port, pushes against one tooth gap, and forces the gears to rotate; the fluid exhausts from the other port.
- **Continuous rotation** possible (unlike vane or lobe).
- Construction is simple, robust, and tolerant of dirty/contaminated fluid.
- Typical applications: **conveyor drives, winches, mobile hydraulics, agricultural machinery, road rollers**.
- Disadvantage: Limited speed range; gear meshing causes pressure ripple and noise.

**Theoretical displacement (volume per revolution), external gear pair:**
$$V_D = 2 \times \pi \times m \times b \times z \quad \text{(approx.)}$$
where $m$ = module, $b$ = face width, $z$ = number of teeth.
For KTU board exams, the simpler form is sufficient:
$$V_D \approx \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \times b$$
where $D_o$ and $D_i$ are the outer and inner gear pitch diameters.

**Theoretical torque:**
$$T = \dfrac{\Delta P \times V_D}{2\pi}$$

### 2.3.2 Vane-Type Rotary Actuator (Vane Motor)

- A **rotor with sliding vanes** is mounted eccentrically inside a stator (cam ring).
- Pressurized fluid enters the inlet port, pushes against the vanes, and turns the rotor.
- Number of vanes = typically **2, 3, 4, 5, 6** (commonly 4).
- Each vane sweeps a chamber whose volume changes from a minimum to a maximum during one revolution.
- The **angle of rotation is limited by ports** (typically a few degrees up to ~360° depending on design).
- High-speed, smooth, low-noise.
- Applications: **Machine tool spindles, compressors, low-power robots, copy-milling heads**.

**Theoretical displacement per revolution (single-vane, double-acting):**
$$V_D = 2 \times b \times (R^2 - r^2) \times \pi \quad \text{for full 360°}$$
**Theoretical torque:**
$$T = \dfrac{\Delta P \times V_D}{2\pi}$$
where $R$ = stator inner radius, $r$ = rotor radius, $b$ = vane width.

> [!NOTE]
> For **finite-rotation vane actuators** (a popular 7-marker topic), the swept volume is $V_D = b \times (R^2 - r^2) \times \theta$ and torque $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \times V_D}{2\pi}$ where $\theta$ is the rotation in radians.

### 2.3.3 Lobe-Type Rotary Actuator (Lobe Motor)

- A **three-lobe (or multi-lobe) rotor** rotates inside a closely fitting stator housing.
- Constructionally similar to a **rotary lobe pump working in reverse**.
- Fluid is trapped between consecutive lobes and the casing; as the rotor turns, this trapped volume expands (suction port) and contracts (delivery port).
- Used for **slow-speed, high-torque applications** (e.g., mixers, agitators, slow conveyors, rotary valves).
- Pulse-free flow, gentle fluid handling, can be used with viscous fluids.
- **Distinct from gear type**: lobes do not mesh — they clear each other with a small gap, so there is **no metal-to-metal contact** at the rotor/stator interface.

---

## 2.4 The Master Formula Sheet (High-Yield for KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table. Every derivation, every 7-marker, every 14-marker in Module 3 of OEAET831 draws from these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Symbol Glossary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder force (extension) | $F = P \cdot A_1$ | **N** | $P$ = pressure, $A_1 = \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ |
| 2 | Cylinder force (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2$ | **N** | $A_2 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ |
| 3 | Piston velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | **m/s** | $Q$ = flow rate, $A$ = active area |
| 4 | Cylinder stroke time | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | **s** | $L$ = stroke length |
| 5 | Cylinder power (hydraulic) | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ | **W** | $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 6 | Pump / Compressor power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{\eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | **W** | $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 7 | Rotary torque (any motor) | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | **N·m** | $V_D$ = displacement/rev, $\Delta P$ = P₁ − P₂ |
| 8 | Rotary speed | $N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$ | **rev/s** | (multiply by 60 for **rpm**) |
| 9 | Motor output power | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T$ | **W** | $\omega$ in rad/s, $N$ in rev/s |
| 10 | Pascal's law | $P_1 = P_2$ (enclosed fluid) | **Pa** | Foundation of fluid power |
| 11 | Volume modulus (oil) | $\beta \approx \mathbf{1.4 \times 10^9 \; Pa}$ to $\mathbf{2.0 \times 10^9 \; Pa}$ | **Pa** | For stiffness problems |
| 12 | Air compressibility | $P V = n R T$ (ideal gas) | — | For pneumatic density calcs |

### Standard Unit Conversions (board exam survival kit)

$$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$$
$$1 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$
$$1 \text{ psi} \approx 0.06895 \text{ bar}$$
$$1 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} \approx 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$
$$1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial robotics**: Pneumatic cylinders for pick-and-place end-effectors (cheap, fast, safe in food lines).
- **CNC machine tools**: Hydraulic cylinders and motors for heavy-duty slides and spindle drives (high stiffness, no vibration).
- **Automotive**: Hydraulic brake boosters, power steering, hydraulic jacks; pneumatic brakes in heavy trucks and trains.
- **Aerospace**: Hydraulic actuation for flaps, landing gear, and flight control surfaces (high power-to-weight).
- **Manufacturing automation**: Hydraulic presses for forging and stamping; pneumatic grippers in assembly lines.
- **Medical & pharma**: Pneumatic diaphragm cylinders (sterile, leak-tight).
- **Mobile equipment**: Excavators, loaders, dump trucks — heavy-duty hydraulic cylinders and vane/gear motors.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Produced by a Double-Acting Cylinder

**Problem Setup (KTU 2024 standard):**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter $D$ and a rod diameter $d$. Pressurised oil at pressure $P_1$ enters the cap (blank) end, while the rod end is at pressure $P_2$. Find (a) the extension force and (b) the retraction force.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the active areas.**
- The full piston area (cap-end side, the bigger side) is:
$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$
- The annular piston area (rod-end side, the smaller side) is:
$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law for the extension stroke.**

The cap-end pressure $P_1$ pushes the piston forward over area $A_1$. The rod-end fluid at $P_2$ opposes the motion over area $A_2$. The net force on the piston (ignoring friction first) is:

$$F_{ext,ideal} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2$$

**Step 3 — Include friction and load weight.**

$$F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 - F_{friction} - W_{load}$$

In most KTU numericals, friction and load weight are either given or neglected. The KTU-recognised simplified form is:

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2}$$

**Step 4 — For the retraction stroke.**

Now pressurise the rod-end at $P_2'$ and vent the cap-end to tank (≈ 0). The retraction force is:

$$F_{ret} = P_2' A_2 - P_1' A_1$$

If we assume a single supply $P$ and the opposite side vented:

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Force ratio (commonly asked).**

$$\dfrac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \dfrac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^2$$

For the standard industry ratio $d/D = 0.5$, the ratio is $0.75$, i.e., the retraction force is **75 % of the extension force** for equal pressure. This is why double-rod cylinders (with rods on both ends) are preferred in high-precision bidirectional drives.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piston Velocity and Cylinder Stroke Time

**Step 1 — Continuity of fluid flow.**

If the cylinder has active area $A$ and the pump delivers a constant flow rate $Q$, then in time $dt$ the volume entering the cylinder is $Q \cdot dt = A \cdot ds$, where $ds$ is the piston displacement. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{ds}{dt} = v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$$

$$\boxed{v = \dfrac{Q}{A}}$$

**Step 2 — Stroke time.**

If the cylinder must travel a stroke length $L$:

$$t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q} = \dfrac{V_{cylinder}}{Q}$$

$$\boxed{t = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}}$$

**Step 3 — Extension/retraction time asymmetry.**

Since $A_1 \neq A_2$ for a single-rod cylinder, the *same* flow $Q$ produces different speeds:

$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1}, \quad v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2}$$

So the retraction stroke is **faster** than the extension stroke (smaller area → higher speed). This is a very common viva/short-answer observation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Flow Through a Hydraulic Cylinder

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power input.**

Fluid at pressure $P$ flowing at rate $Q$ carries power:
$$P_{hyd,in} = P \cdot Q$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical power output.**

The cylinder delivers a force $F = P \cdot A$ at velocity $v = Q/A$:
$$P_{mech,out} = F \cdot v = (P A) \cdot \dfrac{Q}{A} = P \cdot Q$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{P_{hyd,in} = P_{mech,out} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v}$$

The same form holds for rotary actuators: $P = T \cdot \omega = 2 \pi N T$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Torque of a Vane Motor

**Step 1 — Geometry of one chamber.**

Consider a single-vane, double-acting vane motor. The stator is a circular ring of inner radius $R$, the rotor has radius $r$, and the vane width is $b$. For one vane sweeping a full revolution, the swept volume per revolution per side is:

$$V_{one} = \pi (R^2 - r^2) \cdot b$$

**Step 2 — Double-acting (two active sides per vane per revolution):**

$$V_D = 2 \cdot \pi (R^2 - r^2) \cdot b$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical torque derivation.**

The pressure difference $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ acts over the projected vane area. By the principle of virtual work, work done per revolution = pressure × displacement volume:

$$W_{rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

Also, $W_{rev} = T \cdot 2\pi$ (torque × angle in radians). Equating:

$$T \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot b (R^2 - r^2)}{1}}$$

(For a 4-vane, single-acting, finite-rotation actuator with angle $\theta$ rad, $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot b (R^2 - r^2) \cdot \theta}{2\pi}$.)

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Speed of Rotation for a Gear / Vane / Lobe Motor

**Step 1 — Displacement per revolution $V_D$ is the key motor "constant".**

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow balance.**

In one revolution, the motor swallows a volume equal to its displacement. Therefore:

$$N \cdot V_D = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$$

where $N$ is in **revolutions per second** and $Q$ is in **m³/s**.

For **rpm**:

$$\boxed{N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot Q}{V_D}}$$

**Step 3 — Slip correction.**

Real motors leak internally; the actual speed is:

$$N_{actual} = N_{ideal} \cdot \eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \eta_{vol}}{V_D}$$

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical Worked Example — **Model Question**

> **Question:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ and rod diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm}$. The pump supplies oil at a pressure of $P = 80 \text{ bar}$ with a flow rate of $Q = 30 \text{ L/min}$. The cylinder must push a load of $F_L = 40 \text{ kN}$ over a stroke of $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$. Find (i) the extension force, (ii) the retraction force, (iii) the extension time, (iv) the hydraulic power developed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit conversions.**
$$D = 0.1 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.05 \text{ m}$$
$$P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$
$$Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute areas.**
$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1^2 - 0.05^2)}{4} = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Extension force.**
$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 = 8 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 62\,832 \text{ N} \approx 62.83 \text{ kN}}$$
✓ The cylinder can lift a 40 kN load with **22.83 kN** of margin.

**Step 4 — Retraction force.**
$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 = 8 \times 10^6 \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 47\,123 \text{ N} \approx 47.12 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Extension time.**
$$t_{ext} = \dfrac{A_1 \cdot L}{Q} = \dfrac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{t_{ext} = 7.854 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic power.**
$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 8 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4000 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 4 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Actuator

| Application | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Lift 5 tonnes in a press | **Hydraulic DAC** | High force, incompressible, precise stop |
| Open a factory door | **Pneumatic SAC** | Cheap, safe, low duty cycle |
| Rotate a conveyor at 100 rpm | **Hydraulic gear motor** | Continuous rotation, dirty-fluid tolerant |
| Rotate a small tool head 270° | **Pneumatic vane actuator** | Limited rotation, smooth, fast |
| Stir a viscous chemical mix | **Lobe motor (hydraulic)** | Pulse-free, gentle on fluid, low speed, high torque |
| Move a pick-and-place gripper | **Pneumatic SAC/DAC** | Lightweight, clean, fast, cheap |
| CNC milling spindle | **Hydraulic or AC servo** | High stiffness, accurate positioning |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State Pascal's Law and explain its significance in hydraulic actuation systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Pascal's Law states that *pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished and equally in every direction*.

**Significance in hydraulics:**
- It is the fundamental principle that allows a small input force to produce a large output force in a hydraulic cylinder.
- It forms the basis of all force-multiplication devices (lifts, jacks, brakes).
- It guarantees that the pressure at the pump outlet equals the pressure at the actuator inlet, provided line losses are neglected.
- Combined with $F = P \times A$, it allows **independent control of force (by pressure) and speed (by flow rate)** in fluid-power systems.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. Give one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Pressurised chambers | **One** (cap end only) | **Two** (cap end and rod end) |
| Stroke direction of force | Extension only | Both extension and retraction |
| Return mechanism | Spring or gravity or load | Pressurised fluid itself |
| Force output | Unidirectional | Bidirectional |
| Control complexity | Simple | Requires 4/2 or 4/3 DCV |
| Application | **Clamp in a CNC vice** | **Pick-and-place robot arm** |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Instructions (KTU pattern):** Answer **any one** of the following two full questions. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. Derive the expression for its **extension** and **retraction forces**.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (7 marks layout):**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of:
- A **cylinder barrel/tube** (precision honed steel tube).
- A **piston** fitted inside, sealed by **piston seals** (usually polyurethane or nitrile).
- A **piston rod** of diameter $d$ attached to the piston, protruding through one end.
- A **gland bushing and rod seal (wiper)** at the rod-exit end to prevent leakage and contamination.
- **Two end caps** — one closed (cap end) with port $P_1$, and one with the rod-exit hole and port $P_2$.
- **Cushioning** may be provided at one or both ends (a small needle valve traps oil to decelerate the piston near end-of-stroke).
- **Mounting styles**: clevis, trunnion, foot, or flange.

**Working (refer Fig in SECTION 4.3):**
- **Extension stroke:** Pump sends oil at $P_1$ to the **cap end** (port A). Oil pushes the piston forward over the full area $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. The rod-end oil is pushed out through port B back to tank.
- **Retraction stroke:** Pump oil is directed to the **rod end** (port B) at $P_2$. Oil pushes the piston backward over the annular area $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi(D^2 - d^2)}{4}$. Cap-end oil returns to tank.
- Direction reversal is achieved by a **4/2 or 4/3 directional control valve**.

**Derivation — Extension Force:**

By Pascal's law, pressure on cap-end = $P_1$. Net forward force on the piston (ignoring friction):

$$F_{ext} = P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

If rod-end back-pressure is $P_2$:

$$F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2$$

**Derivation — Retraction Force:**

$$F_{ret} = P_2 \cdot A_2 = P_2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

If cap-end back-pressure is $P_1'$:

$$F_{ret} = P_2 A_2 - P_1' A_1$$

**Important consequence:**

$$\dfrac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \dfrac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^2$$

For $d/D = 0.5$, $F_{ret} = 0.75 \, F_{ext}$. This is the basis of the **double-rod (through-rod) cylinder** used where equal bidirectional force is needed.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, and stroke $L = 400$ mm. The pump delivers oil at a flow rate of $Q = 25$ L/min and pressure $P = 100$ bar. Calculate:
**(i) Extension force  (ii) Retraction force  (iii) Extension time  (iv) Retraction time  (v) Hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$D = 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.045 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.400 \text{ m}$$
$$Q = 25 \text{ L/min} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$
$$P = 100 \text{ bar} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**
$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi(0.08^2 - 0.045^2)}{4} = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension force:**
$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 = 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 50\,270 \text{ N}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction force:**
$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 = 10^7 \times 3.436 \times 10^{-3} = 34\,360 \text{ N}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ret} \approx 34.36 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Extension time:**
$$t_{ext} = \dfrac{A_1 \cdot L}{Q} = \dfrac{5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{t_{ext} \approx 4.83 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 5 — Retraction time:**
$$t_{ret} = \dfrac{A_2 \cdot L}{Q} = \dfrac{3.436 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{t_{ret} \approx 3.30 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic power:**
$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 10^7 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} = 4166.7 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_{hyd} \approx 4.17 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` With the help of a diagram, explain the construction and working of a **vane-type rotary actuator**. Derive the expression for its **theoretical torque**.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A vane-type rotary actuator consists of:
- A **stator (cam ring)** of internal radius $R$, fixed to the housing.
- A **rotor** of radius $r$ (< R), mounted on the output shaft, mounted **eccentrically** inside the stator.
- **Sliding vanes** (typically 2 to 6) housed in radial slots in the rotor.
- **Springs or hydraulic pressure** push each vane outward to seal against the stator bore.
- **Two ports**: a pressure inlet and an exhaust port, located to direct fluid into the expanding chambers.

**Working:**
- Pressurised fluid enters the inlet port and fills the **expanding-volume chamber** between two consecutive vanes.
- The pressure pushes against the vane surface and exerts a tangential force, creating torque on the rotor.
- The opposite chamber (on the other side) is connected to exhaust, so its pressure is low.
- The net torque rotates the rotor toward the exhaust side. Rotation may be limited (a few degrees to 360°) or continuous.

**Derivation — Theoretical Torque:**

Consider a vane of width $b$. The pressure $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ acts on the projected vane area (stator-bore area minus rotor area).

Volume displaced per revolution, per vane, per side:
$$V_{one} = \pi (R^2 - r^2) \cdot b$$

For a double-acting vane (pressure acts on both sides of one vane per revolution):
$$V_D = 2 \pi b (R^2 - r^2)$$

Work done by the fluid per revolution:
$$W_{rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

This equals torque times angular displacement per revolution ($2\pi$ radians):
$$T \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \Delta P \cdot b \cdot (R^2 - r^2)}$$

**For a 4-vane, single-acting, finite-rotation actuator** with rotation $\theta$ in radians:

$$T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot b \cdot (R^2 - r^2) \cdot \theta}{2\pi}$$

**Speed of rotation** (continuous motor):

$$N = \dfrac{Q \cdot \eta_{vol}}{V_D} \quad \text{(rev/s)}$$

**Application areas:** Machine tool spindles, low-power robot joints, copying / milling heads, paint sprayers.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` A **vane-type rotary actuator** has a stator radius $R = 60$ mm, rotor radius $r = 40$ mm, and vane width $b = 30$ mm. The supply pressure is $P_1 = 7$ bar and exhaust pressure is $P_2 = 0.5$ bar. The required rotation is $\theta = 90°$. Determine:
**(i) The volume of fluid consumed per stroke.**
**(ii) The theoretical torque developed.**
**(iii) The work done per stroke.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$R = 0.060 \text{ m}, \quad r = 0.040 \text{ m}, \quad b = 0.030 \text{ m}$$
$$P_1 = 7 \text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}, \quad P_2 = 0.5 \text{ bar} = 0.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$
$$\theta = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 1 — Pressure difference:**
$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = (7 - 0.5) \times 10^5 = 6.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Volume per stroke (single-vane, single-acting):**
For $\theta = 90° = \pi/2$ rad, the swept volume is the fraction $\theta / (2\pi)$ of the full revolution volume:
$$V_{stroke} = b (R^2 - r^2) \theta = 0.030 \times (0.060^2 - 0.040^2) \times 1.5708$$
$$V_{stroke} = 0.030 \times (0.0036 - 0.0016) \times 1.5708$$
$$V_{stroke} = 0.030 \times 0.0020 \times 1.5708 = 9.425 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$
$$\boxed{V_{stroke} \approx 94.25 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical torque:**
For a single-vane, single-acting, finite-rotation actuator:
$$T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot b (R^2 - r^2) \cdot \theta}{2\pi}$$
$$T = \dfrac{6.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.030 \times 0.0020 \times 1.5708}{2\pi}$$
$$T = \dfrac{61.26}{6.2832} \approx 9.75 \text{ N·m}$$
$$\boxed{T \approx 9.75 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Work done per stroke:**
$$W = T \cdot \theta = 9.75 \times 1.5708 \approx 15.32 \text{ J}$$
Or equivalently, $W = \Delta P \cdot V_{stroke} = 6.5 \times 10^5 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-5} \approx 61.26$ J — wait, this differs by a factor. The discrepancy is because the **work stored in the system equals $\Delta P \cdot V$**, but the **useful mechanical work on the shaft is $T \cdot \theta$**, and the remaining energy either pressurises the return-stroke oil or is dissipated.

**Reconciliation:** For a double-acting finite actuator, the **net useful work per full cycle (out + back)** is the *difference* of the two halves, because the return stroke consumes energy. For this question, the work done *during the working stroke* is $W = T \cdot \theta$:

$$\boxed{W \approx 15.3 \text{ J}}$$

(Alternative accepted answer: $W = \Delta P \cdot V = 61.26$ J if interpreted as hydraulic energy input.)

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this topic (OEAET831 Module 3):**
> 1. **Unit errors in $A_1, A_2$ calculation** — bore and rod diameters must be in **metres**, not mm, when using $F = PA$ in SI Newtons. **1 mark lost** for every wrong-unit answer.
> 2. **Forgetting that $A_2 < A_1$** — students often write $F_{ret} = F_{ext}$. This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Not stating Pascal's Law** before using $F = PA$ in a derivation — examiners award the "fundamental principle" mark only if you *name* the law.
> 4. **Forgetting the FRL block** in pneumatic circuit diagrams — **1 mark** per missing sub-unit.
> 5. **Confusing $V_D$ (displacement per rev) with cylinder volume** — the units are different. Cylinder volume $V = AL$ is in m³ for one stroke; $V_D$ is in m³/rev.
> 6. **Mixing up gear and lobe motors** — gear = meshing teeth; lobe = clearance fit, no contact. Examiners check this distinction strictly.
> 7. **Writing $T = \Delta P \cdot V_D$ without the $2\pi$ divisor** — half-marks lost.
> 8. **Skipping the unit conversion of $Q$ from L/min to m³/s** — examiners expect $Q$ in m³/s. This is a **silent, automatic 1-mark penalty** in numerical problems.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hydraulic** = incompressible oil, **high pressure, high force, accurate**.
- ✅ **Pneumatic** = compressible air, **low pressure, clean, cheap, compliant**.
- ✅ **Pascal's Law** is the *foundation* — always quote it before using $F = P \times A$.
- ✅ **Single-acting cylinder** = force in **one direction only**; return by spring/gravity/load.
- ✅ **Double-acting cylinder** = force in **both directions**; $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (cap end), $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ (rod end).
- ✅ **Always**: $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ for the same $P$, because $A_1 > A_2$. For $d/D = 0.5$, ratio is **3 : 4**.
- ✅ **Piston velocity** $v = Q / A$ — *smaller area = faster stroke*.
- ✅ **Cylinder power** $P = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ — same numerically, but $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s gives **Watts**.
- ✅ **Stroke time** $t = AL / Q = V / Q$ — easy 1-mark follow-up question.
- ✅ **Rotary actuator** (any of gear / vane / lobe) torque: $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$.
- ✅ **Rotary speed**: $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 Q}{V_D}$ (ideal, no slip).
- ✅ **Gear motor** = teeth mesh, continuous rotation, dirty-fluid tolerant, slight pressure ripple.
- ✅ **Vane motor** = sliding vanes on eccentric rotor, smooth, limited or continuous rotation, low noise.
- ✅ **Lobe motor** = multi-lobe rotor with clearance fit (no metal contact), pulse-free, slow-speed, high-torque, gentle on viscous fluids.
- ✅ **Standard pressure unit in KTU problems**: **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Convert to Pa before multiplying with m².
- ✅ **Standard flow unit in KTU problems**: **L/min** — always convert to **m³/s** (divide by 60000).
- ✅ **Always include the FRL** (Filter–Regulator–Lubricator) in any pneumatic circuit block diagram.
- ✅ **Tandem cylinder** doubles force; **telescopic cylinder** multiplies stroke length; **through-rod cylinder** gives equal force both ways.
- ✅ **Favourite viva**: *Why is retraction stroke faster than extension stroke?* — because $A_2 < A_1$, and $v = Q/A$.
- ✅ **Favourite viva**: *Why is pneumatic position control poor?* — air is compressible, so it "gives" under load.
- ✅ **Favourite viva**: *Why are hydraulic presses used for forging and not pneumatic?* — incompressibility of oil gives high stiffness and no "spring-back".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Between Electrical, Pneumatic, and Hydraulic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparison Between Electrical, Pneumatic, and Hydraulic Actuators ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **actuator** is a mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic energy) into physical motion — typically linear displacement or rotary motion — thereby performing useful work in a mechatronic system. Actuators are the *muscles* of any automated machine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Actuators are classified by their **primary energy domain** into three families: **Electrical Actuators**, **Pneumatic Actuators**, and **Hydraulic Actuators**. The choice among them is governed by force/torque demand, speed, precision, environmental constraints, and cost. --- ### 1.2 The Three Actuator Families #### A. Electrical Actuators A device that converts **electrical energy** into mechanical energy via electromagnetic principles. **Common types:** - **DC Servo Motors** – closed-loop position/velocity control - **Stepper Motors** – open-loop discrete angular steps - **AC Induction / Synchronous Motors** – high-speed industrial drives - **Solenoids** – short-stroke linear push/pull action - **Piezoelectric Actuators** – nanometer-precision motion > [!NOTE] > **Standard metrics:** Operating voltage ranges from **5 V DC** (small motors) to **480 V AC** (industrial drives). Power ratings: **0.1 W to 500 kW**. #### B. Pneumatic Actuators A device that converts **compressed air energy** (pneumatic energy) into mechanical motion. **Common types:** - **Single-acting cylinder** – air on one side, spring return - **Double-acting cylinder** – air on both sides, bidirectional motion - **Pneumatic rotary actuator / motor** – rotary output - **Muscle (bellows) actuators** – flexible contraction > [!NOTE] > **Standard metrics:** Working pressure typically **4 bar to 8 bar** (commonly **6 bar = 600 kPa**). Force range: **10 N to 50,000 N**. #### C. Hydraulic Actuators A device that converts **pressurized fluid energy** (usually oil) into mechanical motion. **Common types:** - **Single-rod / Double-rod hydraulic cylinder** – linear force - **Hydraulic rotary motor** – rotary torque - **Hydraulic vane / gear / piston pump** – energy supply > [!NOTE] > **Standard metrics:** Working pressure typically **70 bar to 350 bar** (commonly **200 bar = 20 MPa**). Force range: **1,000 N to 2,000,000 N**. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body Builder" Model Imagine three workers at a construction site, all asked to lift and move a heavy load: | Worker | Energy Source | Real-World Match | |--------|---------------|------------------| | 🏃 **Electrical Worker** | Plugs into a wall socket — clean, fast, precise, but moderate muscle | Electrical Actuator (Servo/Stepper) | | 💨 **Pneumatic Worker** | Uses a canister of compressed air — quick bursts, light-to-moderate force, lightweight | Pneumatic Actuator (Cylinder) | | 🛢️ **Hydraulic Worker** | Uses a high-pressure oil pump — extremely powerful, slow, heavy, messy | Hydraulic Actuator (Cylinder) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** Electrical = **Precision & Control**; Pneumatic = **Speed & Simplicity**; Hydraulic = **Raw Power & Force Density**. --- ### 1.4 Why This Comparison Matters (KTU Significance) In KTU board examinations and real industrial design, you must justify the selection of an actuator. The comparison is the *backbone* of any mechatronic design problem, especially in: - Robotic arm selection - CNC machine tool drives - Automotive ABS / power steering - Packaging and bottling industries - Aircraft flight control surfaces - Injection moulding machines > [!TIP] > **Memorize this golden rule:** *"If the task needs brain → Electrical; If it needs speed → Pneumatic; If it needs muscle → Hydraulic."* ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principles (Step-by-Step Logic) #### Step 1 — Electrical Actuator: Electromagnetic Conversion 1. A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force (**Lorentz Force**). 2. The interaction between stator (fixed) and rotor (moving) fields produces a continuous torque. 3. In stepper motors, the rotor aligns with discrete stator poles, producing angular steps (typically **1.8°** per step). 4. In servo motors, an **encoder** provides feedback; a closed-loop controller (PID) drives the motor to the demanded position. #### Step 2 — Pneumatic Actuator: Compressed Air Expansion 1. Atmospheric air is compressed by an external compressor and stored at **6–8 bar**. 2. The compressed air is fed into a cylinder chamber through **directional control valves (DCV)**. 3. Air pressure acting on the piston area generates a linear force that pushes the piston rod outward. 4. Exhaust air is vented to the atmosphere; in double-acting cylinders, supply/exhaust alternates to produce bidirectional motion. #### Step 3 — Hydraulic Actuator: Fluid Pressure Transmission 1. A hydraulic pump draws oil from a reservoir and pressurizes it to **70–350 bar**. 2. The pressurized oil is routed through **servo/proportional valves** into the cylinder chamber. 3. Since hydraulic oil is essentially **incompressible**, the system is stiff, precise, and supports very high loads. 4. Flow rate **Q** controls speed; pressure **P** controls force — they are **decoupled** (a major advantage). --- ### 2.2 The Fundamental Force / Torque Equations #### For Pneumatic & Hydraulic Linear Actuators The thrust force produced by a piston is governed by the simple Pascal relationship: $$F = P \times A$$ Where: - $F$ = Output force (in Newtons, **N**) - $P$ = Fluid/Air pressure (in Pascals, **Pa** or **N/m²**) - $A$ = Effective piston area (in m²) For a double-rod cylinder, the same area is used on both strokes. For a single-rod cylinder, the rod-side area is reduced: $$A_{rod\text{-}side} = A_{piston} - A_{rod}$$ #### For Electrical Rotary Actuators $$T = k_t \times I \quad \text{and} \quad \omega = \frac{V - I \times R}{k_e}$$ Where: - $T$ = Motor torque (N·m) - $k_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A) - $I$ = Armature current (A) - $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) - $V$ = Applied voltage (V) - $R$ = Armature resistance (Ω) - $k_e$ = Back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) > [!NOTE] > In SI units, $k_t$ (in N·m/A) numerically equals $k_e$ (in V·s/rad) when the motor is well-designed. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Electrical Actuator** | **Pneumatic Actuator** | **Hydraulic Actuator** | |---------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Energy form** | Electrical (V, I) | Compressed air (P) | Pressurized oil (P, Q) | | **Output (linear)** | $F = T/r$ (via leadscrew) | $F = P \times A$ | $F = P \times A$ | | **Output (rotary)** | $T = k_t \times I$ | $T = \Delta P \times D_{disp}$ | $T = \Delta P \times D_m / 2\pi$ | | **Output power** | $P = V \times I = T \times \omega$ | $P = P_{air} \times Q$ | $P = \Delta P \times Q$ | | **Output speed** | $\omega = (V - IR)/k_e$ | $v = Q/A$ | $v = Q/A$ | | **Energy efficiency** | **High (70–95%)** | **Low (10–30%)** | **Medium (40–70%)** | | **Typical pressure/voltage** | 12 V – 480 V | 4 – 8 bar | 70 – 350 bar | | **Force density (N/kg)** | Moderate | Low | **Very High** | | **Stiffness (load rejection)** | Medium | **Very Low** (air compressible) | **Very High** (oil stiff) | | **Control precision** | **Excellent (sub-µm with piezo)** | Poor (stick-slip) | Good (with servo valve) | | **Operating temperature** | -20°C to 150°C | 0°C to 80°C | 0°C to 120°C | | **Noise level** | Low | **Very High (90–110 dB)** | Medium (70–85 dB) | | **Cleanliness** | Clean | Clean | **Dirty (oil leaks)** | | **Initial cost** | Medium | **Low** | **High** | | **Maintenance** | Low | Medium (filter, lubricator) | **High (seals, filters)** | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Welding robots use **servo motors** (electrical) for path control; clamping fixtures use **pneumatic cylinders** for fast grip/release; press machines use **hydraulic cylinders** for 500-tonne stamping. - **Aircraft:** Flight control surfaces use **hydraulic actuators** (high force, fail-safe); trim motors use **electrical actuators**; cabin door locks use **pneumatic actuators** (lightweight, simple). - **Food & Pharma Packaging:** **Pneumatic systems** dominate because they are clean, fast, and explosion-proof. - **CNC & Machine Tools:** **Servo motors with leadscrews** or direct-drive torque motors for precision; **hydraulic** for heavy-duty roughing. > [!IMPORTANT] > The "Power Density" trend in modern mechatronics is shifting toward **electromechanical actuators (EMA)** because they combine the cleanliness of electrical systems with the force density approaching hydraulics — at the cost of higher controller complexity. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Generated by a Pneumatic Cylinder **Problem context:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 50$ mm and a rod diameter $d = 20$ mm. Supply pressure is $P = 6$ bar. Find the extension force and the retraction force. **Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units.** $$P = 6 \text{ bar} \times 100{,}000 \text{ Pa/bar} = 600{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the full piston area (extension side).** $$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0025)}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute the extension force (full area side pressurized).** $$F_{ext} = P \times A_{piston} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the rod-side area (retraction side).** $$A_{rod} = A_{piston} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} - \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4}$$ $$A_{rod} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} - 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 1.6494 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Compute the retraction force (rod side pressurized).** $$F_{ret} = P \times A_{rod} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.6494 \times 10^{-3}) = 989.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the force ratio.** $$\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{1178.1}{989.6} = 1.190 \approx \frac{1}{1 - (d/D)^2}$$ $$\frac{1}{1 - (20/50)^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.16} = \frac{1}{0.84} = 1.190 \;\checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** The extension force is always greater than the retraction force for a single-rod cylinder. Designers must size actuators for the *weaker* stroke direction. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Delivered by a Hydraulic System **Problem context:** A hydraulic cylinder with $A = 0.01$ m² receives oil at $P = 200$ bar. The flow rate is $Q = 30$ L/min. Find the linear velocity and the hydraulic power. **Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units.** $$P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Convert flow rate to SI units.** $$Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3}{60 \text{ s}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the linear velocity using continuity.** $$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01} = 0.05 \text{ m/s} = 50 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the output force.** $$F = P \times A = (2 \times 10^7) \times (0.01) = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 200 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the hydraulic (output) power.** $$P_{hyd} = F \times v = (2 \times 10^5) \times (0.05) = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Cross-verify using $P = \Delta P \times Q$.** $$P_{hyd} = (2 \times 10^7) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW} \;\checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** This $10$ kW hydraulic cylinder is approximately equivalent in power to a small electric motor — but delivers it in a more compact form because the oil is incompressible and high-pressure. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Servo Motor Torque & Speed **Problem context:** A DC servo motor has $k_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $R = 1.2$ Ω, and is supplied with $V = 48$ V. The load demands a current of $I = 4$ A. Find torque, back-EMF, and no-load speed. **Step 1 — Compute output torque.** $$T = k_t \times I = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.20 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the back-EMF voltage.** $$E_b = V - I \times R = 48 - (4 \times 1.2) = 48 - 4.8 = 43.2 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the actual (loaded) speed.** Since $k_e = k_t = 0.05$ V·s/rad: $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{k_e} = \frac{43.2}{0.05} = 864 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to RPM.** $$N = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 864 \times 9.5493 = 8250.6 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the no-load speed (for reference).** At no load, $I \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V$: $$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{k_e} = \frac{48}{0.05} = 960 \text{ rad/s} = 9173 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the output mechanical power.** $$P_{out} = T \times \omega = 0.20 \times 864 = 172.8 \text{ W}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** The drop from **9173 RPM (no-load) to 8250 RPM (loaded)** represents the speed regulation. A well-designed servo motor has good regulation (small drop). Compare to a stepper motor, which has *zero* speed regulation at constant load but loses steps if overloaded. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Selection Logic) | **Selection Criterion** | **Best Choice** | **Reason** | |------------------------|-----------------|------------| | Force > 100 kN needed | **Hydraulic** | Highest force density (oil at 200 bar) | | Sub-millimeter precision | **Electrical (Servo/Piezo)** | Closed-loop accuracy, no compressibility | | Explosive / dusty environment | **Pneumatic** | No sparks, no oil leaks | | Cleanroom / medical use | **Electrical** | No air exhaust, no oil | | Fastest cycling (>60 cycles/min) | **Pneumatic** | Quick valve response, light components | | Battery / portable operation | **Electrical** | Highest energy efficiency, no compressor | | Continuous rotary motion > 10 kW | **Electrical (AC motor)** | Efficient, mature technology | | Position hold without power | **Hydraulic** | Oil is naturally locked; also stepper (detent torque) | | Lowest initial cost | **Pneumatic** | Compressors and cylinders are inexpensive | | Minimum maintenance | **Electrical** | No seals, no filters, no fluids | > [!TIP] > **Hybrid systems** are common in industry: e.g., a hydraulic press driven by a servo-motor-controlled pump — combining the precision of electrical with the power of hydraulic (called **electro-hydraulic servo systems**). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Actuator Position in a Mechatronic System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
Sensor] --> B[Controller
Microcontroller PLC] B --> C[Signal Conditioner
Driver Amplifier] C --> D[Actuator
Electrical Pneumatic Hydraulic] D --> E[Mechanical Load
Robot Joint Press Clamp] E --> F[Process Output
Motion Force Torque] F -. feedback .-> A ``` --- ### 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start Selection] --> q1{Need
precision?} q1 -- Yes --> q2{Portable
battery?} q1 -- No --> q3{Force
over 100 kN?} q2 -- Yes --> elec1[Electrical Servo Motor] q2 -- No --> elec2[Electrical Stepper or AC Drive] q3 -- Yes --> hyd[Hydraulic Cylinder or Motor] q3 -- No --> q4{Need high
speed cycling?} q4 -- Yes --> pneu[Pneumatic Cylinder] q4 -- No --> q5{Hazardous
explosive area?} q5 -- Yes --> pneu q5 -- No --> q6{Budget
limited?} q6 -- Yes --> pneu q6 -- No --> elec1 ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Architecture — Hydraulic Servo System ```mermaid flowchart LR sub1[Command Signal] --> sub2[Servo Valve Controller] sub2 --> sub3[Electrohydraulic Servo Valve] sub3 --> sub4[Hydraulic Cylinder] sub4 --> sub5[Piston Rod Output] sub4 -. position feedback .-> sub6[LVDT Position Sensor] sub6 --> sub2 sub7[Hydraulic Pump and Reservoir] --> sub3 ``` --- ### 4.4 Block Architecture — Pneumatic Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR p1[Compressor] --> p2[Filter Regulator Lubricator FRL] p2 --> p3[Directional Control Valve DCV] p3 --> p4[Flow Control Valve] p4 --> p5[Double Acting Cylinder] p5 --> p6[Linear Load] p5 -. position sensor .-> p7[Limit Switches] p7 --> p3 ``` --- ### 4.5 Block Architecture — Electrical Servo Drive ```mermaid flowchart LR e1[Command Position] --> e2[PID Controller] e2 --> e3[PWM Amplifier] e3 --> e4[DC Servo Motor] e4 --> e5[Mechanical Output Shaft] e4 -. encoder .-> e6[Optical Encoder] e6 --> e2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Observation for students:** The three architectures look very different physically, but the **control loop structure is identical** — command → controller → driver → actuator → load → feedback. This is the unifying principle of mechatronics (U-2 module concept re-used here). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam - Model Question 1 [3 Marks]** > **Q:** *List any four differences between pneumatic and hydraulic actuators.* **Model Answer:** | S.No | Pneumatic Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator | |------|--------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Uses compressed air as working fluid | Uses pressurized oil as working fluid | | 2 | Operating pressure is low (4–8 bar) | Operating pressure is high (70–350 bar) | | 3 | Air is compressible → low stiffness | Oil is incompressible → high stiffness | | 4 | Low force output, high speed | High force output, lower speed | | 5 | Cleaner, no leakage issues | Prone to oil leakage, fire hazard | | 6 | Requires compressor + FRL unit | Requires pump + reservoir + filter | *(Any 4 points – 3 marks)* --- > **KTU University Exam - Model Question 2 [3 Marks]** > **Q:** *Why are electrical actuators preferred in robotic applications? Mention two reasons.* **Model Answer:** 1. **High Precision & Control:** Electrical actuators (especially servo and stepper motors) offer excellent position, velocity, and torque control through closed-loop feedback (encoders/resolvers). This is essential for accurate path planning in robots. 2. **Clean & Quiet Operation:** They do not leak oil or vent air, making them suitable for cleanroom, medical, and indoor applications. 3. **Easy Integration with Digital Controllers:** Electrical signals are directly compatible with microcontrollers, PLCs, and industrial networks (EtherCAT, CAN bus) — simplifying the mechatronic control architecture. *(Any 2 well-explained points – 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** > **KTU University Exam - Model Paper [14 Marks]** > **(a)** Explain the working principle of a hydraulic actuator with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a pneumatic actuator on the basis of working pressure, force, stiffness, and application. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 35 mm. It is supplied with oil at a pressure of 150 bar. Calculate: > (i) Extension force > (ii) Retraction force > (iii) The piston velocity if the oil flow rate is 40 L/min **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Q.A (a):** **Working Principle of Hydraulic Actuator:** A hydraulic actuator converts the **pressure energy of a fluid (oil)** into mechanical motion. The main components are: - **Reservoir** – stores the hydraulic oil - **Pump** – pressurizes the oil (driven by an electric motor or engine) - **Valves** (directional, pressure, flow) – control direction, pressure, and flow - **Hydraulic cylinder** – converts pressure into linear force **Working sequence:** 1. The pump draws oil from the reservoir and delivers it at high pressure (typically 70–350 bar). 2. The pressurized oil is directed via a **directional control valve (DCV)** into one chamber of the cylinder. 3. The pressure acting on the piston area produces a linear force: $F = P \times A$. 4. The oil from the opposite chamber is returned to the reservoir. 5. Speed is controlled by the flow rate: $v = Q/A$. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | Working pressure | 4–8 bar | 70–350 bar | | Force output | Low–Medium (up to ~50 kN) | Very High (up to 2000 kN) | | Stiffness | Low (air is compressible) | Very High (oil is incompressible) | | Typical applications | Packaging, clamping, pick-and-place | Presses, injection moulding, aircraft control | *(Diagram + table + explanation – 7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A (b):** **Given:** - Bore diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$ - Rod diameter $d = 35 \text{ mm} = 0.035 \text{ m}$ - Pressure $P = 150 \text{ bar} = 150 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ - Flow rate $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **Step 1 — Compute full piston area.** $$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute rod-side area.** $$A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.080)^2 - (0.035)^2)}{4}$$ $$A_r = \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.001225)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.005175)}{4} = 4.0646 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute extension force.** $$F_{ext} = P \times A_p = (1.5 \times 10^7) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ext} = 75{,}398 \text{ N} \approx 75.4 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Compute retraction force.** $$F_{ret} = P \times A_r = (1.5 \times 10^7) \times (4.0646 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ret} = 60{,}969 \text{ N} \approx 61.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Compute piston velocity (extension).** $$v = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 132.6 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $F_{ext} = 75.4 \text{ kN}$ - (ii) $F_{ret} = 61.0 \text{ kN}$ - (iii) $v = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}$ *(extension stroke)* *(7 marks — 2 marks for area, 2 marks for forces, 2 marks for velocity, 1 mark for final units & statement)* --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **KTU University Exam - Model Paper [14 Marks]** > **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop position control system using an electrical servo motor as the actuator. List any four advantages of electrical actuators over pneumatic/hydraulic systems. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** A DC servo motor has the following specifications: Torque constant $k_t = 0.08$ N·m/A, Armature resistance $R_a = 2$ Ω, Applied voltage $V = 60$ V. The motor draws a current of 5 A under load. Calculate: > (i) Developed torque > (ii) Back-EMF > (iii) No-load speed in RPM > (iv) Output mechanical power **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Q.B (a):** **Block Diagram (refer Section 4.5):** Command position → PID controller → PWM amplifier → DC servo motor → shaft output, with encoder feedback to PID. **Working:** 1. A reference position command is given to the controller. 2. The encoder measures the actual shaft position and feeds it back. 3. The PID controller computes the error and generates a control signal. 4. The PWM amplifier drives the motor with a proportional voltage. 5. The motor rotates the shaft to reduce the error → accurate positioning. **Four Advantages of Electrical Actuators:** 1. **High Precision:** Sub-micrometer accuracy possible with closed-loop servo and piezoelectric systems. 2. **Clean Operation:** No oil leaks, no air exhaust — suitable for sterile / cleanroom environments. 3. **High Energy Efficiency:** 70–95% efficient compared to 10–30% for pneumatics. 4. **Easy Digital Interface:** Directly compatible with microcontrollers, PLCs, and IoT — enables Industry 4.0 integration. *(7 marks — 3 for diagram + explanation, 4 for advantages)* --- **Model Solution to Q.B (b):** **Given:** - $k_t = 0.08$ N·m/A - $R_a = 2$ Ω - $V = 60$ V - $I = 5$ A - (SI equivalent: $k_e = k_t = 0.08$ V·s/rad) **(i) Developed torque:** $$T = k_t \times I = 0.08 \times 5 = 0.40 \text{ N·m}$$ **(ii) Back-EMF:** $$E_b = V - I \times R_a = 60 - (5 \times 2) = 60 - 10 = 50 \text{ V}$$ **(iii) No-load speed (in rad/s):** At no load, $E_b \approx V$, so: $$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{k_e} = \frac{60}{0.08} = 750 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N_0 = \omega_0 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 750 \times 9.5493 = 7161.97 \text{ RPM} \approx 7162 \text{ RPM}$$ **(iv) Output mechanical power (under load):** $$P_{out} = T \times \omega_{loaded}$$ First, loaded speed: $$\omega_{loaded} = \frac{E_b}{k_e} = \frac{50}{0.08} = 625 \text{ rad/s}$$ Then: $$P_{out} = 0.40 \times 625 = 250 \text{ W}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $T = 0.40$ N·m - (ii) $E_b = 50$ V - (iii) $N_0 = 7162$ RPM - (iv) $P_{out} = 250$ W *(7 marks — 2 for torque, 2 for back-EMF, 2 for RPM conversion, 1 for power)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often write pressure in *bar* but plug into $F = P \times A$ without converting to **Pa**. This alone causes a 1-mark deduction and an incorrect answer. Always convert: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa**. > 2. **Forgetting Rod Area in Retraction Force:** Single-rod cylinders have *different* areas on the two strokes. Writing $F_{ext} = F_{ret}$ is a classic error. > 3. **Mixing Flow Rate Units:** Confusing **L/min with m³/s** is the most common numerical error. Memorize: **1 L/min = 1.6667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s**. > 4. **Wrong $k_t / k_e$ Relationship:** In SI units, $k_t$ (N·m/A) = $k_e$ (V·s/rad). Students often assume a different ratio. > 5. **Vague Comparison Points:** In 7-mark comparison answers, students list factors without *justification*. Always pair each point with a numerical example or application (e.g., *"Hydraulic uses 200 bar → 10× more force than pneumatic at 6 bar"*). > 6. **Missing Block Diagram in Long Answers:** A textual answer without a labeled block diagram in Q.A(a) or Q.B(a) loses **at least 2 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** ✅ **Three Actuator Families:** Electrical, Pneumatic, Hydraulic — distinguished by energy source. ✅ **Key Formulas (must memorize):** - $F = P \times A$ (linear pneumatic/hydraulic) - $v = Q / A$ (linear velocity) - $P_{out} = P \times Q = F \times v$ (hydraulic/pneumatic power) - $T = k_t \times I$ (electrical torque) - $\omega = (V - IR) / k_e$ (electrical speed) - $P_{elec} = V \times I = T \times \omega$ (electrical power) ✅ **Pressure Levels:** Pneumatic = **6 bar**; Hydraulic = **200 bar** (~33× higher). ✅ **Stiffness Order (lowest to highest):** Pneumatic < Electrical < Hydraulic. ✅ **Efficiency Order (lowest to highest):** Pneumatic < Hydraulic < Electrical. ✅ **Precision Order (lowest to highest):** Pneumatic < Hydraulic < Electrical. ✅ **Best For:** - **Electrical** → Precision robotics, CNC, cleanrooms - **Pneumatic** → High-speed packaging, hazardous zones, low cost - **Hydraulic** → Heavy presses, aircraft, lifting, force density ✅ **Common Mistake:** Assuming all three actuators use the same control signals — **electrical uses voltage/current, pneumatic/hydraulic use pressure-based valves**. ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Expect a 7-mark comparison question and a 7-mark numerical problem in the ESE. Practice the **$F = P \times A$** and **$T = k_t \times I$** numericals thoroughly. ✅ **Recent Trend:** **Electro-hydraulic and electro-pneumatic servo systems** combine benefits — high-yield topic for Part B.

topics.title: Mechanical Power Transmission Systems - Spur Gears, Bevel Gear, Planetary Gear, Belts, and Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Power Transmission Systems - Spur Gears, Bevel Gear, Planetary Gear, Belts, and Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Mechanical Power Transmission Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (OEAET831 — Module 3):** This module establishes the *physical actuation backbone* of every mechatronic product. Whether the prime mover is a DC motor, a stepper, or a servo, the rotational output must be **conditioned** (speed-reduced, torque-amplified, redirected by 90°, or distributed to multiple shafts) before it reaches the load. That conditioning is the job of the **mechanical power transmission system**.

---

## 1.1 Why Mechatronics Needs a Transmission Layer

A mechatronic system typically has four functional layers:

1. **Sensors** (perception)
2. **Controller / Microprocessor** (decision)
3. **Actuator** (motor — typically 1,000–10,000 rpm)
4. **Transmission** ← *(this module)* → **Load / End-effector**

Most industrial loads need **low speed, high torque**, which is the *opposite* of what a small electric motor produces. A transmission bridges that mismatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Gear Analogy**
> Think of a bicycle. When you climb a hill, you shift to a **low gear** (small chainring, large rear sprocket). The pedals feel "easy" but you must turn them many times to move the wheel once. That is a **high torque, low speed** transmission. When you ride downhill, you shift to a **high gear** — pedals turn the wheel almost 1:1, less torque multiplication, but you can reach high speeds. Every transmission in this module performs the same trade-off: it *trades* speed for torque (and vice versa) while keeping power roughly constant.

---

## 1.2 The Two Grand Families of Power Transmission

| Family | Members | Contact Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive (Form-Locking) Drives** | Gears, Chains, Synchronous (Timing) Belts | Teeth mesh — no slip possible | Robotics, CNC, automotive gearboxes |
| **Friction (Force-Locking) Drives** | Flat belts, V-belts, Rope drives, Friction wheels | Friction — slip possible under overload | Fans, pumps, conveyors, long-distance drives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Velocity Ratio (VR)**
> The **velocity ratio** $i$ of any transmission is the ratio of the *driver* speed to the *driven* speed. It is the central performance parameter of the entire module:
> $$i = \frac{N_{\text{driver}}}{N_{\text{driven}}} = \frac{\text{Size of driven element}}{\text{Size of driver element}}$$
> A value of $i > 1$ means **speed reduction with torque amplification**; a value of $i < 1$ means a **speed increaser**.

---

## 1.3 Overview of the Five Drives Covered

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Roadmap (Read in this order):**
> 1. **Spur Gears** — the workhorse; parallel-shaft, straight-tooth meshing.
> 2. **Bevel Gears** — redirect power through **90°** between intersecting shafts.
> 3. **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gears** — coaxial input/output, very high reduction in a small envelope.
> 4. **Belt Drives** — long-distance, low-cost, slip-protected transmission.
> 5. **Chain Drives** — positive engagement at longer distances than gears can handle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spur Gears — The Foundation

A **spur gear** is a cylindrical gear with teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation. It transmits power between **two parallel, co-planar shafts** by positive (tooth-to-tooth) engagement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Module (m)**
> The **module** $m$ is the single most important parameter of a spur gear. It is defined as the *pitch circle diameter divided by the number of teeth* and is expressed in **millimetres**. Two gears mesh correctly **only if they share the same module and the same pressure angle**.
> $$m = \frac{d}{T} \quad \text{[mm]}$$

### 2.1.1 Standard Spur-Gear Geometry (per AGMA / ISO 633 / KTU convention)

| Element | Symbol | Standard Value | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Module | $m$ | chosen by designer | $m = d/T$ |
| Number of teeth | $T$ | integer | — |
| Pitch circle diameter | $d$ | — | $d = m \cdot T$ |
| Circular pitch | $p$ | — | $p = \pi \cdot m$ |
| Addendum | $a$ | $1.0\,m$ | $a = m$ |
| Dedendum | $b$ | $1.25\,m$ | $b = 1.25\,m$ |
| Clearance | $c$ | $0.25\,m$ | $c = b - a$ |
| Tooth thickness at PCD | — | half circular pitch | $p/2$ |
| Whole depth | $h$ | $2.25\,m$ | $h = a + b$ |
| Pressure angle $\phi$ | $\phi$ | **20°** (standard) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tooth-Thickness Rule:** At the pitch circle, the tooth thickness equals the space width — both equal to $p/2$. This is what guarantees conjugate action and smooth rolling.

### 2.1.2 Velocity Ratio and Centre Distance for Spur Gears

For two meshing spur gears (gear 1 drives gear 2):

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1}$$

The **centre distance** between the two shafts is:

$$C = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$$

### 2.1.3 Law of Gearing (Fundamental Conjugate Action)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Theorem — Fundamental Law of Gearing**
> *The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing teeth must always pass through a fixed point on the line joining the two centres.* That fixed point is the **pitch point P**. The radii drawn from P to the two centres are the **pitch-circle radii**. This law is what guarantees a *constant* angular-velocity ratio throughout the engagement — and is the reason involute teeth (not simpler cycloidal teeth) are universally used.

### 2.1.4 Train-of-Gears / Compound Gear Train

When more than two gears are involved, the overall ratio is the **product of the successive ratios**, with *intermediate* shafts cancelling:

$$i_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\text{Product of driven-tooth numbers}}{\text{Product of driver-tooth numbers}} = \frac{N_1}{N_{\text{last}}}$$

---

## 2.2 Bevel Gears — Right-Angle Power Transfer

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Bevel Gear**
> A **bevel gear** is a conically-shaped gear used to transmit motion between two shafts whose axes **intersect** (most commonly at **90°**). It is the only simple gear type that can redirect rotation through 90° with positive engagement.

### 2.2.1 Pitch Cone Geometry

Each gear has a **pitch cone** of apex angle $\phi$. The two pitch cones roll on each other without slipping — exactly like two ice-cream cones touching at their tips.

| Parameter | Pinion (driver) | Gear (driven) |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch cone angle | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_2$ |
| Number of teeth | $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
| Pitch diameter | $d_1$ | $d_2$ |
| Pitch cone radius (slant length) | $R$ | $R$ |

For shafts at 90°:

$$\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 90°$$

### 2.2.2 Velocity Ratio of Bevel Gears

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bevel-Gear Velocity Ratio (KTU Key Formula)**
> $$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{\sin \phi_2}{\sin \phi_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1}$$
> For the 90° case ($\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 90°$, so $\sin \phi_2 = \cos \phi_1$):
> $$i = \frac{\cos \phi_1}{\sin \phi_1} = \cot \phi_1 = \tan \phi_2$$

### 2.2.3 Types of Bevel Gears

| Type | Description | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight bevel** | Straight teeth, simplest, noisy at high speed | Most common in KTU problems |
| **Spiral bevel** | Curved oblique teeth, quieter, can carry more load | Used in automotive differentials |
| **Mitre gear** | $T_1 = T_2$, $\phi_1 = \phi_2 = 45°$ — pure direction change, no speed change | $i = 1$ |
| **Hypoid** | Shafts do **not** intersect; used in car rear axles | Not in KTU syllabus |

---

## 2.3 Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Trains

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Epicyclic / Planetary Gear Train**
> An **epicyclic gear train** has one or more gears (called *planets*) revolving around a central *sun* gear, all carried on a common *arm* (also called *carrier* or *planet carrier*). It achieves **very large speed reductions in a small coaxial envelope** and is the heart of automatic-transmission gearboxes and robot joint reducers (harmonic drives are an extreme form).

### 2.3.1 Nomenclature

| Element | Symbol | Common KTU Labels |
|---|---|---|
| Sun gear | $S$ | Central gear |
| Ring (annulus) gear | $R$ | Internal tooth gear |
| Planet gears | $P$ | 3 or 4 carried on the arm |
| Arm / Carrier | $A$ | $C$ or $b$ |
| Number of teeth on each | $T_S,\;T_R,\;T_P$ | — |

### 2.3.2 The Tabular (Algebraic) Method — *KTU Standard*

This is the **method KTU examiners expect**.

**Step 1.** Hold the arm *stationary* ($N_A = 0$). Compute the speed of every gear in this "fixed-arm" world using simple meshing relations.

**Step 2.** Subtract the arm speed from every speed found in Step 1, expressing the result as the *relative* speed of each gear with respect to the arm.

**Step 3.** Use the relative speeds to write the *meshing condition* between the two gears of interest. Solve for $N_A$.

**Step 4.** Substitute $N_A$ back to find the desired output speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Train Value" Shortcut**
> For a single-stage simple epicyclic train with sun $S$, arm $A$ and ring $R$:
> $$\text{Train Value} = \frac{N_R - N_A}{N_S - N_A} = \left(-\frac{T_S}{T_R}\right)^{\text{sign}}$$
> The negative sign is used when the *external* mesh (sun–planet) gives a reversal; the ring is internal and gives no reversal. A quick table is the cleanest KTU method.

### 2.3.3 Common Planetary Configurations & Their Ratios

| Configuration | Input | Output | Fixed | Speed Ratio $N_{\text{in}}/N_{\text{out}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple reduction | Sun $S$ | Arm $A$ | Ring $R$ | $1 + T_R/T_S$ |
| Reverse reduction | Ring $R$ | Arm $A$ | Sun $S$ | $1 + T_S/T_R$ |
| Overdrive | Sun $S$ | Ring $R$ | Arm $A$ | $-\,T_R/T_S$ |
| Direct coupled (lock-up) | — | — | $S$ and $R$ locked | $1:1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A typical automatic-transmission simple planetary has $T_S = 30$, $T_R = 90$, so the simple-reduction ratio is $1 + 90/30 = 4:1$. Compact and powerful.

### 2.3.4 Real-World Utility

* **Robotic joints** (harmonic-drive is a special epicyclic with $i$ up to 320:1).
* **Automatic car gearboxes** (Ravigneaux and Simpson sets).
* **Wind-turbine yaw drives** — the high-ratio epicyclic turns a fast motor into a slow, huge-torque yaw motion.
* **Mixer grinder / washing-machine gearboxes** in your home — they are planetary.

---

## 2.4 Belt Drives

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Belt Drive**
> A belt drive uses a *flexible continuous loop* running over two pulleys. Power is transmitted by **friction** between the belt and the pulley surfaces (form-locking synchronous belts are the exception).

### 2.4.1 Types of Belts

| Type | Cross-section | Key property | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat belt** | Rectangular | High speed, low power, can run on crowned pulleys | Old line shafts, sewing machines |
| **V-belt** | Trapezoidal | Wedges into groove — multiplies normal force → higher friction | Automotive accessories, industrial motors |
| **Timing (toothed / synchronous) belt** | Toothed | Positive engagement — no slip | CNC, camshaft drives, 3D printers |
| **Round belt** | Circular | Very small drives, twisting drives | Tape decks, small instruments |
| **Poly-vee / ribbed** | Multiple V ribs | Compact, flexible | Washing machines, power tools |

### 2.4.2 Belt Geometry

Let $d_1, d_2$ be the pulley pitch diameters and $C$ the centre distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Belt Length Equation (KTU must-know)**
> $$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossed-Belt Length Equation (KTU must-know)**
> $$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_2 + d_1)^2}{4C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belt Velocity & Velocity Ratio (KTU must-know)**
> $$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi d_2 N_2}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$
> $$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} \quad \text{(open belt, no slip)}$$

### 2.4.3 Belt Tensions — Euler's Eytelwein Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Theorem — Capstan / Belt-Friction Equation**
> For a belt on the verge of slipping on a pulley, the tensions on the tight and slack sides follow:
> $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$
> where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $\theta$ is the *angle of wrap* (contact angle) in **radians**. For a 180° wrap, $\theta = \pi$ rad.

For an **open belt** (both pulleys external):

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{2C}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = \pi + 2\alpha, \; \theta_2 = \pi - 2\alpha$$

For a **crossed belt**:

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d_1 + d_2}{2C}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha$$

### 2.4.4 Centrifugal Tension — *Always subtract*

When the belt moves at high speed, an *additional* outward tension is created:

$$T_c = m v^2 \quad \text{[N]}$$

where $m$ is the belt mass per unit length (kg/m). The **net effective tensions** on both tight and slack sides become $T_1 - T_c$ and $T_2 - T_c$.

### 2.4.5 Power Transmitted by a Belt

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Equation (KTU Key Formula)**
> $$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v \quad \text{[W]}$$

---

## 2.5 Chain Drives

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Chain Drive**
> A **chain drive** transmits power by *positive engagement* between the chain links (pins & rollers) and the sprocket teeth. It combines the **positive engagement of gears** with the **long centre-distance capability of belts** — the engineering compromise between the two.

### 2.5.1 Types of Chains

| Type | Use | KTU relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Roller chain** (the standard) | Bicycles, motorcycles, conveyors | Most-asked |
| **Silent chain** (inverted-tooth) | Automotive timing (older) | Mentioned |
| **Leaf chain** | Forklifts, lifting | Out of scope |
| **Double-pitch roller chain** | Low-speed conveyors | Mentioned |

### 2.5.2 Geometry and Velocity Ratio

The chain is sized in *pitches* $p$ (the centre-to-centre distance of adjacent pins). The sprocket pitch diameter is:

$$D = \frac{p}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{180°}{T}\right)}$$

A *very* good approximation (used in KTU problems):

$$D \approx \frac{p\,T}{\pi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chain Velocity Ratio (KTU Key Formula)**
> $$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$
> Note that the velocity ratio is **fixed by the tooth counts**, exactly as for spur gears — *no slip can occur* — but the chain can run over much longer centre distances.

### 2.5.3 Chain Length (in pitches)

$$L_p = \frac{2C}{p} + \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + \frac{p\,(T_2 - T_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C}$$

(Length must be **rounded up to the nearest even integer** in pitches — chains are made in even-pitch links.)

---

## 2.6 Comparative Summary — *When to use what?*

| Parameter | Spur Gear | Bevel Gear | Planetary | Belt | Chain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shafts | Parallel | Intersecting (90°) | Coaxial | Parallel/long | Parallel/long |
| Positive? | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (except timing) | Yes |
| Slip possible? | No | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Max centre distance | Small | Small | Very small | **Very large** | Large |
| Efficiency | 98–99% per mesh | 97–98% per mesh | 95–97% | 95–98% | 97–98% |
| Noise | Low–Med | Med | Med | Low | Med |
| Needs lubrication? | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes (sparingly) |
| Typical app | Gearbox, robotics | Differential, drill | Auto gearbox, robot joint | Fan, conveyor | Motorcycle, bike |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** *Belts* for cheap, quiet, long-distance, overload-safe drives; *chains* when you need positive drive with long distance; *gears* when shafts are close and you need precision and high efficiency.
