# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note for KTU 2024:** Every derivation below is worked out in the *exact* algebraic sequence that KTU examiners use when marking a 7-mark numerical. Mastering these derivations guarantees marks in Part B.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Centre Distance and Module of Two Meshing Spur Gears

**Given:** Pinion has $T_1$ teeth at module $m_1$; gear has $T_2$ teeth at module $m_2$. For correct meshing $m_1 = m_2 = m$.

**Step 1** — Write the pitch-circle diameters.

$$d_1 = m \cdot T_1 \qquad d_2 = m \cdot T_2$$

**Step 2** — For two external gears in mesh, the centre distance is the sum of the two pitch radii:

$$C = \frac{d_1}{2} + \frac{d_2}{2}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute:

$$C = \frac{m \cdot T_1}{2} + \frac{m \cdot T_2}{2} = \frac{m}{2}\,(T_1 + T_2)$$

$$\boxed{\,C = \dfrac{m}{2}\,(T_1 + T_2)\,}$$

**Step 4** — Solve for the module (the form asked in most KTU problems):

$$m = \frac{2C}{T_1 + T_2}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 60$, $C = 80$ mm → $m = 160/80 = 2$ mm. Then $d_1 = 40$ mm, $d_2 = 120$ mm, and $C = 40/2 + 120/2 = 20 + 60 = 80$ mm ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Velocity Ratio of a Compound (Reverted) Gear Train

A **reverted gear train** has an intermediate shaft carrying *two* gears of different sizes; it is the standard KTU 14-mark setup.

**Setup:**

* Shaft 1 (input) carries gear $A$ with $T_A$ teeth.
* Shaft 2 (intermediate) carries gear $B$ meshing with $A$, and gear $C$ on the same shaft, with $T_C$ teeth.
* Shaft 3 (output) carries gear $D$ meshing with $C$, with $T_D$ teeth.
* $A$–$B$ pair: centre distance $C_1$; $C$–$D$ pair: centre distance $C_2$.
* For a *reverted* train, the input and output shafts are **coaxial**, so $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

**Step 1** — First mesh velocity ratio:

$$i_1 = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_B}{T_A}$$

**Step 2** — Second mesh velocity ratio (note: $B$ and $C$ are on the same shaft, so $N_C = N_B$):

$$i_2 = \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{T_D}{T_C}$$

**Step 3** — Multiply the two:

$$i_{\text{total}} = i_1 \cdot i_2 = \frac{T_B}{T_A} \cdot \frac{T_D}{T_C}$$

$$\boxed{\,i_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C}\,}$$

**Step 4** — Apply the *reverted* condition. Both pairs share the same centre distance $C$, and they share the same module $m$ (else the teeth would not be the same size on the same shaft physically, so for KTU $m$ is constant):

$$C = \frac{m}{2}(T_A + T_B) = \frac{m}{2}(T_C + T_D) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_A + T_B = T_C + T_D$$

This single equation lets you solve for any one unknown tooth count once the others are fixed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Planetary Gear Train (Tabular Method) on a Classic KTU 14-Mark Problem

**Problem:** Sun has 40 teeth, ring has 80 teeth, arm input at 100 rpm clockwise, ring fixed. Find the speed of the sun.

**Step 1 — Add a column header.** We will tabulate speeds of every element in two views: *world frame* and *arm frame* (relative).

| Element | Step 1: $N_A = 0$ | Step 2: $N_A$ added | Step 3: Rel. to arm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arm $A$ (fixed) | $0$ | $0 + N_A = N_A$ | $0$ |
| Sun $S$ | $x$ | $x + N_A$ | $x$ |
| Ring $R$ (fixed) | $0$ | $0 + N_A = N_A$ | $0$ |
| Planet $P$ | — | — | — |

**Step 2 — Find the relative speed of the sun when the arm is held still.**
With the arm locked, the gear train is a *simple* mesh between $S$ and $R$ through $P$. The ring is fixed ($N_R = 0$). The internal mesh (planet–ring) gives *no* reversal, the external mesh (sun–planet) gives one reversal:

$$\frac{N_S - N_A}{N_R - N_A} = -\,\frac{T_R}{T_S} \quad \text{(sign convention)}$$

Plugging in:

$$\frac{N_S - 100}{0 - 100} = -\,\frac{80}{40} = -2$$

$$N_S - 100 = -2 \cdot (-100) = +200$$

$$\boxed{\,N_S = 300 \text{ rpm (same direction as arm)}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the train-value formula directly:**

$$\text{Train Value} = \frac{N_S - N_A}{N_R - N_A} = 1 + \frac{T_R}{T_S} = 1 + \frac{80}{40} = 3$$

$$\Rightarrow N_S - N_A = 3 (N_R - N_A) = 3(0 - 100) = -300$$

$$N_S = N_A - 300 = 100 - 300 = -200 \text{ rpm}$$

(The minus sign here is the *sign convention* of the tabular method; magnitudes match, 200:100 = 2:1, ✓.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Open-Belt Length Equation

**Geometry:** Two pulleys of pitch diameters $d_1, d_2$ with centre distance $C$. The belt touches each pulley over an arc and runs as two straight tangents between them. By symmetry, the two tangents are equal in length; let each tangent be of length $L_t$.

**Step 1 — Wrap angle geometry.** The line joining the centres makes an angle $\alpha$ with the perpendicular bisector of the tangent line. With the help of right-triangle geometry:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{2C}$$

**Step 2 — Length of one straight portion.** From the geometry, the length of each straight run is:

$$L_t = C \cos \alpha = \sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Length of arc on each pulley.** The smaller pulley has a wrap of $\pi - 2\alpha$ rad (radians of belt contact), the larger $\pi + 2\alpha$:

$$L_{a1} = \frac{d_1}{2}(\pi - 2\alpha) \qquad L_{a2} = \frac{d_2}{2}(\pi + 2\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Total length.** Add the two straights and the two arcs:

$$L = 2L_t + L_{a1} + L_{a2}$$

$$L = 2\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{d_2-d_1}{2}\right)^2} + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{d_2 - d_1}{2} \cdot 2\alpha$$

For *long* centre distances, $\alpha$ is small and $\cos \alpha \approx 1$. Expanding with the binomial series and dropping higher-order terms gives the **standard KTU open-belt formula**:

$$\boxed{\,L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Crossed-belt version.** The only changes are (i) both pulleys now have the *same* wrap angle of $\pi + 2\alpha$, (ii) the difference $d_2 - d_1$ becomes a sum inside the last term, giving:

$$\boxed{\,L_{\text{crossed}} = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \dfrac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked numerical (KTU pattern, 7 marks).**
> Open belt, $d_1 = 200$ mm, $d_2 = 600$ mm, $C = 1500$ mm.
>
> $L = 2(1500) + (\pi/2)(800) + (400)^2/(4 \cdot 1500)$
>
> $L = 3000 + 1256.64 + 26.67$
>
> $\boxed{L = 4283.3 \text{ mm}}$

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Power Transmitted by a Belt

**Step 1 — Friction-limited tension ratio.**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

**Step 2 — Centrifugal tension subtracts equally from both sides** (it does no work):

$$T_{\text{net tight}} = T_1 - T_c, \qquad T_{\text{net slack}} = T_2 - T_c$$

**Step 3 — Net driving force** (this is what does useful work on the driven pulley):

$$F_{\text{net}} = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = T_1 - T_2$$

**Step 4 — Power** is force × velocity:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\, v = (T_1 - T_2)\, \frac{\pi d N}{60}$$

For a V-belt, the contact is *not* simply on the bottom of the groove — the wedging action multiplies the friction force by a factor $1/\sin(\beta/2)$, where $\beta$ is the groove angle. So the V-belt tension ratio is:

$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin(\beta/2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why V-belts grip harder:** For a typical 40° groove, $1/\sin(20°) \approx 2.92$. The belt "sees" an *effective* wrap of nearly 3× the geometric wrap — that's why a tiny V-belt can transmit huge power.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Chain Length in Pitches

**Step 1 — Linear chain length ≈** 2 × straight-run length + 2 × arc on sprockets.

**Step 2 — The two straight portions together cover a length of $2C$ exactly** (for an open chain drive, ignoring catenary sag).

**Step 3 — Each sprocket wraps half its pitch circumference** (chain enters and leaves tangentially):

$$L_{\text{arc, total}} = \frac{p}{2}(T_1 + T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Correction for the *sag-catenary* on long centres** (small term):

$$\Delta L \approx \frac{p\,(T_2 - T_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to number of pitches** by dividing by $p$:

$$\boxed{\,L_p = \dfrac{2C}{p} + \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + \dfrac{p\,(T_2 - T_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical (KTU pattern, 7 marks).**
> $T_1 = 17$ teeth, $T_2 = 51$ teeth, $p = 12.7$ mm (½-inch standard), $C = 500$ mm.
>
> $L_p = 2(500)/12.7 + (17+51)/2 + (12.7 \times 34^2)/(4\pi^2 \times 500)$
>
> $L_p = 78.74 + 34 + (12.7 \times 1156)/(19\,739)$
>
> $L_p = 78.74 + 34 + 0.744 = 113.48$
>
> Round up to next even integer: $L_p = 114$ pitches (i.e. 57 pairs of inner/outer links).

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — *Mapping every parameter of this module*

| Parameter | Spur | Bevel | Planetary | Belt | Chain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity ratio formula | $T_2/T_1$ | $\sin\phi_2/\sin\phi_1$ | Tabular method | $d_2/d_1$ (open) | $T_2/T_1$ |
| Centre distance | $\tfrac{m}{2}(T_1+T_2)$ | $R(\sin\phi_1+\sin\phi_2)$ | $\tfrac{m}{2}(T_S+T_R)$ | $C$ (chosen) | $C$ (chosen) |
| Slip possible? | No | No | No | Yes | No |
| Slip-affected equation | — | — | — | $v_1 \ne v_2$ | — |
| Length equation | — | — | — | Open / Crossed | $L_p$ formula above |
| Power equation | $P = T\omega$ | $P = T\omega$ | $P = T\omega$ | $(T_1-T_2)v$ | $P = T\omega$ |
| Critical governing law | Law of gearing | Pitch cones roll | Tabular method | Eytelwein | Mesh constraint |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark distribution per KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3):** Part A contributes 10 marks (3 + 3 + 4 short answers); Part B contributes 14 marks (single 14-mark question with internal choice of TWO alternatives). The questions below are tuned to those exact weightings.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "module" of a spur gear. Why must two meshing gears have the same module?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **module** $m$ of a spur gear is the ratio of its pitch-circle diameter $d$ (in mm) to the number of teeth $T$:

$$m = \frac{d}{T} \quad \text{[mm]}$$

Two meshing gears must have the *same module* because (1) otherwise their circular pitches $p = \pi m$ would differ, leading to tooth-thickness mismatch, and (2) the common module ensures that the *addendum* and *dedendum* of one gear engage correctly with the tooth space of the other, producing a constant velocity ratio (law of gearing). *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the two reasons.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between an open belt drive and a crossed belt drive. State the velocity ratio in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open Belt | Crossed Belt |
|---|---|---|
| Belt layout | Pulleys rotate in **same** direction | Pulleys rotate in **opposite** directions |
| Wrap on smaller pulley | $< 180°$ | $> 180°$ |
| Length formula | $2C + \tfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \tfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$ | $2C + \tfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \tfrac{(d_1+d_2)^2}{4C}$ |
| Velocity ratio | $i = d_2/d_1$ | $i = d_2/d_1$ (same formula, *opposite rotation*) |

*(1 mark each for the table rows. Both have the same VR formula but opposite rotation sense.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** *Either (a) OR (b)* style — answer **any ONE full question** of 14 marks. Below are TWO complete, independent 14-mark question papers (A and B). Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ❑ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**A(a) [7 marks].** A pair of spur gears in external mesh consists of a pinion with 18 teeth and a gear with 72 teeth. The module is 4 mm. Calculate:
(i) the pitch-circle diameters of both gears,
(ii) the centre distance,
(iii) the addendum, dedendum, and circular pitch,
(iv) the velocity ratio when the pinion runs at 1440 rpm.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pitch-circle diameters** (each 1 mark):

$$d_1 = m \cdot T_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_2 = m \cdot T_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Centre distance** (1 mark):

$$C = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{72 + 288}{2} = 180 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Standard proportions** (3 × 0.5 marks):

$$\text{Addendum } a = 1.0\,m = 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Dedendum } b = 1.25\,m = 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Circular pitch } p = \pi\,m = 4\pi = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Velocity ratio** (1 mark):

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{72}{18} = 4$$

Output speed:

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{1440}{4} = 360 \text{ rpm}$$

**A(b) [7 marks].** A crossed-belt drive connects two pulleys of diameters 300 mm and 600 mm with a centre distance of 2 m. Find (i) the length of the belt, (ii) the power transmitted if the belt mass is 0.6 kg/m, runs at 12 m/s, the tight-side tension is 1500 N, the slack-side tension is 600 N, and the angle of wrap is $\pi$ rad on both pulleys.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Belt length** — Crossed-belt formula (3 marks):

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(2000) + \frac{\pi}{2}(300 + 600) + \frac{(300 + 600)^2}{4 \times 2000}$$

$$L = 4000 + \frac{\pi \times 900}{2} + \frac{900^2}{8000}$$

$$L = 4000 + 1413.72 + 101.25$$

$$\boxed{L = 5514.97 \text{ mm} \approx 5.515 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Power transmitted** (2 marks):

Centrifugal tension: $T_c = m v^2 = 0.6 \times 12^2 = 86.4$ N.

Net driving tension: $T_1 - T_2 = 1500 - 600 = 900$ N.

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v = 900 \times 12 = 10\,800 \text{ W} = 10.8 \text{ kW}$$

Friction coefficient check (optional, 1 mark): From $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ for ideal no-centrifugal case — not required since tensions are given directly.

---

### ❑ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**B(a) [7 marks].** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **planetary (epicyclic) gear train**. A simple epicyclic train has a sun gear of 30 teeth, three identical planet gears of 20 teeth each, and a ring gear of 70 teeth. The arm is the input at 100 rpm clockwise and the sun is the output. Find the speed of the ring gear.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch** (2 marks): A labelled diagram showing the sun at centre, three planet gears on a carrier arm, and the ring gear (with internal teeth) surrounding them. The arm is the input shaft.

**Working explanation** (2 marks): The arm carries the planets around the sun. With the sun fixed, the planets "walk" along it, and because they mesh internally with the ring, the ring rotates in the *same* sense as the arm but at a *reduced* speed — exactly the action of an epicyclic reducer. If the sun rotates as well, the ring speed is the vector sum of these motions.

**Speed calculation — Tabular method** (3 marks):

Step 1 — Hold arm fixed. With $N_S$ unknown and $N_R$ unknown but the gear-train value:

$$\frac{N_R - N_A}{N_S - N_A} = +\,\frac{T_R}{T_S} \cdot \frac{T_P}{T_P} = \frac{70}{30} = \frac{7}{3}$$

Step 2 — Sun is the *output* and arm is the *input*, with $N_S = ?$, $N_A = 100$ rpm, $N_R = ?$. We need one more relation. Given the input is arm and output is sun, with **ring fixed**? — *Re-read problem*: It says arm is the input and sun is the output, so the **ring must be the fixed reaction member** (the standard epicyclic reducer arrangement).

So $N_R = 0$. Substitute:

$$\frac{0 - 100}{N_S - 100} = \frac{7}{3}$$

$$-300 = 7(N_S - 100)$$

$$N_S - 100 = -\frac{300}{7} = -42.86$$

$$\boxed{N_S = 57.14 \text{ rpm (clockwise)}}$$

(Note: the sun rotates *slower* than the arm, exactly as a reducer should do.)

---

**B(b) [7 marks].** A chain drive uses a roller chain of pitch 15.875 mm connecting a driver sprocket of 21 teeth to a driven sprocket of 63 teeth, with a centre distance of 800 mm. Find (i) the velocity ratio, (ii) the length of chain in pitches, (iii) the chain length in metres.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Velocity ratio** (1 mark):

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{63}{21} = 3$$

**(ii) Chain length in pitches** (4 marks):

$$L_p = \frac{2C}{p} + \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + \frac{p\,(T_2 - T_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C}$$

Substituting $C = 800$ mm, $p = 15.875$ mm, $T_1 = 21$, $T_2 = 63$:

$$\frac{2C}{p} = \frac{2 \times 800}{15.875} = 100.787$$

$$\frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = \frac{21 + 63}{2} = 42$$

$$\frac{p\,(T_2-T_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C} = \frac{15.875 \times (42)^2}{4 \times 9.8696 \times 800} = \frac{15.875 \times 1764}{31\,582.7}$$

$$= \frac{28\,003.5}{31\,582.7} = 0.887$$

$$L_p = 100.787 + 42 + 0.887 = 143.67$$

Round up to next even integer: $L_p = 144$ pitches.

**(iii) Chain length in metres** (2 marks):

$$L = L_p \times p = 144 \times 15.875 = 2286 \text{ mm} = 2.286 \text{ m}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on this module — and how to avoid them**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign in planetary tabular method.** When you add $N_A$ back, students frequently *subtract* it instead of *add*. **Mnemonic:** *"*Add the arm back, then ask what's the relative spin."* The relative column should always have the arm as 0.
> 2. **Confusing velocity ratio direction.** $i = N_{\text{driver}}/N_{\text{driven}}$, *not* the other way around. Writing $i = T_1/T_2$ when asked "ratio of driven to driver" loses 1 mark every time.
> 3. **Forgetting centrifugal tension.** In a belt power problem, examiners *always* test whether you remembered to subtract $T_c = mv^2$ from *both* tight and slack tensions before applying the Eytelwein equation. Skipping this is a guaranteed -2.
> 4. **Off-by-one tooth in chain length.** The chain-length formula gives a *real* number; the answer must be **rounded up to the next even integer of pitches**. Many students leave it as a decimal.
> 5. **No diagram on 14-mark answers.** KTU examiners routinely deduct 1–2 marks for a numerical answer with *no* supporting free-body diagram. Always draw the gear pair / pulley pair / planetary arrangement with all labels.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this and keep it for the last 24 hours before ESE)**
>
> - [x] **Module** $m = d/T$ is the gateway to *every* spur-gear quantity.
> - [x] Standard proportions: $a = m,\; b = 1.25\,m,\; c = 0.25\,m,\; h = 2.25\,m$.
> - [x] Centre distance for spur gears: $C = \tfrac{m}{2}(T_1 + T_2)$.
> - [x] Spur-gear VR: $i = T_2/T_1 = N_1/N_2 = d_2/d_1$.
> - [x] **Reverted-gear** constraint: $T_A + T_B = T_C + T_D$.
> - [x] **Bevel-gear** VR: $i = \sin\phi_2/\sin\phi_1$ and $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 90°$ for 90° shafts.
> - [x] **Planetary (epicyclic) — use the tabular method.** Steps: (1) Lock arm, (2) Add $N_A$ back, (3) Use train value, (4) Solve.
> - [x] Train value for simple epicyclic (sun + planet + ring): $(N_R - N_A)/(N_S - N_A) = +T_R/T_S$ (positive — internal mesh).
> - [x] **Open-belt length:** $L = 2C + \tfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \tfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$.
> - [x] **Crossed-belt length:** Replace $d_2 - d_1$ with $d_1 + d_2$ in the last term.
> - [x] **Belt power:** $P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$; **centrifugal tension** $T_c = mv^2$ subtracts from *both* $T_1$ and $T_2$ before applying Eytelwein.
> - [x] **Chain VR** = $T_2/T_1$ exactly like gears (no slip, but long distances allowed).
> - [x] **Chain length in pitches:** $L_p = 2C/p + (T_1+T_2)/2 + p(T_2-T_1)^2/(4\pi^2 C)$; **round up to even integer**.
> - [x] Two big families: **positive (gears, chains, timing belts)** vs **friction (flat/V belts)**.
> - [x] Always include a **labelled diagram** with your 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direction Control Valve, Pressure Control Valves, Solenoid Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET831 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direction Control Valve, Pressure Control Valves, Solenoid Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Direction Control Valve (DCV) — Formal Definition

A **Direction Control Valve (DCV)** is a fluid power component used to direct, block, or permit the flow of hydraulic oil (or compressed air in pneumatics) between the pump, actuator, and tank/reservoir by changing the position of an internal movable element (spool, poppet, or rotary disc). DCVs are characterized by two key numerical specifications: the **number of ports (ways)** and the **number of positions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A DCV is always denoted in the format **"X/Y"**, where **X = number of ports/flow paths** and **Y = number of discrete operating positions** the spool can occupy. Example: a 4/3 valve has 4 ports and 3 spool positions.

### Common DCV Variants Encountered in KTU Board Exams

| Valve Type | Common Industrial Use |
|------------|----------------------|
| **2/2** | Single-acting cylinder control, simple on/off |
| **3/2** | Single-acting pneumatic cylinder |
| **4/2** | Double-acting cylinder (most common in KTU problems) |
| **4/3** | Double-acting cylinder with center position (tandem/open/closed) |
| **5/2** | Double-acting cylinder, pneumatically piloted |
| **5/3** | Double-acting cylinder with mid-position (closed/open/tandem) |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Police" Analogy

Think of a DCV as a **traffic policeman standing at a road intersection**. The road has multiple lanes (ports: P-pump supply, T-tank return, A & B- actuator ports). The policeman can stand in different positions (2-position, 3-position) and raise his hand to allow or block specific lane connections. In the **normal/blocked position**, no traffic flows (pump → tank blocked). When actuated, he "routes" the traffic — pressure oil reaches cylinder port A, return oil exits from B back to tank — and the double-acting cylinder extends/retracts. Each spool shift = a new traffic routing configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Principle:** A DCV does **NOT regulate flow rate** or **system pressure**; it ONLY controls the **direction** of fluid flow. Pressure and flow regulation are handled by their respective dedicated valves.

---

## 1.2 Pressure Control Valves (PCV) — Formal Definition

A **Pressure Control Valve** is a fluid power component that uses a **spring-loaded poppet or spool** actuated by system hydraulic force to limit, regulate, or maintain fluid pressure within a circuit. The spring force (adjustable via a knurled knob) is compared against the hydraulic pressure force acting on a known area — when hydraulic force exceeds spring preload, the valve opens/closes to control pressure.

### Three Primary Pressure Control Valves

**(a) Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** — The "Safety Guard" of the circuit. It opens when circuit pressure exceeds a preset maximum, diverting excess flow back to tank, thereby protecting the pump, actuators, and lines from over-pressure damage.

**(b) Pressure Reducing Valve** — Used in sub-circuits where a lower, regulated pressure is needed (e.g., clamping circuit = 30 bar, while main circuit = 140 bar). It restricts flow until downstream pressure falls below the setting, then reopens to maintain it.

**(c) Sequence Valve** — A pressure-operated valve that opens a new flow path only when a preset pressure is reached. Used to sequence operations: e.g., "first clamp the workpiece, then start the cutting stroke."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Pressure Cooker" Analogy

A **Pressure Relief Valve** is exactly like the **safety whistle on a pressure cooker**. When steam pressure inside exceeds the spring-loaded weight setting, the whistle lifts and releases steam until pressure drops back to safe levels. Similarly, a PRV lifts at its set pressure and dumps oil to tank until pressure falls back to a few bar below the setting (called **blowdown** or **cracking pressure differential**).

---

## 1.3 Solenoid Valve — Formal Definition

A **Solenoid Valve** is an **electromechanically actuated valve** in which an **electrical solenoid** (a coil of wire wound around a ferromagnetic plunger/core) generates a linear magnetic force when energized with current, to shift a spool or poppet that controls fluid flow. It is the **fundamental interface between the electrical control domain and the fluid power domain** in mechatronics.

### Constructional Anatomy

> [!NOTE]
> A solenoid essentially converts **electrical energy → mechanical linear force**. The generated force shifts the valve spool against a return spring, thereby changing flow paths.

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Coil (solenoid winding)** | Carries current; produces magnetic flux |
| **Plunger / Armature** | Soft iron core pulled into coil when energized |
| **Yoke / Pole piece** | Completes magnetic circuit; concentrates flux |
| **Return spring** | Pushes plunger back when coil is de-energized |
| **Spool / Poppet** | Connected to plunger; redirects fluid |
| **Manual override (optional)** | Allows manual actuation during power failure |

### Types of Solenoid Actuation

**(i) Direct-Acting Solenoid Valve** — The solenoid force directly overcomes both the spring force AND the fluid pressure force. Used for low flow, low pressure applications (typically < 50 L/min, < 350 bar).

**(ii) Pilot-Operated Solenoid Valve** — A small pilot solenoid operates a control chamber that uses system pressure to shift the main spool. The solenoid only needs to overcome a tiny pressure differential, allowing high flow capacity. This is the **most common industrial type**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell" Analogy

A solenoid works exactly like an **electric doorbell's striker mechanism**. Press the switch (energize coil) → magnetic field pulls the iron plunger forward → plunger hits the bell/dings. Release the switch (de-energize) → spring pushes the plunger back to rest. In a solenoid valve, the plunger doesn't ring a bell — it shifts a spool that redirects fluid. The "switch" in mechatronics is the **PLC output**, the "plunger" is the **valve actuator**, and the "bell" is the **hydraulic cylinder's movement**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A solenoid valve is classified as **Normally Closed (NC)** if flow is blocked in the de-energized state, and **Normally Open (NO)** if flow passes in the de-energized state. The classification is determined by the spring's rest position.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direction Control Valve — Operating Logic

### Spool Configuration Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify ports:** P = Pump (pressure inlet), T = Tank (return), A & B = Actuator work ports.

**Step 2 — Identify positions:** Each distinct square in the ISO symbol represents one spool position. A 4/3 valve has **3 squares**, a 5/2 valve has **2 squares**, a 3/2 valve has **2 squares**.

**Step 3 — Read each square:** Inside each square, arrow lines (→ or ← or ↑) show which ports are connected in that position. A "T" (or blocked symbol) shows which ports are blocked.

**Step 4 — Identify actuation:** A triangle on the left of the symbol = spring return (passive return). A box on the right = solenoid/pilot actuation. The arrow inside the box indicates the actuation direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use 3 positions instead of 2?** A 3-position valve (4/3 or 5/3) provides a **center condition** that is critical for safety and energy conservation. For example, in a 4/3 with **closed center**, all ports are blocked at center — the cylinder is hydraulically locked (no drift). In a **tandem center**, P → T is open (pump unloads at zero pressure to tank, saving energy). In a **float center**, A → B → T (cylinder can move freely in both directions).

### DCV Actuation Methods (Exam Favourite Topic)

| Actuation Type | Symbol Convention | Application |
|----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Manual (push-button/lever)** | Push button symbol on side | Shop floor, jigs, fixtures |
| **Mechanical (cam/plunger)** | Roller/Plunger symbol | Limit-switch triggered end-position valves |
| **Solenoid (electrical)** | Square box with arrow | PLC-controlled automation (most common) |
| **Pilot (pneumatic/hydraulic)** | Dashed line box with arrow | High-flow, hazardous (explosion-proof) environments |
| **Spring return** | Triangle | Always present to provide default position |

### Solenoid-Operated 5/2 DCV — Industrial Standard

A **5/2 single solenoid DCV** is the **backbone of automated pneumatic circuits** in KTU lab experiments. It has:
- **5 ports:** 1 (pressure), 3 & 5 (exhaust), 2 & 4 (cylinder ports A & B)
- **2 positions:** Left (rest/solenoid de-energized) and Right (solenoid energized)
- **1 solenoid** for actuation + **1 spring** for return
- On energize: P → A (extend cylinder), B → exhaust
- On de-energize: spring returns, P → B (retract cylinder), A → exhaust

A **5/2 double solenoid DCV** uses two solenoids (one for each position) and a **memory detent** (no spring). The valve "remembers" its last position even after power is removed. This is critical for **fail-safe automation** where losing power should NOT cause the cylinder to move.

---

## 2.2 Pressure Control Valve — Operating Logic

### Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) — Operating Sequence

**Step 1 — At rest (low system pressure):** Spring force $F_s$ > Hydraulic force $F_h$. Poppet is firmly seated on its seat. Pump flow has no path to tank (unless a separate unloading line exists).

**Step 2 — Pressure rises:** As pump delivers flow into a blocked circuit, pressure $P$ rises, increasing $F_h = P \cdot A$ where $A$ is the poppet seat area.

**Step 3 — Cracking pressure reached:** When $F_h = F_s$, the poppet begins to lift. The pressure at which this occurs is the **cracking pressure** (or **set pressure**).

$$P_{set} = \frac{F_s}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Full flow relief:** As pressure continues to rise slightly above $P_{set}$, the poppet lifts further, dumping all pump flow $Q_{pump}$ back to tank at a **blowdown pressure** $P_{blowdown} < P_{set}$.

**Step 5 — Reset:** When downstream demand resumes, pressure falls below $P_{blowdown}$, spring reseats the poppet, and the cycle restarts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blowdown** is the pressure difference $P_{set} - P_{blowdown}$ (typically 5–15% of $P_{set}$). The percentage is called **blowdown ratio** = $\frac{P_{set} - P_{blowdown}}{P_{set}} \times 100\%$. Smaller blowdown = more precise pressure control.

### Pressure Reducing Valve — Operating Logic

A reducing valve maintains a **constant lower pressure** in a secondary (sub) circuit, regardless of higher primary pressure fluctuations.

**Step 1 — Spool is held open by spring.** Primary pressure is freely communicated to secondary circuit.

**Step 2 — Secondary pressure $P_2$ rises** to the set value. Hydraulic force under spool end = $P_2 \cdot A_{spool}$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium:** When $P_2 \cdot A_{spool} = F_{spring}$, the spool throttles the flow path.

**Step 4 — Compensation:** If $P_2$ tries to rise further (e.g., secondary actuator locks), spool shifts to further restrict — preventing overpressure in the secondary circuit. If $P_2$ falls (e.g., secondary load demands flow), spool opens more.

### Sequence Valve — Operating Logic

A sequence valve operates like a PRV but instead of dumping to tank, it **directs flow to a secondary actuator** when set pressure is reached. Used to ensure **Operation B starts only after Operation A completes**.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Industrial Clamping Application: A sequence valve set to **35 bar** is placed in a clamping cylinder's supply line. The cutting cylinder's supply (set at **140 bar**) is routed *through* the sequence valve. Until clamping pressure reaches 35 bar, the sequence valve remains closed → no oil reaches cutting cylinder → safety ensured. Once clamped, pressure rises past 35 bar → sequence valve opens → cutting stroke begins.

---

## 2.3 Solenoid Valve — Operating Logic & Magnetic Theory

### Force Generation in a Solenoid

When current $I$ flows through $N$ turns of a coil, a magnetic flux $\Phi$ is established along the coil axis. The flux density $B$ in the air gap pulls the plunger with a force given by Maxwell's magnetic stress equation:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_{gap}}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{\Phi^2}{2 \mu_0 A_{gap}}$$

where:
- $A_{gap}$ = cross-sectional area of the working air gap (m²)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (permeability of free space)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux through the gap (Wb)

The magnetic flux is given by:

$$\Phi = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}}$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the total magnetic reluctance of the iron path + air gap. Since iron reluctance is negligible compared to air gap:

$$\mathcal{R} \approx \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A_g}$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi = \frac{N I \mu_0 A_g}{l_g}$$

Substituting back:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{\mu_0 A_g}{2} \left(\frac{NI}{l_g}\right)^2$$

This is the **fundamental solenoid force equation** used by valve designers to size solenoids for required actuation force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the equation:** Solenoid force is **proportional to the square of current** and **inversely proportional to the square of air gap length**. This is why solenoids produce their **maximum pull-in force at the moment of energization** (largest flux) and **minimum holding force** at full plunger insertion (smallest air gap, force drops). Designers must ensure the **worst-case (maximum air gap) condition** is met for actuation.

### Direct-Acting vs Pilot-Operated Solenoid Valve

| Parameter | Direct-Acting | Pilot-Operated |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Solenoid shifts main spool directly? | **Yes** | **No** — solenoid only moves a tiny pilot poppet |
| Force requirement on solenoid | Must overcome full $P \cdot A_{spool}$ | Must overcome only pilot chamber pressure differential |
| Maximum flow capacity | Limited (~50 L/min) | High (>500 L/min possible) |
| Maximum operating pressure | Limited (~350 bar) | Up to **420 bar** and beyond |
| Response time | Fast | Slightly slower (pilot pressure must build/dump) |
| Cost | Lower for small valves | Higher |
| Industrial use | Instrument air, low-power circuits | Main machine tool hydraulics |

### NC vs NO Solenoid Valve Logic

A **Normally Closed (NC)** solenoid valve is **closed in the de-energized state** (spring holds poppet/spool on seat). When the coil is energized, the magnetic force lifts the element → flow passes.

A **Normally Open (NO)** solenoid valve is **open in the de-energized state**. When the coil is energized, the magnetic force pushes the element against its seat → flow is blocked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Convention:** A **3/2 NC solenoid valve** is the standard "energize-to-extend" configuration used in pneumatic single-acting cylinders. A **5/2 single solenoid** is the standard "spring return" configuration for double-acting cylinders. These two form **>70% of KTU pneumatic lab circuit questions**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | DCV notation | $X/Y$ | dimensionless | X = ports, Y = positions |
| 2 | Cracking pressure of PRV | $P_{set} = F_s / A_{poppet}$ | **bar (Pa)** | $F_s$ = spring preload, $A$ = seat area |
| 3 | Blowdown ratio | $\frac{P_{set} - P_{blowdown}}{P_{set}} \times 100$ | % | Typically 5–15% |
| 4 | Sequence valve opening condition | $P_{inlet} \geq P_{set}$ | bar | Triggers secondary flow path |
| 5 | Solenoid magnetic force (max) | $F_{mag} = \frac{\mu_0 A_g}{2}\left(\frac{NI}{l_g}\right)^2$ | **Newton (N)** | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| 6 | Magnetic flux through coil | $\Phi = \frac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Weber (Wb) | $\mathcal{R}$ = reluctance |
| 7 | Air gap reluctance | $\mathcal{R} \approx l_g / (\mu_0 A_g)$ | A-t/Wb | $l_g$ = gap length |
| 8 | Hydraulic force on poppet | $F_h = P \cdot A$ | Newton (N) | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 9 | Pump flow to tank (relief condition) | $Q_{pump}$ all bypasses actuator | L/min | When $P \geq P_{set}$ |
| 10 | Solenoid DC coil resistance | $R = V / I$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | Determines current draw |
| 11 | Solenoid power dissipation | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I = I^2 R$ | Watt (W) | Determines coil heating |
| 12 | Solenoid valve response time | $t_r \approx L/R$ (electrical) + pilot delay | ms (millisecond) | For DC solenoids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility of these formulas:** These formulas are used by **Bosch Rexroth, Parker Hannifin, Festo, SMC, and Yuken** design engineers to size pressure valves for hydraulic presses (up to 700 bar) and to design energy-efficient solenoid coils (warm-up time, duty cycle). The cracking pressure formula is a daily tool for hydraulic system commissioning engineers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solenoid Pull-In Force as a Function of Plunger Position

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU mechatronics for solenoid valves. It proves why solenoid force is **maximum at start of stroke** and decreases as the plunger travels.

### Starting Point: Energy Conservation

When the plunger moves a small distance $dx$ into the coil, the magnetic stored energy in the air gap changes. The mechanical work done equals the change in magnetic co-energy:

$$F_{mag} \cdot dx = dW_{magnetic}$$

For a current-controlled magnetic system, the energy stored is:

$$W_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

where $L$ is the coil inductance. The mechanical force is therefore:

$$F_{mag} = \frac{\partial W_{mag}}{\partial x}\bigg|_{I = constant} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$$

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Transition

**Step 1 — Express inductance in terms of reluctance:**

$$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{l_g}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate L with respect to plunger position x:**

In a typical solenoid, the air gap $l_g$ **decreases linearly** as the plunger moves in by $x$:

$$l_g(x) = l_{g,0} - x$$

$$\frac{dL}{dx} = N^2 \mu_0 A_g \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{1}{l_{g,0} - x}\right)$$

$$\frac{dL}{dx} = N^2 \mu_0 A_g \cdot \frac{1}{(l_{g,0} - x)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into force equation:**

$$F_{mag}(x) = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \cdot \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_g}{(l_{g,0} - x)^2}$$

$$\boxed{F_{mag}(x) = \frac{\mu_0 A_g (NI)^2}{2(l_{g,0} - x)^2}}$$

### Verification of Limiting Behavior

At $x = 0$ (plunger at rest, maximum air gap):

$$F_{mag}(0) = \frac{\mu_0 A_g (NI)^2}{2 l_{g,0}^2} \quad \text{(smallest force)}$$

As $x \to l_{g,0}$ (plunger fully inserted, air gap → 0):

$$F_{mag} \to \infty \quad \text{(theoretically)}$$

In practice, **saturation of the iron core** limits the force to a finite maximum. This is why practical solenoids are designed to ensure that **the required pull-in force is met at $x = 0$** (the worst case).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The formula shows that the solenoid generates the **smallest** mechanical force exactly at the moment it must overcome the **largest** opposing force (the spring + hydraulic force at the rest position). This is the fundamental engineering challenge in solenoid valve design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cracking Pressure and Force Balance of a Pressure Relief Valve

### Physical Setup

A poppet of effective seat area $A$ is held against its seat by a spring of stiffness $k$ compressed by an amount $x_0$ (preload). The pump supplies pressure $P$ acting on the poppet.

### Force Balance Equation

At the instant of poppet lift (cracking condition), the upward hydraulic force equals the downward spring force:

$$F_{hydraulic,up} = F_{spring,down}$$

$$P_{crack} \cdot A = k \cdot x_0$$

**Step 1 — Solve for cracking pressure:**

$$P_{crack} = \frac{k x_0}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Relate to maximum system pressure setting:** If the adjustment screw compresses the spring additionally by $\Delta x$ (knob turned), the new setting is:

$$P_{set} = \frac{k(x_0 + \Delta x)}{A}$$

**Step 3 — After poppet lifts by $y$, the spring further compresses:** The total spring force becomes $k(x_0 + \Delta x + y)$. The hydraulic force at full lift is $P_{blowdown} \cdot A$. Therefore:

$$P_{blowdown} = \frac{k(x_0 + \Delta x + y_{full})}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Blowdown pressure is therefore slightly higher than $P_{set}$ at full lift (to overcome extra spring compression):**

$$\boxed{P_{blowdown} - P_{set} = \frac{k \cdot y_{full}}{A}}$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Numerical Sample:** A PRV has $k = 50$ N/mm, $A = 100$ mm², initial compression $x_0 = 5$ mm, knob compression $\Delta x = 5$ mm. Find $P_{set}$.
>
> $P_{set} = (50 \times 10) / 100 = 5$ N/mm² = **50 bar**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Valve Types for Exam Use

| Feature | DCV | Pressure Relief Valve | Pressure Reducing Valve | Sequence Valve | Solenoid Valve |
|---------|-----|----------------------|-------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Primary function | Direct flow path | Limit max pressure | Maintain lower downstream pressure | Enable flow in sequence | Provide electrical actuation |
| Movable element | Spool / Poppet / Disc | Poppet | Spool | Poppet / Spool | Spool (via solenoid) |
| Number of ports | 2, 3, 4, 5 | 2 (P, T) | 3 (P_in, P_out, T) | 3 (P, A, T) or 3 (P, A, B) | 2, 3, 4, 5 |
| Number of positions | 2 or 3 | 2 (closed/open) | 2 (open/throttling) | 2 (closed/open) | 2 (NC/NO) |
| Actuation | Manual / Mech / Solenoid / Pilot | Hydraulic vs. spring | Hydraulic vs. spring (downstream) | Hydraulic vs. spring (inlet) | Solenoid |
| Adjustment | No | Yes (knob for $P_{set}$) | Yes (knob for $P_{reduced}$) | Yes (knob for $P_{sequence}$) | No (fixed by design) |
| Energy dissipation | Negligible | High (heat in dumped oil) | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| Failure mode | Spool stuck | Spring breaks → overpressure! | Spring breaks → full P to secondary | Spring breaks → flow blocked | Coil burn-out |
| KTU exam frequency | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Very High |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pump Power Loss in a Pressure Relief Valve

When a PRV is dumping full pump flow, **all hydraulic power is converted to heat**. This is critical for thermal design.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power generated by pump:**

$$P_{pump} = P_{set} \cdot Q_{pump}$$

where $Q_{pump}$ is in m³/s when $P$ in Pa.

**Step 2 — Useful power to actuator** (if any actuator is moving):

$$P_{useful} = P_{set} \cdot Q_{actuator}$$

**Step 3 — Wasted (heat) power dumped through PRV:**

$$P_{heat} = P_{set} \cdot Q_{dumped} = P_{set} \cdot (Q_{pump} - Q_{actuator})$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to temperature rise:** Using the oil's specific heat $c_p \approx 1800$ J/(kg·°C) and density $\rho \approx 870$ kg/m³ for hydraulic oil, the temperature rise in tank of volume $V$ over time $t$ is:

$$\Delta T = \frac{P_{heat} \cdot t}{c_p \cdot \rho \cdot V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** This is why hydraulic systems always need a **heat exchanger (oil cooler)** in the tank return line when the duty cycle involves long relief-valve dumping (e.g., holding a cylinder in position with no work being done).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Circuit Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Motor Electric Drive] --> node2[Hydraulic Pump Fixed Displacement]
    node2 --> node3[Pressure Relief Valve Set 140 bar]
    node3 --> node4[Tank Reservoir]
    node2 --> node5[DCV 4 by 3 Solenoid Actuated]
    node5 --> node6[Double Acting Cylinder]
    node6 --> node5
    node5 --> node4
    node5 --> node7[Pressure Gauge Indicator]
    node8[PLC Controller 24V DC] --> node9[Solenoid Coil S1]
    node9 --> node5
    node10[Limit Switch LS1] --> node8
    node11[Limit Switch LS2] --> node8
    node8 --> node12[Direction Control Logic]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Solenoid Valve Operating States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[PLC Output 24V DC Signal] --> nodeB{Energized or De energized}
    nodeB -->|Energized| nodeC[Current Flows in Coil]
    nodeB -->|De energized| nodeD[Spring Pushes Plunger Back]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Magnetic Flux Generated]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Magnetic Force on Plunger]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Plunger Pulls Into Coil]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Spool Shifts to Active Position]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Fluid Path P to A Active]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Plunger Returns to Rest]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Spool Returns to Default]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Fluid Path Blocked NC Type or P to B NO Type]
```

## 4.3 State Diagram: 5 by 2 Single Solenoid DCV

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RestState
    RestState: Position 1 Spring Returned
    RestState: Port 1 to 2 Active
    RestState: Port 4 to 5 Exhaust
    RestState: Cylinder Retracts
    Energized: Position 2 Solenoid Pulled
    Energized: Port 1 to 4 Active
    Energized: Port 2 to 3 Exhaust
    Energized: Cylinder Extends
    RestState --> Energized: Coil Energized 24V DC
    Energized --> RestState: Coil De energized Spring Return
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Pressure Relief Valve Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[System Pressure P acts on Poppet] --> node2[Upward Force Fh = P times A]
    node3[Spring Preload Fs] --> node4[Downward Force on Poppet]
    node2 --> node5{Poppet Net Force}
    node4 --> node5
    node5 -->|Fh greater than Fs| node6[Valve Opens Oil to Tank]
    node5 -->|Fh less than Fs| node7[Valve Sealed No Flow to Tank]
    node6 --> node8[Tank Reservoir with Heat Dissipation]
    node7 --> node9[Full Pressure to Actuator]
```

## 4.5 Component Anatomy — Solenoid Valve Cross-Section (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Electrical Terminal Block] --> nodeB[Insulated Coil Winding N turns of copper]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Magnetic Yoke Iron Path]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Plunger Soft Iron Armature]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Working Air Gap l g]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Spool Connection Mechanical Linkage]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Fluid Ports P T A B]
    nodeH[Return Spring] --> nodeD
    nodeI[Manual Override Optional Push Pin] --> nodeD
```

## 4.6 Functional Flow: Sequence Valve Operation in Clamping Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Main Pump 140 bar] --> node2[Direction Control Valve]
    node2 --> node3[Sequence Valve Set 35 bar]
    node3 -->|Pressure less than 35 bar| node4[Clamping Cylinder P port]
    node4 --> node5[Clamping Cylinder T return to tank]
    node3 -->|Pressure greater than 35 bar| node6[Cutting Cylinder P port]
    node6 --> node7[Cutting Cylinder T return to tank]
    node5 --> node8[Pressure rises as clamp holds]
    node8 --> node9[Safety interlock cutting disabled until clamp achieved]
```
